Rawr-semantic-architecture-snapshot


RAWR semantic architecture snapshot


What this is


This is the current canonical design snapshot.


It is not a broad architecture inventory and it is not a narrow implementation checklist.


It captures the current semantic target, why it matters, what has been learned so far, what should be locked now, what should still be pressure-tested, and what sequence creates the most leverage for the next phase.


The goal is simple: create a system whose structure becomes legible enough that future enforcement, scaffolding, worktree harnessing, and eventually autonomous stewardship can be built on top of it without fighting ambiguity at every layer.



The core realization


The repo is not only becoming a software system. It is becoming a machine-operable environment.


That changes what matters.


The main job is no longer just choosing frameworks or making code reusable. The main job is to make the architecture semantically explicit enough that:


humans can reason about it quickly,

agents can operate inside it safely,

boundaries can be pressure-tested and then enforced,

runtime embodiment can stay separate from semantic truth,

later graph tooling like Nx can encode real structure instead of premature guesses.


The design pressure has made one thing clear: the system needs explicit distinction between semantic capability truth, runtime embodiment, and runtime container/boot authority.


That leads to the locked target ontology below.



Locked target semantic architecture


Top-level roots


packages/   pure/shared/supporting code
services/   contract-bearing in-process service boundaries
plugins/    runtime-hosted embodiments and adapters
apps/       host implementations


This is the current target.


Why this is the right split


packages/ and services/ are not the same thing.


A pure package is shared matter: types, helpers, SDKs, adapters, utilities, lower-level primitives, and anything else that does not itself define a first-class service boundary.


A service is a semantic boundary. It owns contract-bearing behavior, stable context lanes, service-wide middleware semantics, and the internal modules/procedures that make up a meaningful capability surface.


A plugin is not the service itself. A plugin is how some runtime surface mounts, wraps, adapts, exposes, or runs one or more services.


An app is not the business logic center. An app is a host. It owns bootstrapping, runtime composition, lifecycle, and mounting.


That gives the system a clean four-part model:


package = support matter
service = semantic capability boundary
plugin = runtime embodiment / adapter
app = host / boot authority


This is the minimum vocabulary that makes the rest of the architecture legible.



Why services/ won


Several alternatives were explored: domains, capabilities, boundaries, kernel, and staying under packages/.


kernel was rejected because it implies privileged global centrality and would blur more than clarify.


domains was rejected as the primary root because some service-boundary units may become large and domain-heavy, but many may remain smaller or more mixed in scope. Using domain for all of them would overweight the small ones and encourage unnecessary ceremony.


capabilities was strong, but in practice services is simpler, more natural, and more immediately readable once the runtime plugin subtype is renamed away from service.


That is the key trick: keep services/ for semantic service boundaries, and use runtime-specific nouns under plugins.


Examples:


services/todo
services/support

plugins/web/support-panel
plugins/api/support-api
plugins/worker/support-triage
plugins/background/todo-sync


This keeps service as the semantic concept and gives runtime embodiments their own clearer names.



The service concept, locked


A service is a contract-bearing in-process boundary.


It is not merely “code with behavior.”
It is not merely “a package.”
It is not yet “a deployed microservice.”


It is the semantic unit that owns:


stable boundary contracts,

stable context lane structure,

required service-wide middleware semantics,

central service implementer composition,

internal module/procedure decomposition,

business-capability truth for that boundary.


The service package is where semantic truth lives.


It may be small or large.
It may grow domain weight over time.
That does not change its type.


The important thing is that its meaning is now explicit.



The plugin concept, clarified


A plugin is a runtime-hosted embodiment.


It owns runtime-facing concerns:


mounting onto a host,

transport and surface adaptation,

runtime middleware,

host integration,

lifecycle participation,

optional background/worker behavior,

runtime-specific orchestration.


A plugin may wrap a service directly.
A plugin may expose a service through a particular runtime surface.
A plugin may sometimes compose runtime-specific behavior across services.


But plugins should not become the default home of semantic composition.


That pressure-test question is still being actively resolved, but the current target principle is:


semantic composition belongs in services when it is part of service truth

plugins compose only when the composition is genuinely runtime-specific


This distinction is one of the most important remaining architecture questions, and it must be pressure-tested rather than guessed.



The host concept, formalized


The current repo already behaves as if a host exists. It just has not been named strongly enough.


A host is the boot authority for a runtime surface.


It owns:


process/app bootstrap,

runtime identity,

config and repo-root authority,

telemetry installation,

runtime lifecycle,

route/app composition,

plugin mounting,

base URL / port / worktree identity.


That means the current system should explicitly recognize:


service = what behavior exists
plugin = how behavior is embodied or mounted
host = where behavior boots and lives at runtime


This matters now because the future requires allowing some plugins to begin mounted on a shared host and later graduate into their own dedicated host/server without collapsing semantic boundaries.


So the target model becomes:


apps/       host implementations
plugins/    runtime embodiments that target hosts
services/   host-agnostic semantic service truth
packages/   pure support matter


This is the correct foundation for later shared-host and dedicated-host topologies.



Why this ontology speeds things up


The point of the ontology is not tidiness.
The point is leverage.


Right now ambiguity costs time in every direction:


when deciding what belongs where,

when pressure-testing cross-boundary work,

when asking whether a plugin is wrapping behavior or defining it,

when deciding what future Nx graph types should exist,

when deciding what should be scaffolded,

when deciding what agents should be allowed to operate over.


The locked ontology reduces ambiguity in advance.


It gives a simpler set of questions:


Is this support matter? Then it is a package.

Is this a semantic boundary with contracts and service lanes? Then it is a service.

Is this runtime-hosted adaptation or embodiment? Then it is a plugin.

Is this bootstrapping and runtime container authority? Then it is a host app.


That makes cross-boundary design work faster because it narrows the kinds of architectural mistakes that are even thinkable.


It also sets up later graph law cleanly: once the ontology is stable, Nx tags and graph policy become encoding, not exploration.



What is already locked vs what still needs pressure


Locked enough to act on


The following is sufficiently clear to treat as the target semantic direction:


service as a first-class semantic boundary,

package as pure/shared support matter,

plugin as runtime embodiment,

app as host,

hosts as first-class architectural concept,

service truth separate from runtime embodiment,

eventual Nx encoding should follow semantic truth, not invent it.


Still needs pressure-testing


The biggest unresolved boundary question is at the plugin edge.


Specifically:


When should a plugin directly wrap one service router 1:1?

When should a plugin compose multiple services directly into a runtime surface?

When should cross-service semantic composition become a composed service package that a plugin then mounts thinly?


This is the live architectural frontier.


It should be resolved through concrete examples, not abstract preference.



Canonical pressure-test program


The next phase is not just “finish todo.”


The next phase is to use one golden service plus additional service shapes to force the remaining truth out of the system.


Pressure-test stack


Golden single service


finish the todo example as the canonical single-service boundary

lock service semantics, lane structure, implementer seam, module setup pattern, middleware layering

One smaller service


narrower and simpler than todo

proves the lower bound of service shape

One larger composed service


composes the smaller services or parts of them

proves what semantic composition wants to look like in service land

Plugin-boundary wiring tests


one plugin wraps a service nearly 1:1

one plugin composes multiple services directly

one plugin mounts a composed service thinly


Why these examples matter


This is the only reliable way to answer:


what plugins should be allowed to own,

where semantic composition should land,

where runtime composition should land,

whether service truth remains centered in services under real pressure,

what should later be encoded in Nx generators and graph rules.


Without this phase, later codification risks freezing the wrong ontology.



Sequencing for maximum leverage


The correct next sequence is not a broad refactor. It is a domino chain.


Phase 1 — lock the service definition and golden exemplar


Finish the todo service until it stops teaching fundamentally new lessons every day.


This is where service truth gets sharp.


Why first: later naming, graph law, and scaffolding all depend on this.


Phase 2 — make service semantics explicit in the repo


Once the service definition is clear enough, move proven service-boundary units into services/.


Do this as semantic clarification, not final over-encoding.


Why now: explicit naming improves the quality of the next cross-boundary experiments by changing how both humans and agents see the repo.


Phase 3 — run the cross-boundary pressure test


Add:


one smaller service,

one larger composed service,

plugin wrappers/compositions over them.


This phase resolves the plugin/service boundary law.


Why now: this is the major unresolved truth that remains.


Phase 4 — extract the canonical project taxonomy


Once the pressure test resolves the boundary law, write down the stable project kinds and what they mean:


package
service
plugin
app/host


For each, define:


what it may import,

what it may expose,

what responsibilities it may own,

what targets and gates apply,

what future scaffold/generator it deserves.


Why now: this becomes the bridge from semantic truth to graph law.


Phase 5 — encode graph-shaped truth into Nx


Only after the project taxonomy and boundary law are proven should Nx become structural law.


Use Nx for:


graph visibility,

affected analysis,

project kinds and tags,

dependency constraints,

generators,

scaffoldability,

later distribution and conformance.


Do not ask Nx to discover the ontology.
Use Nx to encode the ontology once learned.


Why now: at this point the graph is expressing real truth rather than guesses.


Phase 6 — build the worktree harness around the locked kernel


Once services, plugins, hosts, and graph law are stable enough, build the per-worktree boot harness:


host identity,

port allocation,

server/web/runtime boot,

worktree-aware telemetry identity,

reproducible local runtime bundles.


Why now: the semantic kernel makes worktree harnessing much easier and much less ambiguous.


Phase 7 — add real worktree observability


Make each worktree legible via:


structured logs,

OpenTelemetry resource identity,

collector routing,

simple backend/UI path.


This is not for enterprise polish. It is for machine-operable runtime truth.


Phase 8 — add governance primitives


Only after the semantic kernel, graph law, and worktree harness exist should the governance system deepen:


tension bank,

failed approach capture,

collision with external signals,

experiment loop,

steward workbench,

review and escalation policy.


This is where the system begins to accumulate agenda continuity rather than just execution capability.



Why this sequence is the highest-leverage path


Each step creates the substrate the next step depends on.


golden service truth
    ↓
semantic naming clarity
    ↓
cross-boundary truth
    ↓
project taxonomy
    ↓
Nx graph law and scaffoldability
    ↓
worktree harness
    ↓
observability
    ↓
governance / autonomous stewardship


If this order is inverted, the later steps become weaker:


Nx too early encodes guesses.

worktree harness too early wraps ambiguity.

governance too early becomes philosophy without substrate.

observability too early becomes generic infra noise.


The sequence above avoids that.



The critical insight: this semantic kernel is the leverage point


The most important thing to see is that the service/plugin/host/package split is not just naming.


It is the kernel that allows the later system to exist coherently.


Once this kernel is stable, several downstream wins become possible.


1. Nx becomes high-value instead of premature


Without the semantic kernel, Nx is mostly graph tooling.
With the semantic kernel, Nx becomes:


architecture law,

project taxonomy,

dependency policy,

generator authority,

scaffoldable correctness,

affected reasoning over meaningful units.


This is the point where the graph stops being just dependency visibility and starts becoming machine-readable architectural truth.


2. Worktree boot becomes clean and scalable


Per-worktree boot only becomes elegant when the system already knows:


what a host is,

what services are,

what plugins mount where,

what runtime identity should attach to each host and worktree.


The semantic kernel makes worktree harnessing straightforward rather than ad hoc.


3. Observability becomes attached to real units


Instead of generic “app telemetry,” the system can observe:


host behavior,

plugin behavior,

service boundaries,

worktree identity,

runtime composition patterns.


That makes observability useful for architecture and future agents, not just debugging.


4. The tension bank and governance layer become grounded


Governance ideas like a tension bank, failed approach memory, steward workbench, and generative experimentation only become serious when the system already has real semantic units to attach pressure to.


The semantic kernel gives governance a place to live:


tensions can belong to services,

runtime tensions can belong to plugins or hosts,

graph relationships can surface collisions,

stewards can own bounded areas rather than vague folders.


5. Autonomous stewardship becomes plausible


A fully autonomous development environment does not begin with smarter generation.
It begins with a codebase that has a legible political structure.


The semantic kernel is that political structure.


Once stable, it gives agents:


clear units of ownership,

graph-visible relationships,

enforceable import/dependency law,

known boot/runtime surfaces,

known places where semantic truth should live,

known escalation points between service/plugin/host decisions.


That is what makes later persistent stewards, workbench operations, and self-improving harnesses realistic.



Final snapshot


The architecture is converging toward this:


packages/   pure/shared support matter
services/   semantic service boundaries
plugins/    runtime embodiments and adapters
apps/       hosts / boot authorities


The next unlock is not a broad system rewrite.
It is to use this clarified ontology to force out the remaining plugin/service/host boundary truth through targeted pressure testing.


Then, and only then, the system should be codified into Nx graph law and scaffolding.


That is the highest-leverage design path because it turns naming into clarity, clarity into pressure, pressure into truth, truth into graph law, and graph law into the substrate for everything that follows.


That is the design moment.


That is the snapshot.


I put it together in a markdown document in the canvas: “rawr-semantic-architecture-snapshot.”


It captures:


the locked semantic target,

the rationale for packages / services / plugins / apps,

the service/plugin/host distinctions,

the leverage-maximizing sequence,

the pressure-test program,

and how this kernel turns into Nx graph law, worktree harnessing, observability, governance, and eventually a fully autonomous development environment.

let’s say one of my services needs to author a complex underlying repository later, which the rest of the service uses to build the business logic. This would include db schemas and migrations, repo access patterns, policies, and so on.


Should that live nested in the service or split into a package? Keep in kind all of the axes and tensions we are working with at one time, and the overall goals of the system

Default answer: start nested inside the service.


Do not split it into a package just because it is “large” or “important.” Split it only when it becomes a genuinely reusable or separately governed unit.


The key tension here is that your system is trying to preserve semantic truth. If the repository layer, schemas, migrations, policies, and access patterns exist primarily to serve one service’s business boundary, then making them a separate package too early weakens that truth. It turns a service-internal implementation core into an apparently general-purpose thing, which invites the wrong reuse and muddies ownership.


So the default placement should be:


services/
  foo/
    src/
      service/...
      repository/...
      db/...
      policies/...
      migrations/...


or whatever exact internal shape you prefer.


Why nested is the better default:


The schemas, migrations, repo patterns, and persistence policies are often not generic infrastructure. They are part of how that service expresses and protects its own boundary. Even if they are sophisticated, they are still usually internal service mechanics, not shared support matter.


That means nesting preserves the correct message:


this persistence model belongs to this service


That is valuable for:


humans,

agents,

future Nx graph rules,

and pressure-testing the service as a coherent unit.


If you split too early into a package, you create three risks.


First, false generality.
A package looks reusable even when it should not be.


Second, semantic leakage.
Other services start depending on the persistence internals of a service instead of going through service truth.


Third, premature graph hardening.
You end up encoding a dependency edge that may only exist because of folder neatness, not real architectural need.


So the better question is not “is it complex enough to deserve a package?”
It is:


is it part of the service's internal implementation truth,
or is it truly support matter shared across multiple semantic boundaries?


That is the axis that matters.


My rule would be:


Keep it nested in the service when it is:


specific to that service’s domain model,

tightly coupled to its contracts and policies,

defining storage semantics that should not be reused directly,

or still evolving rapidly as part of service pressure-testing.


Split it into a package only when it becomes one of these:


A genuinely shared lower-level primitive
Example: a reusable DB client wrapper, migration runner, SQL helper toolkit, policy DSL, common repository base abstractions.

A separately governed support subsystem
Example: a cross-service event store package, common auth policy engine, standardized migration framework.

A portability seam that needs independence
Example: you need to swap storage implementations or run the persistence layer in multiple service contexts and the abstraction is real, not aspirational.

A generated/scaffolded support layer that many services should consume consistently
Example: a common package for base schema conventions, migration conventions, typed DB primitives.


Notice what is not on that list: “it got big.”


Bigness alone is not a reason to split.


Given your overall goals, I would lean even more strongly toward nesting at first because you are still working through multiple architecture axes at once:


service truth vs support matter,

service vs plugin responsibility,

future Nx graph law,

agent legibility,

scaffoldability,

worktree harnessing,

and eventual autonomous stewardship.


In that environment, premature extraction is especially dangerous because it hides the actual semantic center of gravity.


The strongest pattern is probably this:


services/foo
  owns:
  - contracts
  - business policies
  - internal repository logic
  - service-specific schemas/migrations
  - service-specific persistence access patterns


Then later, if repeated patterns emerge across several services, extract the true shared layer into packages/.


That means your extraction direction should be:


service-internal first
shared-package second


not the other way around.


A useful mental model:


Service internals are allowed to be rich.

Packages should earn their generality.


That is the cleanest way to stay aligned with the overall system goals.


One nuance: if the repository/storage side becomes large enough, I would still keep it inside the service, but give it its own internal substructure rather than flattening it into the service root.


For example:


services/foo/
  src/
    contract/
    service/
    repository/
      schema/
      migrations/
      policies/
      adapters/
      repos/


That gives you internal clarity without lying to the outer architecture.


So my bottom-line recommendation is:


Nest first. Extract later only when the reuse/governance seam becomes undeniably real.


That preserves semantic ownership, avoids false abstraction, and gives the system cleaner truth for the later graph/scaffolding/autonomous layers.
But how would we realistically handle a conditional like this? It requires agents to decide at some point to split it out, and all the meanwhile “should I” remains an open question in context

You do not handle that by hoping the agent has good taste in the moment.


You handle it by making “should this split out?” an explicit governed decision with triggers, not a floating contextual judgment.


That is the real answer.


If you leave it as:
“keep it nested until it feels reusable”
then yes, agents will carry an unresolved background ambiguity forever, and that is exactly the kind of ambiguity that causes drift.


So the system needs a formal intermediate state.


The pattern I would use is:


default = nested
possible future = extractable
actual extraction = gated decision


That means every service-internal subsystem starts life as nested unless proven otherwise. But the question of extraction is not left as ambient uncertainty. It becomes a tracked architectural decision class.


The cleanest way to do that is to define extraction criteria and review triggers.


For example, a nested repository/storage subsystem remains nested by default. It only becomes a candidate for extraction when one or more of these become true:


1. A second service needs the same thing.
2. The service is reimplementing the same persistence/policy pattern another service already has.
3. The nested subsystem has become more stable than the service around it.
4. The subsystem now has a distinct owner/lifecycle/governance profile.
5. The service boundary is being distorted to preserve nesting.


That already kills a lot of ambiguity, because now the agent is not asking “should I split this?” every time it edits a file.


It is asking a much narrower question:


did I hit an extraction trigger?


That is much better.


Then you add a second rule:


hitting a trigger does not mean auto-extract.
It means: open an architectural tension / extraction candidate.


So the flow becomes:


nested by default
    ↓
trigger hit
    ↓
log extraction candidate
    ↓
evaluate against policy
    ↓
either keep nested with rationale
or extract with scaffolded move


That is exactly the kind of thing your tension-bank/governance model is good for.


This is where the tension bank stops being “nice philosophy” and becomes practical architecture hygiene.


You would create a specific tension type or architectural review type like:


Possible extraction candidate:
<service>/<subsystem>
reason:
- duplicate use
- policy divergence
- ownership split
- service distortion
status:
- watch
- assess
- approved
- rejected


Now the question is no longer floating in agent context. It is persisted.


That matters a lot.


Because then the local coding agent can operate under a stable rule:


Stay nested unless extraction has been approved.
If triggers are hit, record the candidate.
Do not spontaneously extract during unrelated work.


That is the kind of rule agents can actually follow.


So the real solution is not “teach agents to know better.”
It is:

Because otherwise one agent starts extracting, another assumes nesting is still canonical, and now you have architecture churn by accident.

nested

The policy I would recommend is:


Default structural policy


Service-internal persistence/repository layers stay nested by default.

Trigger policy

another service wants the same subsystem,

duplicate implementation pressure appears,

a distinct ownership/lifecycle emerges,

and an explicit note on what must remain in the service.


This makes the system tractable.

maybe, and it is now a recorded candidate

yes, because the candidate was approved

A useful analogy:

So my strong recommendation is:


Do not make extraction a continuous judgment.
Make it a governed transition with triggers and explicit review.


That gives you:


lower context burden,

less architecture drift,

better agent consistency,

and a clean bridge into future tension-bank / Nx / scaffoldable law.

The current shape also aligns with intuitive semantic understanding better than it first appears. A human or agent can read:

The stronger rule is:


Default: module-local all the way down.

At n=1, it is obvious.

At n=100, the cost of cross-module ambiguity dominates, so locality matters even more.


A global service-internal shared folder model usually starts feeling “organized” around n=3, then quietly becomes a junk drawer by n=20.


So my blunt answer is:


Current module encapsulation is the better default kernel.

3. If multiple services need it, only then consider package extraction.


That gives you a three-step progression that kills ambient “should I move this?” ambiguity.


So the answer is not “your current structure or my earlier sketch.”
The stronger version is:


keep the current module-encapsulated model as the default,
and treat flatter shared internal service folders as earned exceptions.


That is the best fit for your goals of:


reduced optionality,

and scaling cleanly from small to large services.

module-local first

What I would not do:

As modules get deeper, they should still own their own local structure first.

A good service/shared/ probably contains only a few classes of things:


repository/ for shared internal repo primitives

src/

    base.ts
    impl.ts

That is the structure I think scales best.

service-shared truth explicit and scarce,

and package extraction as a later, governed move.

A useful concrete split is:

This is where raw persistence mechanics live.


Typical contents:


schema definitions

migration files

table/column metadata

query builders or SQL fragments

transaction helpers

low-level row mapping

connection/pool adapters

database-specific constraints or DDL (Data Definition Language) concerns

how is data stored and queried mechanically?


repository/


This is where service-facing persistence operations live.


Typical contents:


findById

insertTask

listAssignmentsForTask

markCompleted

findTagsByWorkspace

aggregate loading patterns

persistence operations expressed in service language, not SQL language


This layer answers:
what data operations does this service/module need in its own terms?


So, concretely:


db/
  schema.ts
  migrations/
  sql.ts
  transaction.ts

repository/
  task-repository.ts
  tag-repository.ts
  assignment-repository.ts


The repository uses the db layer.
The service handlers use the repository layer.


That gives you this flow:


handler -> repo -> db


That is the cleanest chain.


When should they not be separate?


If the service is small enough that splitting them would create fake architecture, then do not split them.


For a small module, this is totally fine:


modules/tasks/
  repository/
    index.ts
    schema.ts
    migrations/


where the same local repository area contains both the low-level db mechanics and the service-facing repo operations.


The split only becomes worth it when:


storage mechanics are getting substantial,

multiple repositories share the same schema/query primitives,

or you want to keep handler-facing operations cleanly insulated from DB details.


So my real advice is:


small module: combine db + repository locally
larger/deeper service: split db from repository


Do not force the split too early.


What “policies” are concretely


In your current service base, policy is already declared as static service-wide vocabulary, with entries like readOnlyRejected and assignmentLimitReached. The docstring explicitly says policy names are static service semantics that runtime observability may consume, and that they are distinct from caller-facing errors and from telemetry behavior itself.


base


That is the clue.


A policy is not just a boolean check.
It is a named business/rule decision surface.


A good policy layer contains things like:


read-only mode rules

assignment limits

workspace-level permission rules

lifecycle/state transition rules

visibility rules

mutation eligibility rules

dedupe/uniqueness rules at the service/business level

rules for whether an operation is allowed, denied, throttled, escalated, or flagged


Policies answer:
under what rule conditions is this behavior permitted, rejected, or modified?


They are not:


raw DB constraints

generic helpers

transport middleware

plain error types


A concrete example:


Instead of this logic living inline in a handler:


if (context.config.readOnly) { ... }
if (task.assignments.length >= context.config.limits.maxAssignmentsPerTask) { ... }


you might eventually have:


policies/
  read-only.ts
  assignment-limit.ts
  task-visibility.ts
  mutation-eligibility.ts


Then handlers, repositories, or service middleware can call those policies.


Who would use policies?


Potential users:


procedure handlers

service middleware

module setup

repositories, sometimes, if the repository needs to enforce service-owned access semantics before querying/mutating

observability/analytics layers, which may emit named policy events

future composed services that need to preserve the same business rule decisions


The important part is that policies are semantic rule units, not just convenience functions.


A clean mental model:


errors = how failure is expressed
policies = why a decision was made
observability = how that decision is recorded
repository = how data is accessed
db = how storage works


That split is very strong.


Concrete example


For a tasks module, you might eventually have:


modules/tasks/
  contract.ts
  setup.ts
  router.ts

  repository/
    task-repository.ts

  db/
    schema.ts
    migrations/
    queries.ts

  policies/
    can-create-task.ts
    can-update-task.ts
    can-assign-task.ts


And then:


router.ts calls canCreateTask(...)

task-repository.ts handles insert, findById, update

db/queries.ts contains SQL/query-builder details

observability can emit todo.policy.read_only_rejected or todo.policy.assignment_limit_reached using the canonical policy vocabulary already declared in base.ts
base


But again, this is the larger version. Do not force this shape at n=1.


The scaling rule I’d use is:


1. Start with module-local repository only.
2. Split db from repository when storage mechanics become meaningfully distinct.
3. Add policies when rule logic starts repeating or deserves named semantic reuse.
4. Lift anything to service/shared only after duplication across modules is real.


Sanity check


Bad split:


db and repository separated just because “clean architecture”

policies folder full of random predicates and helpers

handlers bouncing through 5 files for trivial logic


Good split:


db exists because storage mechanics are actually nontrivial

repository gives handlers a clean service-language interface

policies holds named, reusable, semantically meaningful rule decisions


So the blunt answer is:


db is storage mechanics. repository is service-language data access. policies are named business/rule decisions.
Keep them combined until the complexity actually earns the split.

what if I have two small services that want to write to the same db? in one case they need to share some domain entities/patterns/etc; in another they are separate entirely but want to write to the same db for simplicity?


Or am I thinking about it wrong, and there’s a better model for working through database sharing vs. not sharing in the shared host model?

You are partly thinking about it in the wrong dimension.


The important question is usually not “do these two services share a database?”
The important questions are:


1. do they share storage infrastructure?
2. do they share schema ownership?
3. do they share write authority over the same tables/entities?
4. do they share semantic truth, or only physical persistence?


Those are very different.


In a shared-host model, it is completely normal for multiple services to use the same physical Postgres instance or DB pool. In your current service definition, dbPool is already a host-owned dependency supplied through stable service deps, which means the architecture already expects the host to provide database capability to services.


base


That is good. It means shared infrastructure is normal.


So the first clarification is:


sharing the same DB instance is not the problem.
The real question is whether services are sharing schema ownership and write authority.


That gives you three clean levels.


Level 1: shared database instance, separate schema ownership


This is the easiest and usually the best default.


Two services both use the same database server / connection pool / app host, but each service owns its own tables and migrations.


Example:


service A owns:
  tasks
  task_tags

service B owns:
  support_items
  support_events


They are in the same Postgres database for operational simplicity, but ownership is separate.


This is usually fine.


In your architecture, the host can provide one dbPool to both services, while each service keeps its own repository/schema/migration truth nested under itself. That keeps semantic ownership clean while keeping deployment simple.


base


[


setup


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/packages/example-todo/src/service/modules/tasks/setup.ts)


This is the model I would default to.


Level 2: shared domain entities/patterns, but one service owns the writes


This is where people get confused.


If two services “share” an entity, the most important thing is usually that one service should still own the canonical writes, and the other service should consume that truth through the owning service boundary or through explicitly shared lower-level primitives.


So if both services genuinely revolve around the same entity family, you usually want one of these:


Option A: they are actually one larger service with modules


If the shared entities/policies are deep and central, then splitting them into two services may be artificial.


Option B: one service owns the entity, the other calls it


The non-owning service goes through the owning service’s router/client, not direct writes to the same tables.


Option C: shared lower-level support package, but still separate service ownership


If the overlap is schema conventions, query helpers, row codecs, or common policy primitives, extract only that shared support matter into packages/, while one service still owns the actual business writes.


This is the important principle:


shared domain semantics do not automatically justify shared write authority


If both services write directly to the same business tables because “it’s simpler,” you usually create semantic drift fast.


Level 3: same DB for simplicity, but no semantic overlap


This is fine. Probably the most common happy path.


Two unrelated services can absolutely write to the same database instance, as long as:


each owns its own tables,

each owns its own migrations,

each owns its own repository layer,

and they do not casually reach into each other’s tables.


This is just shared infrastructure, not shared semantics.


So yes, you can have:


shared host
shared dbPool
same Postgres database
different services
different tables
different migrations
different repositories


That is clean.


The real wrong model


The dangerous model is this:


two services
same host
same db
same tables
both write directly


That is where “service” stops meaning much.


Because once two services can both mutate the same business tables directly, you no longer really have semantic boundaries. You have multiple façades over one persistence truth, and the architecture will get weird:


duplicated policies

inconsistent invariants

migration coupling

surprise breakage

unclear observability ownership

unclear stewardship ownership later


That is exactly the kind of ambiguity your overall system is trying to eliminate.


So the rule I would use is:


shared DB instance: yes
shared dbPool: yes
shared tables with multiple direct writers: generally no


What if they need to share some domain entities/patterns?


Then work through it in this order.


Case 1: shared patterns, not shared business ownership


Extract shared support matter to packages/.


Examples:


base SQL helpers

shared codecs

migration utilities

common row mappers

generic policy DSL

common repository primitives


But do not extract the domain truth just because two services both use Postgres similarly.


Case 2: shared entity family and rule system


This is a signal they may actually be one service with multiple modules, not two independent services.


Because remember: the current service shape is explicitly modular already. The service router composes module routers once at the service level, and modules get their own setup/handler boundaries beneath that.


router


[


setup


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/packages/example-todo/src/service/modules/tasks/setup.ts)[


router


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/packages/example-todo/src/service/modules/tasks/router.ts)So if two “small services” deeply share entities and policy, the architecture already has a better home for that: one service, multiple modules.


Case 3: separate services, but one needs some data from the other


Prefer read access via service boundary, projections, or explicitly sanctioned read models rather than random direct table ownership sharing.


Better model for the shared-host world


Yes, there is a better model than “are they sharing the DB or not?”


Think in three layers:


host shares infrastructure
services own semantics
repositories/db own storage under each service


That means the shared host may provide:


one DB pool

one telemetry install

one runtime process

one config surface


But services still retain:


their own contracts

their own repositories

their own migrations

their own ownership zones


So the real design matrix is:


same host?              often yes
same db instance?       often yes
same db pool?           yes, often
same tables?            usually no
same write authority?   strongly prefer no
same domain truth?      only if they should actually be one service


That is the sharper model.


Practical default policy


Here is the policy I would actually use.


Default


Multiple services may share one physical Postgres instance and one host-provided dbPool.

Each service owns its own tables, migrations, repositories, and write invariants.


Escalation signal


If two services want to write to the same tables/entities:


first ask whether they are actually one service with multiple modules,

otherwise assign one service as canonical owner and make the other consume via service boundary,

only extract shared lower-level support matter if the reuse is truly infrastructural.


Anti-pattern


Two services directly co-owning business tables because it is convenient.


That is the one to avoid.


In your kernel pattern


Given everything you are optimizing for — low ambiguity, semantic clarity, scaffoldable graph law, later agent stewardship — I would lean hard toward:


shared database infrastructure is okay
shared business-table ownership is not the default


That gives you a clean future:


Nx can treat services as meaningful units

observability can attribute writes and failures to one owner

tensions can belong to one service

agents know where to look

plugins know what they are wrapping

hosts know what they are booting


That is much stronger than “same DB means shared data layer.”


So the short answer is:


You are not wrong to think about shared DBs, but the important distinction is not physical sharing. It is ownership sharing. In the shared-host model, it is fine to share the physical database. What you want to avoid is ungoverned shared write ownership across service boundaries.

  focused on service-internal structure and ownership law


That is the better information architecture.


Because the real risk now is not losing information. It is mixing levels. And once levels mix, both humans and agents get slower.
Okay create the doc

Service-internal-structure-and-ownership


Created the second doc in canvas: “service-internal-structure-and-ownership.”


It covers:


module-local by default

service/shared as an earned exception

db vs repository vs policies

shared DB vs shared ownership

extraction triggers and governed transitions

when two “small services” are really one service with modules

and how this structure supports later Nx/scaffolding/agent behavior
Do we have a controller concept in our system? Does it want or need one?

Not really, at least not as a first-class named concept. And I do not think you should add one as a primary architectural noun.


What you have instead is a split that already covers the jobs controllers usually try to cover:


host/app boot owns runtime bootstrap and mounting
bootstrap
rawr

service owns semantic boundary and contract/middleware assembly
base
impl

module setup.ts narrows and prepares execution context for a module
setup

module router.ts handlers own concrete procedure behavior and router composition for that module
router

service router.ts composes module routers into the final service router exactly once
router


That means the classic “controller” responsibilities are already distributed across clearer seams.


Why I would avoid adding “controller” now


In most systems, controller is an overloaded bucket for some mix of:


request adaptation

orchestration

validation

calling domain logic

shaping response output

transport-specific glue


Your current architecture is explicitly trying to remove junk-drawer layers and make responsibilities more legible. Adding “controller” would likely reintroduce a blurry middle layer right when the system is getting sharper.


It would create questions like:


is the router handler the controller?

is setup a controller?

is the plugin wrapper a controller?

is host route mounting a controller?

is a composed service façade a controller?


That is exactly the kind of ambient ambiguity you are trying to kill.


So my answer to “do we have a controller concept?” is:


implicitly, router handlers play the closest role
explicitly, no, not as a first-class concept


And to “does it want or need one?”:


as a primary architectural concept: no
as a narrow transport-adapter idea in some contexts: maybe, but probably under another name


What is acting like a controller today


The closest thing is the module router handler layer.


For example, in tasks/router.ts, the file explicitly says it owns concrete handler implementations and router composition, while setup.ts owns module setup and contract.ts owns boundary shape.


router


That is basically the controller job, but in a cleaner decomposition:


contract defines the boundary

setup prepares local execution context

handler implements the procedure behavior


That is better than a generic controller file.


So if you need a mental mapping:


classic controller
≈ module router handler layer


But I would not name it that way in the architecture.


When you might want a controller-like concept


Only if you end up with a very explicit transport adaptation layer that sits outside service truth and inside a plugin or host surface.


For example:


an HTTP-specific request/response adapter

a web endpoint adapter that reshapes transport payloads

a webhook ingress normalizer

a legacy API compatibility shim


But even there, I would probably call that:


adapter

ingress

endpoint

transport wrapper

façade


not controller.


Because “controller” says almost nothing about where the logic should live.


What the system actually wants instead


I think the architecture wants these nouns, not controller:


host
plugin
service
module
handler
adapter


Those are much more precise.


A clean mental model is:


host = boot and mount authority
plugin = runtime embodiment / surface adaptation
service = semantic capability boundary
module = internal subdivision of service truth
handler = concrete procedure behavior
adapter = translation between transport/runtime shape and service shape


That is enough.


Sanity check


If you introduced controllers, what would they own that is not already owned more clearly by one of:


host

plugin

service

setup

handler

adapter


I do not think there is a stable answer. That is a sign the concept is not needed.


So the blunt answer is:


No, your system does not really have a first-class controller concept.
And no, I do not think it wants one.
The closest existing equivalent is the module router handler layer, with setup and contract surrounding it. That is already a stronger and less ambiguous pattern than introducing controllers as a new layer.

Okay should you update the canvas with these mental model updates? Don’t destroy anything that the canvas already does well obv.


You also had 2-3 other points from before to integrate maybe?

Failed to edit Service-internal-structure-and-ownership


Service-internal-structure-and-ownership


Service internal structure and ownership


What this document is


This document sits one level below the semantic architecture snapshot.


The snapshot locks the main ontology:


packages/ for pure/shared support matter

services/ for semantic service boundaries

plugins/ for runtime embodiments

apps/ for hosts


This document answers the next question:


Once a service exists, how should its internals be structured so the system stays semantically clear, low-ambiguity, and scalable from small to large services?


The goal is not maximal flexibility. The goal is to remove optionality where it creates drift, while preserving a clean path for earned exceptions.



Core rule


A service should be module-encapsulated by default.


That means the default ownership zone inside a service is the module, not a broad shared internal layer.


The service kernel should stay small and explicit:


declarative service definition

service-wide assembly seam

final service router composition

a narrow shared internal layer only when duplication proves it is needed


This gives the service a strong shape:


services/foo/
  src/
    index.ts
    router.ts

    service/
      base.ts
      impl.ts
      router.ts
      shared/
      modules/
        module-a/
        module-b/


The rule is:


module-local first
service-shared second
package extraction last


That is the main law.



Why module-local first is stronger


At small scale, broad shared internal folders feel convenient.
At large scale, they create constant ambiguity.


A flatter service-internal structure creates repeated questions like:


does this belong to this module or the shared repository area?

is this policy local or service-global?

is this schema module-specific or shared?

is this helper generic enough to move upward?


That is exactly the kind of ambient uncertainty the system is trying to eliminate.


Module-local structure is stronger because it makes locality the default. Most questions get the same answer:


keep it with the module unless there is a proven reason not to.


This scales better because the filesystem mirrors semantic ownership.



Canonical service-internal shape


The recommended shape is:


services/foo/
  src/
    index.ts
    router.ts

    service/
      base.ts
      impl.ts
      router.ts
      shared/
        repository/
        db/
        policies/
        middleware/
        providers/
        types/
      modules/
        tasks/
          contract.ts
          setup.ts
          router.ts
          repository/
          db/
          policies/
        tags/
          contract.ts
          setup.ts
          router.ts
        assignments/
          contract.ts
          setup.ts
          router.ts


Meanings


src/index.ts and src/router.ts are thin package boundary surfaces.

src/service/base.ts is the declarative service definition and service vocabulary.

src/service/impl.ts is the service-wide assembly seam.

src/service/router.ts composes module routers once.

src/service/modules/* is the default ownership area.

src/service/shared/* is the earned exception layer for service-internal sharing.



What belongs in the service kernel


The service kernel should stay narrow.


base.ts


Owns:


the service definition

stable context lanes

service-wide metadata vocabulary

service-wide policy vocabulary

builders/factories tied to the service concept


impl.ts


Owns:


service-wide implementer construction

required service-wide middleware attachment

service-wide runtime assembly that should happen exactly once


router.ts


Owns:


final composition of module routers into the service router


These files define the service as a semantic boundary.
They should not become junk drawers.



Module-local by default


Each module should own as much of its own internal truth as possible.


A module may contain:


contract shape

setup/context narrowing

router/handlers

repository logic

db logic

policies

views/mappers

module-local middleware


Example:


modules/tasks/
  contract.ts
  setup.ts
  router.ts
  repository/
    task-repository.ts
  db/
    schema.ts
    migrations/
    queries.ts
  policies/
    can-create-task.ts
    can-update-task.ts


This is preferred over pulling everything upward early.


Why:


it preserves local semantic ownership

it reduces “where does this go?” ambiguity

it makes the module understandable in isolation

it prevents service-shared folders from becoming internal dumping grounds



What service/shared/ is for


service/shared/ is not the default place to author internals.
It is the place for proven service-internal shared seams.


It should be explicitly scarce.


A good service/shared/ exists for things that are:


reused across multiple modules in the same service

not appropriate as public/package-level support matter

still semantically owned by this one service


Examples:


shared service-internal repository primitives

shared service-internal schema/query helpers

shared service-internal policy logic

shared service-local middleware or providers

shared service-internal types


Bad signs:


random helpers landing there by convenience

vague common/, utils/, or lib/ folders

anything public-facing or cross-service landing there


The path should mean something precise:


service/shared/* = shared within this service only


That precision matters.



db vs repository


These only need to be separate when the separation buys clarity.
They are not automatically different folders at every scale.


db


db is for storage mechanics.


Typical contents:


schema definitions

migrations

table metadata

SQL/query-builder fragments

row mapping

transaction helpers

database-specific construction and persistence mechanics


Question answered:


How is data stored and queried mechanically?


repository


repository is for service-facing data access semantics.


Typical contents:


findById

insertTask

listAssignmentsForTask

markCompleted

aggregate load/save patterns

persistence operations expressed in service language


Question answered:


What data operations does this service/module need in its own terms?


Relationship


handler -> repository -> db


That is the ideal layering when the service is large enough to deserve it.


When to keep them combined


For small modules, splitting them can create fake architecture.


Small module rule:


keep db and repository together under one local area if the distinction is not yet buying much.


Larger module/service rule:


split them once storage mechanics and service-facing data semantics are diverging enough to make the split clarifying.


So the rule is:


small = combine locally
large = separate for clarity



What policies are


Policies are named semantic rule decisions.


They are not just convenience predicates.
They are not just errors.
They are not telemetry.


They answer:


Under what rule conditions is this behavior permitted, rejected, constrained, or modified?


Examples:


read-only mode rules

assignment limits

mutation eligibility

task visibility rules

lifecycle/state transition rules

permission checks tied to service semantics


A useful split is:


errors = how failure is expressed
policies = why a decision was made
observability = how that decision is recorded
repository = how data is accessed
db = how storage works


Who can use policies:


handlers

setup/middleware

repositories, when service-owned rule checks are needed before access

service observability/analytics layers

future composed services that must preserve the same decision logic


Policies become worth their own area when rule logic is repeated, important, or semantically named enough that it should not stay buried inline.



Shared database instance vs shared ownership


Multiple services can use the same database instance or the same host-provided dbPool without sharing semantic ownership.


This is the important distinction:


shared infrastructure != shared semantic ownership


Good default


same host: often yes

same Postgres instance: often yes

same dbPool: yes, often

same tables with multiple direct writers: usually no


Preferred model


Each service owns:


its own tables

its own migrations

its own repositories

its own write invariants


Even when several services use the same physical database.


Warning sign


If two services both want direct write authority over the same business tables/entities, one of two things is usually true:


they are actually one service with multiple modules, or

one service should be canonical owner and the other should go through it or through explicitly governed projections.


The shared-host model is not a reason to weaken semantic ownership.



When two “small services” are actually one service


If two candidate services deeply share:


domain entities

write invariants

policy logic

migration ownership

repository semantics


then they may not actually be separate services.


They may be one service with several modules.


This is important because the service structure is already modular. Separation should not be forced prematurely just because the repo can physically hold multiple services.


The question is not “can these be separate?”
The question is “does separate service ownership produce cleaner semantic truth?”


If not, use one service with multiple modules.



Extraction should not remain ambient judgment


One of the biggest risks is leaving “should this internal thing become shared or extracted?” as an open contextual question.


That creates drift.


The rule should be explicit:


Default


Keep internals nested and module-local unless an extraction trigger is hit.


Extraction triggers


Create an extraction candidate when:


a second module needs the same internal mechanism

repeated duplication appears

the internal subsystem becomes more stable than the service-specific logic around it

ownership/lifecycle/governance starts to diverge

the service boundary is being distorted to preserve nesting


Important rule


Hitting a trigger does not mean auto-extract.
It means open a governed extraction candidate.


Flow:


nested by default
  ↓
trigger hit
  ↓
record extraction candidate
  ↓
assess
  ↓
keep nested or extract deliberately


This turns fuzzy architectural taste into a manageable governance process.



Progression law


The whole internal structure should follow this progression:


module-local first
  ↓
service/shared if duplication is real
  ↓
package extraction only if cross-service support value is real


This progression matters because it preserves semantic truth at the smallest scope long enough for the real reuse seams to reveal themselves.


A package should earn its generality.
A shared internal seam should earn its centrality.
A module should keep ownership by default.



What this unlocks later


This internal structure is not just about tidiness.
It supports the later system in several ways.


1. Better graph law


Once modules, service-shared internals, and packages have distinct meanings, later Nx policy becomes much easier to encode without ambiguity.


2. Better scaffolding


Generators can scaffold the kernel pattern consistently because the progression law is explicit.


3. Better agent behavior


Agents get fewer ambient “should I move this?” questions because defaults and exceptions are clear.


4. Better observability and stewardship


Ownership zones become easier to observe, reason about, and assign stewardship over.


5. Better pressure-testing


Cross-boundary work becomes easier to evaluate because service-internal truth is not constantly leaking outward through premature package extraction.



Final law


If there is one rule to carry forward, it is this:


Services should be module-encapsulated by default.
Service-shared internals are earned exceptions.
Package extraction is a governed move, not a convenience refactor.


That is the internal structure law that best matches the larger goals of the system:


low ambiguity

strong semantic ownership

clear scaling from small to large

future graph enforcement

future scaffoldability

future autonomous stewardship


I tried to make a minimal, non-destructive update to the snapshot doc and keep the current structure intact, but the canvas edit failed because the text anchors I targeted did not match the live document exactly. Nothing was changed.


The updates I was trying to integrate were:


make explicit that the system does not want a first-class controller layer, because host/plugin/service/setup/handler already cover that territory cleanly,

add the rule that shared DB infrastructure is fine but shared semantic ownership is not the default,

and clarify that later Nx value is not just graph visibility, but encoding real semantic transitions once they are proven.


I still think those are worth folding in, lightly, and the right place is:


the main snapshot for the controller and shared-DB-ownership mental model at a high level,

the service-internal structure doc for the more precise internal-structure and promotion/extraction implications.


If you want, I’ll do a cleaner targeted rewrite pass next instead of pattern-based patching.
