So basically, like, would it make sense to basically say that for Nanoclaw, the only plugin that we would actually have is a standalone like steward or agent, and then that is basically just a declarative interface that pulls in skill packs plus identity and memory bindings plus, you know, channels. So it kind of just becomes a way to declare an agent. Does that make sense, or is there something I'm missing here?

Thought for 43s


Yes, that mostly makes sense.


The piece you were missing is the center of gravity.


A steward should not be “whatever NanoClaw needs.”
A steward should be a RAWR-native declaration of persistent agency that NanoClaw consumes.


That matches your product docs. RAWR says identity is stewards: persistent agent identities with domain scope, GitHub accounts, and accumulated decision history, not stateless personas. It also says NanoClaw is the agent dispatch runtime, while RAWR owns the opinions about how the pieces compose.


rawr-product-synthesis


rawr-product-synthesis


So the right model is:


RAWR defines the steward.
NanoClaw runs the steward.


That is the clean split.


The steward authoring model should therefore be declarative and RAWR-first. NanoClaw is just one runtime backend that consumes that declaration. That part of your instinct is right.


Where I would push back is on collapsing everything into “the only plugin is a steward.” That is too much responsibility in one noun. It will become mush fast.


A steward should declare:


identity

domain scope

memory bindings

allowed toolsets / skill packs

channel bindings

runtime preferences


But the steward should reference channels and skill packs, not own their implementation.


Why? Because channels and skill packs are reusable infrastructure or capability surfaces, while a steward is a persistent identity and operating boundary. Your own docs separate persistent steward identity from the workbench operations the steward uses. The workbench is a composed oRPC service with operations like orient, assess, contribute, govern, and observe, and the principle is that if an agent has to grep, infer, cross-reference, or guess, the workbench failed.


steward-workbench-design


steward-workbench-design


So a steward is not primarily “a bag of tools.”
A steward is “an identity plus scope plus memory plus allowed operating surfaces.”


Concretely, I think the right shape is this:


plugins/agents/* stays what you originally meant: cross-runtime skill packs for Claude/Codex/CLI agents.


plugins/channels/* becomes a real thing if you want channel adapters to be first-class in RAWR. That actually fits NanoClaw well, because NanoClaw has WhatsApp built in and expects additional channels like Telegram, Discord, and Slack to be added as code/skills in the fork rather than through a formal plugin API. In other words, NanoClaw does not really give you a plugin system; it gives you a small host that you extend by code, tools, and skills. NanoClaws.io


Then stewards/* becomes the declaration layer.


That gives you three distinct authoring centers:


skill packs: reusable agent capability/instruction/tool overlays

channel plugins: ingress/egress adapters

steward definitions: persistent identities that bind scope, memory, channels, and skill packs together


That is the clean architecture.


So yes, it makes sense for the NanoClaw integration on the RAWR side to mostly consume:


RAWR skill packs

RAWR channel plugins

RAWR steward definitions

RAWR workbench tools


But no, I would not call the steward itself “the NanoClaw plugin.” The steward is higher-level than that.


The simplest concrete mental model is:


RAWR
  defines:
    skill packs
    channel adapters
    steward declarations
    workbench/service tools

agent host
  adapts:
    RAWR declarations -> NanoClaw runtime

NanoClaw
  runs:
    sessions
    channels
    memory
    containers
    long-lived agent execution


That is the right gravity.


What would a steward look like concretely?


Something like this:


export const coordinationSteward = defineSteward({
  id: "coordination-steward",
  displayName: "Coordination Steward",
  domain: {
    scopes: ["@rawr/coordination", "@rawr/coordination-*"],
    agentsMdRoots: ["packages/coordination/AGENTS.md"],
  },
  identity: {
    githubAccount: "rawr-coordination-bot",
    autonomyProfile: "earned",
  },
  memory: {
    kind: "persistent",
    changelog: true,
    decisionHistory: true,
    claudeMdOverlay: "stewards/coordination/CLAUDE.md",
  },
  channels: [
    { ref: "local-dev-console" },
    { ref: "slack-engineering" },
  ],
  skills: [
    { ref: "rawr-core-navigation" },
    { ref: "rawr-contract-authoring" },
    { ref: "coordination-domain-pack" },
  ],
  tools: [
    { ref: "workbench.orient" },
    { ref: "workbench.assess" },
    { ref: "workbench.contribute" },
    { ref: "workbench.govern" },
    { ref: "workbench.observe" },
  ],
  runtime: {
    backend: "nanoclaw",
    containerProfile: "repo-write",
    swarmRole: "lead",
  },
});


That is a steward.


Notice what it is not doing:


it is not implementing Slack

it is not implementing the skill pack

it is not implementing the tool

it is not implementing NanoClaw itself


It is just binding those things into a persistent agent identity.


That is exactly the level you want.


Now the NanoClaw side.


NanoClaw’s actual center of gravity is:


one small process

channels

SQLite-backed persistent state

per-group CLAUDE.md memory

isolated containers

scheduled tasks

tools added directly in code

channels beyond WhatsApp added via code/skills in your fork, not through a real formal plugin API NanoClaws.io


That means the RAWR-to-NanoClaw adapter on the agent host should do translation, not invention.


It should:


register RAWR tools as NanoClaw tools

map RAWR channel plugins into NanoClaw channel handlers

materialize steward-specific memory overlays into NanoClaw’s storage / CLAUDE.md conventions

load steward skill packs into the runtime context

start one or more NanoClaw identities/sessions according to steward declarations


So the authoring center remains RAWR declarations, while NanoClaw is the runtime executor.


That also means your statement “our NanoClaw plugin would basically just be a declarative config” is close, but I would rename it.


It is not really a “NanoClaw plugin.”
It is better thought of as a steward declaration consumed by the agent host’s NanoClaw adapter.


That sounds pedantic, but it matters because NanoClaw itself does not present a serious first-class plugin API. Its own site leans hard on “small enough to understand,” “one process,” “no microservices,” and “no plugin API; just a function in src/tools/.” NanoClaws.io


So the architecture should reflect reality:


NanoClaw is not the plugin host

RAWR is


That is the core correction.


Now, on channels specifically: yes, I think channels should be a separate plugin type if you want this to be portable and pluggable in RAWR. That gives you a stable abstraction independent of NanoClaw, and then the NanoClaw adapter can consume that interface and wire it into NanoClaw’s runtime model. NanoClaw already expects channels to be added in code/skills; you can retrofit that by making your agent host generate or register those bindings from plugins/channels/* instead of hand-coding them every time. NanoClaws.io


So the final shape I would recommend is:


plugins/agents/*      reusable skill packs
plugins/channels/*    ingress/egress adapters
stewards/*            persistent agent declarations
apps/agent-host/*     NanoClaw integration + runtime adapter


And the steward declaration becomes the thing that says:


“I am this persistent agent.
I own this domain.
I can use these skill packs.
I am reachable on these channels.
I keep memory this way.
Run me on NanoClaw.”


That is clean.


The other reason this fits your product docs well is that RAWR is explicitly trying to make autonomous stewardship structural, not vibe-based. The workbench exists so the steward can ask one operation instead of grepping. govern.evaluate exists so autonomy can be earned and mechanically thresholded instead of improvised. observe.changelog exists so the steward can catch up between sessions.


steward-workbench-design


steward-workbench-design


steward-workbench-design


That means a steward in RAWR is not just “an agent config.”
It is the system-level identity that ties:


workbench access

trust history

domain scope

channels

memory

runtime


into one persistent boundary.


So yes, the steward should be declarative.
Yes, it should pull in skills, channels, memory bindings, and tool references.
Yes, that is the right place to “declare an agent.”


But do not let that flatten the system into one plugin type.


The clean boundary is:


channels are plugins

skills are plugins/packs

stewards are declarations

NanoClaw is the runtime host backend


That is the model that stays composable instead of collapsing into semantic soup.

Thought for 6s


Thought for 17s


Called tool


Thought for 22s


Called tool


Thought


Called tool


Thought


Called tool


Thought


Called tool


The cleanest categorization is: do not treat these as equivalent “plugin kinds.” They live on different axes.


In this repo, the target model is already explicit: shared capability logic lives in packages/*, runtime adapters live in plugins/*, final assembly lives in rawr.hq.ts, and hosts in apps/* mount those surfaces rather than inventing their own capability wiring


PLUGINS


[


SYSTEM


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/docs/SYSTEM.md). The current manifest already shows the shape: one composed oRPC router, one workflow trigger router, and one host-owned Inngest bundle per process[


rawr.hq


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/rawr.hq.ts).


So the right mental model is not “plugin type = deployment type.” It is:


packages/* = capability/kernel/domain

plugins/* = surface adapters

apps/* = actual hosts/processes

rawr.hq.ts = composition authority


From that, here is how I would categorize each.


API plugins


These are boundary-surface adapters for the shared server host. They are not workers, not sidecars, and not standalone services by default. They adapt package capabilities into caller-facing oRPC/OpenAPI routes. That is already the documented target: plugins/api/* own boundary contracts/routers, and the server mounts them into /rpc and /api/orpc/* via manifest composition


PLUGINS


[


ARCHITECTURE


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/docs/projects/orpc-ingest-workflows-spec/ARCHITECTURE.md)[


rawr


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/apps/server/src/rawr.ts).


So category:


logical category: boundary adapter

runtime category: server-mounted plugin

deployment category: usually in the main server process

process model: not a sidecar, not a worker


Workflow plugins


These are dual-surface plugins. This is the one people blur.


A workflow plugin has:


a caller-facing trigger surface mounted under /api/workflows/<capability>/*

a durable execution surface implemented as Inngest functions behind /api/inngest


That split is explicitly locked in the spec: API boundary and durable execution remain split; workflow triggers are caller-facing; /api/inngest is runtime ingress only; one runtime-owned Inngest bundle exists per process


ARCHITECTURE


. The manifest reflects that by exporting both workflows.triggerRouter and inngest.{ client, functions, handler }[


rawr.hq


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/rawr.hq.ts), and the server host mounts them in that order[


rawr


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/apps/server/src/rawr.ts).


So category:


logical category: durable-workflow adapter

runtime category: split between boundary trigger surface and workflow runtime surface

deployment category: usually shared with the server host at first, but conceptually the durable half is closer to a worker runtime

process model: not a sidecar; it is part server adapter, part durable worker topology


This is the key distinction: a workflow plugin is not “just another API plugin.” It straddles triggering and execution.


CLI plugins


These are local command-surface adapters. They are not server plugins at all. They adapt package capabilities into command invocations through the rawr entrypoint. The docs already call out plugins/cli/* as a plugin root and rawr.hq.ts as the place where CLI command registries are composed for host mounting


SYSTEM


.


So category:


logical category: operator/developer command adapter

runtime category: local process entry surface

deployment category: runs in the CLI host process when invoked

process model: ephemeral foreground process, not a server, not a sidecar, not a worker by default


Cron plugins


I would not make cron a peer semantic category beside API, workflows, and CLI.


Cron is not really a surface. It is a trigger mode.


If the cron job exists to kick off durable execution, it belongs under workflow plugins. The schedule is just another ingress into the workflow runtime. Inngest already naturally fits this shape. So “cron workflow” is a workflow plugin with a schedule trigger, not a new top-level plugin kind.


Only make a distinct cron plugin kind if you are saying, very specifically, “this thing is host-scheduled and not part of the durable workflow system.” That is a weaker, less coherent model.


So my recommendation:


do not create plugins/cron/* as a primary kind

treat cron as either:
a workflow trigger mode, or

a host scheduler registration attached to a worker/background runtime


Background plugins


This is too vague to be a good primary kind.


“Background” describes execution posture, not interface shape. It tells you that something should not block a request, but it does not tell you whether the thing is:


scheduled,

queue-driven,

event-driven,

durable,

ephemeral,

in-process,

isolated in another process.


So I would avoid background as a canonical plugin root. It is a smell category, like naming something utils.


If you keep it at all, it should be a second-order classification inside a more concrete kind:


workflow plugin with background execution

worker plugin with background consumption

API plugin that enqueues background work


Worker plugins


This one can be legitimate, but only if you define it sharply.


A worker plugin should mean: a plugin whose primary role is runtime execution/consumption, not boundary exposure. That could mean queue consumers, stream consumers, daemon loops, or continuously running processors. Unlike workflow plugins, which are specifically durable-execution adapters around an Inngest-style model, worker plugins would cover non-Inngest long-running runtime roles.


So category:


logical category: execution/runtime adapter

runtime category: non-boundary process role

deployment category: usually separate worker process, though could share host initially

process model: sibling application process, not sidecar by default


This is where your earlier sidecar question matters. A worker plugin is usually not a sidecar because it carries core application behavior, can scale independently, and is part of the product/system runtime rather than merely supporting another app instance.


The real categorization stack I would use in RAWR is this:


Primary plugin kinds by surface


api = caller-facing boundary adapter

workflows = durable trigger + execution adapter

cli = command adapter

web = frontend/web runtime adapter

agents = agent capability adapter

mcp = MCP action adapter


That matches the current docs exactly


PLUGINS


[


SYSTEM


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/docs/SYSTEM.md).


Then add a separate runtime-role axis:


Runtime roles


server-mounted

worker-executed

scheduled

ephemeral-cli

browser/web

agent-runtime


That is the missing clarity.


So if you expand the system, I would categorize your proposed additions like this:


API plugin: surface kind

Workflow plugin: surface kind with durable runtime

CLI plugin: surface kind

Cron: trigger mode, not primary kind

Background: execution posture, not primary kind

Worker: runtime role or possibly a distinct kind only for non-workflow execution plugins


That means the good model is not:


api | workflows | cli | cron | background | worker


That is mixing surface kinds with runtime roles and trigger styles.


The better model is:


Surface kinds:
api | workflows | cli | web | agents | mcp

Runtime roles / execution modes:
server-mounted | worker | scheduled | background | interactive


Then the combinations become obvious:


API plugin → server-mounted

Workflow plugin → server-mounted trigger surface + worker/durable execution runtime

CLI plugin → interactive/ephemeral

Scheduled workflow → workflow plugin + scheduled trigger

Queue consumer → worker plugin or workflow plugin, depending on whether it is Inngest-native durable execution

Background email sender → usually workflow plugin or worker runtime, not “background plugin”


Sanity check against the repo’s current host model:


The server host today mounts /api/inngest, /api/workflows/*, and /rpc + /api/orpc/* in explicit order


rawr


. The manifest is already the single authority that composes oRPC routes and Inngest runtime seams[


rawr.hq


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/rawr.hq.ts). That means your architecture already wants surface-first classification with host-owned runtime composition. Lean into that.


My blunt recommendation:


Do not add background as a top-level plugin type.
Do not add cron as a top-level plugin type.
Only add worker as a top-level plugin type if you need a real non-Inngest execution surface.


Otherwise:


cron belongs to workflows

background belongs to runtime semantics

worker is either workflows or a separate execution host class


The strongest version of the model is:


plugins/* should classify how the system is extended at an interface/runtime seam, not every incidental way code happens to run.


If you want, I can turn this into a concrete proposed directory + manifest contract for plugins/workers/* versus folding scheduled/background execution into plugins/workflows/*.
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
Lets say we expect to grow a little bit. Right now everything runs on one host. However, I can imagine that the host for the server vs. the async execution runs sent by inngest vs. background worker plugins may want their own processes or machines in the future. I don’t know.


What would the minimal yet performant architecture for this look like? Or is it really all fine running on “one host”? Should we keep one “host” mental model such that 1 plugin system : 1 host and the plugin types don’t matter? Or does that break?
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
The right answer is: keep one HQ/plugin system, but do not collapse that into “there is one host forever, so plugin types don’t matter.”


That breaks as soon as runtime behavior diverges.


The clean model is:


1 HQ/plugin system
N host roles


Not:


1 plugin system = 1 physical host/process forever


Your repo is already pointing in that direction. rawr.hq.ts is the single composition authority for surfaces and runtime seams, while host apps mount those exports instead of re-authoring capability wiring


rawr.hq


[


SYSTEM


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/docs/SYSTEM.md). The workflow architecture also explicitly separates boundary APIs from durable execution and says there is one runtime-owned Inngest bundle per process, not one globally forever[


ARCHITECTURE


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/docs/projects/orpc-ingest-workflows-spec/ARCHITECTURE.md). That “per process” wording matters.


So the mental model that scales is:


one logical HQ

one plugin registry/composition authority

potentially several runtime hosts/process roles


Those roles can all run on one machine today and separate later without changing plugin semantics.


The minimal yet performant architecture is this:


Stage 1: one machine, two or three processes max


Keep everything on one box if the load is modest. That is fine. What matters is separating runtime roles, not necessarily physical machines.


The default shape I would use is:


Machine A
  - process 1: server host
      mounts:
      - /rpc
      - /api/orpc/*
      - /api/workflows/<capability>/*
      - /api/inngest (if needed initially)

  - process 2: async runtime host
      runs:
      - Inngest durable execution
      - background worker plugins
      - scheduled jobs / cron triggers

  - optional process 3: web host / asset host
      only if needed separately


This is minimal, because it is still one machine.
This is performant, because you isolate request/response latency from async execution.
This is scalable, because you can move process 2 to another machine later with minimal conceptual churn.


That is the architecture I would recommend.


Why not keep literally everything in one process?


Because the first real split in systems like this is not “microservices.” It is foreground vs background.


Your server host has different requirements:


low latency

stable memory

predictable tail latency

request-scoped auth/context

easy horizontal scaling later


Your async execution host has different requirements:


retries

long-running work

burst tolerance

looser latency requirements

separate concurrency controls

safe failure isolation


If you keep them in one process too long, this will break because:


a burst of async work will steal CPU and memory from user-facing requests

one bad workflow/plugin can destabilize the main server

concurrency tuning becomes impossible without compromising one side

observability gets muddier because foreground and background work share one pool and one failure domain


So “one host” is fine as a machine model for now.
It is not fine as a single runtime role model once you expect growth.


That means the best mental model is:


one system, one manifest, multiple host roles


I would define the host roles like this.


1. Server host


This is the caller-facing boundary host.


It mounts:


API plugins

workflow trigger routers

maybe web plugins if server-served

maybe CLI is separate, not here


Its job is:


accept requests

authenticate/authorize

validate contracts

enqueue or trigger async work

return responses quickly


In your current architecture, this corresponds to the mounted route families in apps/server/src/rawr.ts: /api/inngest, /api/workflows/*, and /rpc + /api/orpc/* in explicit order


rawr


. But for future shape, I would treat /api/inngest as the thing most likely to move off this host first in responsibility, even if the HTTP ingress point remains nearby.


2. Async execution host


This is the execution/runtime host.


It runs:


Inngest durable functions

worker plugins

scheduled triggers

queue consumers

long-running/background logic


Its job is not to be the public server.
Its job is to execute work reliably.


This host is where I would put:


workflow durable execution

worker plugins

cron/scheduled plugins


That gives you one clean runtime class for “non-request work.”


3. CLI host


This is already separate by nature. CLI plugins are not part of the long-running server topology. They are invoked processes. Treat them as their own host class conceptually, even if they run on the same machine during development.


Now the plugin categorization becomes much cleaner.


Do not think “plugin types don’t matter.”
They do matter, but not because each one deserves its own machine.


They matter because each plugin type attaches to one or more host roles.


A better mapping looks like this:


API plugins
  -> server host

Workflow plugins
  -> server host (trigger surface)
  -> async execution host (durable execution)

Worker plugins
  -> async execution host

Cron/scheduled behavior
  -> async execution host
  -> usually as a trigger mode, not a whole new top-level worldview

CLI plugins
  -> CLI host

Web plugins
  -> web host and/or server host depending on delivery model


That is the model that survives growth.


Now the practical question: is one machine still okay?


Yes, absolutely, if you split by process role.


A very good near-term setup is:


Railway service A: server
Railway service B: async
Optional Railway service C: web
Shared Postgres / Redis / backing services


Or if you truly want one deployed machine:


one VM/container host
  supervises:
    bun run server
    bun run async


That is enough for a long time.


The thing I would not do is make plugin type the unit of deployment. That becomes nonsense fast.


You do not want:


one process for each API plugin

one process for each workflow plugin

one process for each worker plugin


That is fake modularity and real ops pain.


The deployment unit should be host role, not plugin kind.


So the actual answer to your “1 plugin system : 1 host” question is:


Keep 1 plugin system : 1 composition authority.
Do not keep 1 plugin system : 1 runtime host forever.


That breaks once execution modes differ.


The best durable mental model is:


One HQ/plugin system
  -> one manifest/composition authority
  -> many plugin surfaces
  -> mounted into a small number of host roles
  -> each host role can run as one process today and separate later


That gives you minimal complexity now and a clean migration path later.


Here is the staged path I would recommend.


Stage A: now


one machine

one server process

one async process

same repo, same plugin system, same manifest authority


Stage B: moderate growth


separate deploy units for server and async

same plugin contracts

same rawr.hq composition semantics

host-specific mounting/build steps


Stage C: only if needed


isolate especially hot or risky worker classes

maybe dedicated workflow runner or heavy compute worker

maybe separate web serving

still do not split by plugin kind blindly


Here is the sanity check.


If a runtime role has different needs in:


latency,

concurrency,

failure tolerance,

scaling,

uptime,

or resource profile,


then it should probably be its own host process.


By that test:


server and async should be separate soon

CLI is already separate by nature

web may or may not be separate

individual plugin kinds usually should not each get their own process


My blunt recommendation:


Do not keep the mental model that plugin types do not matter.
Do not keep the mental model that one HQ equals one forever-host.


Keep:


one HQ

one manifest authority

two primary long-running host roles: server and async

optional web and CLI host roles as needed


That is the minimal architecture that is still honest.


The baseline version in concrete terms:


HQ / plugin system
  rawr.hq.ts = composition authority

Host roles
  server-host:
    mounts api + workflow triggers

  async-host:
    runs inngest durable execution + worker plugins + schedules

  cli-host:
    runs cli plugins


That is the model I would lock.


The main sanity check against your current repo is good: the codebase already distinguishes surface composition from host mounting, and already treats Inngest runtime composition as process-owned rather than as a universal singleton


ARCHITECTURE


[


rawr.hq


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/rawr.hq.ts)[


rawr


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/apps/server/src/rawr.ts).


So the move is not to invent a new worldview. It is to finish the one the repo is already hinting at.
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
Check our project files for the design snapshot for semantics. We planned to distinguish service orpc private clients from packages.


Would those effectively be running on the worker process you mentioned? Or is it flexible (can be called in-process by wherever caller calls OR use RPC link)? If so, how would we even do that? Would we expose an internal port for things to call into over RPC? Would we just directly “mount” the private service routers on that worker, much the same we would on the API server?


I guess that’s where “worker” as a plugin makes the most sense right? And a worker plugin is either an orpc router or a cron scheduler or another interface?
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
Called tool


Thought for 31s


Called tool


Yes — but only if you keep the distinction clean.


The model you were pushing toward in prior design discussions is basically:


packages/* = pure/shared capability logic and schemas

services/* or service-owned oRPC shells = private runtime-callable capability boundaries

plugins/* = caller/runtime adapters

hosts/* or apps/* = actual processes that mount and run things


That means the private service client is not inherently “the worker thing.” It is a call boundary that can be used in different execution contexts.


The repo snapshot I can verify right now does support that direction, even if I could not cleanly locate your exact “design snapshot” doc through the available search path. The current architecture already locks in three relevant ideas:


First, host composition is manifest-owned, not plugin-owned. rawr.hq.ts composes routers and the Inngest bundle, then hosts mount those seams


rawr.hq


.


Second, server-internal callers are supposed to use in-process package clients by default, not local HTTP self-calls. That is stated directly in the workflow architecture doc: “server-internal callers use in-process package internal clients with trusted service context and no local HTTP self-calls as default”


ARCHITECTURE


.


Third, plugins own boundary contracts, while packages own shared domain logic and internal client/service layers only


ARCHITECTURE


.


That combination strongly implies this answer:


A private service oRPC client should be callable:


in-process, by default

optionally over RPC later, if the service is mounted in another host/process


So yes, it is flexible.
No, it should not start life as “must be a network hop.”


That is the whole point of having a service boundary separate from the package core.


The right mental model is:


package logic = pure capability implementation
service router/client = private callable boundary for that capability
plugin = adapter that exposes or triggers capability on some surface
host = process that mounts service/plugin routers and executes them


Now to your concrete question:


Would those private service clients effectively be running on the worker process?


Sometimes, yes.
But not by definition.


They run wherever the mounted service router lives.


That means there are two valid modes.


Mode 1 is the default and should be your baseline:
the caller and service are in the same process, so the “private client” is just an in-process router client.


That is already what the repo does in rawr.hq.ts. It creates a router client against supportExampleRouter with injected context, no network involved, then enriches host context with that client for downstream callers


rawr.hq


.


That is the pattern to preserve.


Mode 2 is the scale-out mode:
the service router is mounted on another host/process, and the caller uses an RPC transport link instead of in-process invocation.


That gives you the exact same semantic boundary with a different transport.


So the proper abstraction is not “worker = service.”
It is:


service boundary
  -> in-process transport now
  -> RPC transport later if mounted elsewhere


That is what you want.


How would you do that?


You define the service contract/router once.
Then provide two client constructors:


createInProcessServiceClient(...)
createRemoteServiceClient(...)


Both satisfy the same interface.


Then your host context decides which one to inject.


For example:


type BillingServiceClient = {
  invoice: {
    create(input: CreateInvoiceInput): Promise<CreateInvoiceOutput>;
  };
};

function resolveBillingServiceClient(env: {
  mode: "in-process" | "remote";
}): BillingServiceClient {
  if (env.mode === "in-process") {
    return createRouterClient(billingServiceRouter, {
      context: createTrustedServiceContext(),
    });
  }

  return createRpcLinkClient({
    baseUrl: process.env.BILLING_SERVICE_INTERNAL_URL!,
  });
}


That is the real unlock.


Now, should you expose an internal port for things to call into over RPC?


Only when the service is actually running in another process or another machine.


Do not add an internal port just because it feels architecture-y.
That is fake complexity.


Start with in-process private service clients.
When a service/runtime role moves to another host, mount its private router there and give it an internal-only port or route family.


So yes, if you later separate an async host, you may absolutely mount private service routers there, much like you mount boundary routers on the API server.


But there is an important difference:


API server mounts public/boundary routers

async/worker host mounts private/internal service routers and execution surfaces


That means the worker host can expose an internal-only oRPC surface for trusted callers.


Something like:


server host
  public:
    /rpc
    /api/orpc/*
    /api/workflows/*

worker host
  internal only:
    /internal/rpc
    /internal/services/*
    /api/inngest


Or even just one internal /rpc on a private network.


The point is not the exact path. The point is that the worker host can mount private routers just like the server host mounts public ones.


Now the harder question: should a worker plugin be the abstraction for this?


Mostly yes — but not exactly the way you phrased it.


A worker plugin makes sense if it means:


“a runtime plugin that contributes execution behavior to the async host.”


That execution behavior might include:


mounting private internal service routers

registering background consumers

registering schedules

registering Inngest functions

registering queue handlers or daemons


That is coherent.


But the worker plugin should not itself be “an oRPC router or a cron scheduler or another interface” in a vague untyped blob.


It should be a structured registration surface.


For example:


type WorkerPluginRegistration = {
  capability: string;

  internalRouters?: Array<{
    namespace: string;
    router: unknown;
  }>;

  schedules?: Array<{
    name: string;
    cron: string;
    run(ctx: WorkerContext): Promise<void>;
  }>;

  consumers?: Array<{
    name: string;
    start(ctx: WorkerContext): Promise<Disposable>;
  }>;

  inngest?: {
    functions: InngestFunction.Any[];
  };
};


That is a real abstraction.


Then the async host composes these registrations.


That is where “worker plugin” actually has semantic value:
it is not just “stuff that runs in background.”
It is “stuff mounted into the async host role.”


That gives you a clean split:


plugins/api/* = public boundary adapters for server host

plugins/workflows/* = workflow trigger + durable execution adapters

plugins/worker/* = async-host runtime adapters for private services, schedules, consumers

plugins/cli/* = CLI adapters


Now the leverage/complexity tradeoff.


You do not want every capability forced into a remote private-service model from day one.
That will break because you will spend a ton of effort on internal networking, auth, service discovery, and transport plumbing before you need it.


The minimal correct architecture is:


Phase 1:


private service routers exist conceptually

clients are in-process by default

worker host may still share the same machine

remote transport is optional and adapter-based


Phase 2:


when a service/runtime role moves out of process, swap its client factory to RPC link

mount its private routers on the async host

expose only an internal port/network surface


So the clean answer to your core question is:


Yes, private service oRPC clients can run on the worker process.
No, they are not defined by the worker process.
They should be transport-flexible:


in-process when colocated

RPC when remote


And yes, that is exactly where a worker plugin starts to make real sense:
not as “background stuff generally,” but as the plugin surface for the async host, where you mount private service routers, schedules, consumers, and durable execution pieces.


The sanity check against the current repo is strong:


manifest-first composition already exists
rawr.hq

hosts already mount distinct route families explicitly
rawr

internal callers are already supposed to prefer in-process clients over local HTTP self-calls
ARCHITECTURE


So the natural extension is not to abandon that.
It is to formalize one more host role and one more plugin surface.


My recommendation, concretely:


Lock this model:


packages/*          pure/shared capability logic
services/*          private callable service boundaries (router + client factory)
plugins/api/*       public boundary adapters
plugins/workflows/* workflow trigger + durable execution adapters
plugins/worker/*    async-host adapters (private routers, schedules, consumers)
apps/server/*       public host
apps/worker/*       async/internal host
rawr.hq.ts          composition authority


And inside services/*, always define the client in transport-neutral terms, with:


an in-process implementation first

an RPC-link implementation later


That gives you the minimal architecture without boxing yourself in.


The one uncertainty ledger item: I could not directly pull the exact prior “design snapshot” file you referenced from the currently accessible repo tooling, so I am grounding this answer on the current architecture docs and manifest code rather than that specific snapshot. The direction above matches those current files cleanly.
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
Okay so putting all of this together, what should our “host” model finally look like to support both the single-process mode and the scale-out mode?


Earlier I thought we landed on a model where “host” was something we could bundle as a “host client” and deploy on any infra/machine. The multi-process scale-out make sense, but it sounds like you were still talking about one machine…


Is that the right model? Why not have each process on its own machine? or is that more just a matter of simple railway config and is immaterial to the rest? Why would you force everything into one machine—don’t separate processes still clog the throughput?


More importantly, how do we retain the simple clean mental model when we want l, say, some APIs to graduate into their own ecosystem? Basically, say that a domain grows to be its own thing, with enough services + plugins to merit its own host?


Is that the same or different as deploying individual plugins as individual machines?


———


Don’t let me questions confuse you — answer the underlying thread/question cohesively, not piecemeal
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
Called tool


Yes. The final host model should be:


One RAWR HQ can be packaged and deployed anywhere as a host bundle, but a host bundle is not “one machine” and not “one process.” It is a deployable runtime composition unit that can run in single-process, multi-process same-machine, or multi-machine mode without changing the core semantics.


That is the clean model.


The repo already wants this. rawr.hq.ts is the single cross-surface composition authority, while host apps mount the composed exports and do not author capability wiring themselves


RAWR_HQ_MANIFEST_COMPOSITION


[


rawr.hq


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/rawr.hq.ts). The architecture docs also lock that workflow/API surfaces are separate and that the Inngest bundle is owned per process, which is the key clue that runtime topology is allowed to fan out without changing the system model[


ARCHITECTURE


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/docs/projects/orpc-ingest-workflows-spec/ARCHITECTURE.md).


So the final mental model should be:


A host is a deployable runtime assembly built from one manifest/composition authority.
A runtime role is a process class inside that host assembly.
A machine is just where one or more runtime roles happen to run.


That gives you three levels:


RAWR HQ / Host Bundle
  -> runtime roles (server, async, cli, web, maybe agent)
      -> deployed as 1 or more processes
          -> placed on 1 or more machines


That is the missing simplification.


Right now you were conflating host with machine and sometimes with process. That is what was making the model wobble.


The right final model is not “force everything onto one machine.” It is not “every process gets its own machine” either. Both of those are deployment choices, not architecture truth.


The architecture truth is:


one host bundle can materialize as multiple runtime roles


and


those runtime roles can be colocated or separated


That means the “host client” idea still holds, but it needs one refinement. It should not mean “one binary that always runs everything in one process.” It should mean:


Host Kit / Host Bundle = the packaged distributable authority for a RAWR instance, including:


manifest composition

shared config

capability registry

plugin registry

host role definitions

deployment adapters / entrypoints


Then from that same bundle, you can launch:


server role

async role

cli role

web role


Sometimes all on one machine.
Sometimes split.
Same semantics either way.


That is the durable model.


Here is the final architecture I would lock.


The core unit is the HQ host bundle.


It contains:


rawr.hq.ts as composition authority

packages/* shared capability logic

services/* private callable service boundaries

plugins/* runtime surface adapters

apps/* or hosts/* runtime role entrypoints


Then define a small fixed set of runtime roles.


The minimal set is:


server
async
cli
web (optional)


Where:


Server role
Owns caller-facing boundaries.
Mounts:


public oRPC / OpenAPI APIs

workflow trigger APIs

maybe web delivery if combined


Async role
Owns background execution.
Runs:


Inngest durable execution

worker plugins

schedules / cron

internal/private service routers if needed


CLI role
Owns command execution.


Web role
Only if you want separate frontend/runtime hosting.


That is the right abstraction boundary.


Now the direct answer to your “single-process mode and scale-out mode” question:


The host model should support three deployment modes with the same internal semantics.


Mode 1: single-process dev/simple mode


1 host bundle
1 process
1 machine
server + async + maybe web mounted together


Use this when you want maximum simplicity.
Fine for dev and light production.


Mode 2: multi-process colocated mode


1 host bundle
2-3 processes
1 machine
process A = server
process B = async
process C = web optional


This is the real “minimal but honest” production mode.
Same machine, separate processes, lower ops complexity.


Mode 3: scale-out distributed mode


1 host bundle
2-3 deploy units
N machines/services
server on machine A
async on machine B
web on machine C optional


This is the same architecture, different placement.


That is why “same machine vs different machine” is mostly immaterial to the mental model. It matters for performance and ops, but it should not change the semantics of your system.


Now to the important challenge you raised: why not just put each process on its own machine immediately?


Because machine isolation is expensive in ways process isolation is not.


You do not pay just CPU cost. You pay:


more deployment units

more config

service discovery

internal auth between services

network latency

network failure modes

more observability surface area

more cost

more operational drift


If the real reason to split is only “these two things should not share event-loop/memory pressure,” then separate process solves most of that already. Different processes on the same machine do not share a Bun event loop or JS heap. They are isolated enough for most early-stage needs.


So your throughput concern is slightly off. Separate processes on one machine do not “still clog the throughput” in the same naive way as a single process. They still share machine resources, yes, but that is a different problem. It is much easier to reason about and often plenty until one role truly needs more resources than the box can give.


Sanity check:


Single process:
  shared event loop
  shared heap
  shared crash domain

Separate processes, same machine:
  separate event loops
  separate heaps
  separate crash domains
  shared CPU/RAM/disk/network capacity

Separate machines:
  everything above plus separate host capacity and failure domains
  but more network/ops complexity


So the normal path is:


split by process first

split by machine second

split by ecosystem last


That is the right leverage order.


Now the deeper part: how do you keep the mental model clean when some APIs or domains “graduate” into their own ecosystem?


This is where you need one more distinction:


A host is not a plugin.
A host is not a domain.
A host is a deployable assembly around a coherent bounded system.


So when one domain grows enough to become its own thing, you do not deploy individual plugins as individual machines by default. You promote a coherent cluster of packages/services/plugins into its own host bundle.


That means the graduation path is:


inside Host A:
  packages/payments
  services/payments
  plugins/api/payments
  plugins/workflows/payments
  plugins/worker/payments

later:

Host B = Payments HQ
  packages/payments/*
  services/payments/*
  plugins/*/payments/*
  its own rawr.hq.ts
  its own runtime roles


That is fundamentally different from “deploy each plugin as its own machine.”


Why?


Because plugins are surface adapters around capabilities. They are too fine-grained to be a stable deployment unit.


A domain becoming its own host means:


it has enough internal coherence

it needs its own runtime roles

it has multiple surfaces and services

it can own its own scaling and lifecycle

it deserves its own composition authority


That is real separation.


Deploying an individual plugin as an individual machine is usually fake separation. It creates tiny brittle deploy units around surface adapters rather than around actual bounded systems.


So the rule should be:


Scale out by runtime role first.
Split into a new host only when a bounded capability family becomes its own system.
Do not split by plugin unless the plugin is effectively the entire system.


That gives you a very clean ladder.


The final host model, put together, looks like this:


RAWR Host Bundle
  composition authority:
    rawr.hq.ts

  core layers:
    packages/*   shared capability logic
    services/*   private service boundaries
    plugins/*    surface/runtime adapters

  runtime roles:
    server
    async
    cli
    web optional

  deployment modes:
    single-process
    multi-process colocated
    multi-machine distributed


And the plugin mapping becomes:


api plugins
  -> server role

workflow plugins
  -> server role for trigger surfaces
  -> async role for durable execution

worker plugins
  -> async role

cli plugins
  -> cli role

web plugins
  -> web role and/or server role


That preserves the simple model while supporting scale-out.


Now the exact “host client” formulation I would use is this:


Do not call it just a “host client” if that suggests one client binary running everything.


Call it something closer to:


Host Kit

Host Bundle

HQ Runtime Bundle

HQ Distribution Kit


Because what you are really distributing is:


one semantic system

one composition authority

multiple possible runtime entrypoints


Then each infra target just decides how to place those entrypoints.


For example, on Railway:


one service for server

one service for async

one optional service for web


On a single VM:


one supervisor starts all three processes


On Encore or another substrate:


same roles, different deployment mechanics


That is why the infra choice is mostly orthogonal once the host model is right.


The final sanity check against your current repo is strong:


composition belongs in rawr.hq.ts, not in hosts
RAWR_HQ_MANIFEST_COMPOSITION

host apps mount composed surfaces, not capability logic directly
RAWR_HQ_MANIFEST_COMPOSITION

the workflow architecture already distinguishes caller-facing vs durable execution and says the Inngest bundle is process-owned, not universal
ARCHITECTURE

the current server host mount order already treats route/runtime families explicitly rather than pretending everything is one undifferentiated app
rawr


So the final answer is:


Keep one host bundle mental model, not one-machine mental model.
Inside that host bundle, define a few stable runtime roles.
Let those roles run in one process, many processes on one machine, or many machines.
When a domain grows into its own ecosystem, give it its own host bundle, not one machine per plugin.


This will break only if you make plugins the unit of deployment. That is the wrong granularity.


The right granularity is:


plugin < runtime role < host bundle < ecosystem


That is the cohesive model to lock.
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
Wouldn’t it make sense, to some extent, to just make all async work flow through inngest?


we would still have an async host, but all requests to it flow through inngest triggers so that we get all of the durable execution benefits, retries, observability, etc.
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
Called tool


Yes, to a point. But not as a universal law.


The right model is:


Use Inngest as the default durability harness for async workflows.
Do not force literally all async activity through Inngest if some of it is really just local background execution, stream consumption, tight internal daemons, or infrastructure support work.


That distinction matters.


Your own architecture docs already lean this way. They explicitly lock that API-exposed workflow triggers dispatch into Inngest durable execution, and that durable execution functions remain Inngest function definitions


08-workflow-api-boundaries


. They also keep caller-facing workflow APIs separate from /api/inngest, which is runtime ingress only[


08-workflow-api-boundaries


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/docs/projects/orpc-ingest-workflows-spec/axes/08-workflow-api-boundaries.md). So the system already assumes Inngest is the canonical path for durable async workflows.


That is good. I would keep that.


Where I would push back is on the word “all.”


The strong version that works is:


“All business-level async work that benefits from durability, retries, timelines, scheduling, and observability should flow through Inngest.”


That is a very good default.


The bad version is:


“All async work of any kind must go through Inngest.”


That will break because not every async thing is a workflow.


Some async work is really one of these:


A tiny fire-and-forget local side effect that you can safely await or queue in-process.


A long-lived worker/consumer loop that is driven by an external system and does not map cleanly to event-triggered durable functions.


A very hot, high-throughput internal execution path where the eventing/durable envelope adds unnecessary overhead.


A support/runtime concern like cache warming, telemetry flushing, websocket fanout, or local cleanup.


Those are not all the same animal.


So the clean mental model is:


Inngest should be the default async orchestration path.
Worker plugins still exist, but their primary job is not “invent another durable execution system.”
Their primary job is to contribute one of:


Inngest functions

schedules that dispatch into Inngest

consumers/adapters that translate external stimuli into Inngest events

rare non-Inngest background runtimes where that is actually the better fit


That is the best version of the architecture.


In other words, the async host becomes mostly an Inngest runtime host plus adapter host.


That is strong.


The shape would look like this:


server host
  -> API plugins
  -> workflow trigger plugins
  -> sends events / trigger requests

async host
  -> Inngest serve handler
  -> Inngest durable functions
  -> worker adapters that feed or support Inngest
  -> rare direct background consumers where Inngest is not the right primitive


That gives you the durability benefits by default without pretending every background concern is a workflow graph.


This is also how to keep the model simple.


Do not think:


API plugins do API

workflow plugins do Inngest

worker plugins do random other async stuff


That gets muddy.


Think:


workflow plugins define caller-facing workflow triggers plus durable execution

worker plugins define async-host runtime adapters

most worker plugins should either feed Inngest or host private/internal execution seams around it


That keeps the async side coherent.


The practical policy I would lock is this:


Route async work through Inngest when the work needs one or more of:


retries

idempotent durable execution

step-level timelines

observability of execution flow

scheduling

fan-out/fan-in orchestration

resumability

decoupling from request lifecycle


That covers most business async work.


Do not force Inngest when the work is:


ultra-low-latency and local

continuously running rather than event/run based

infrastructural rather than business workflow

external-consumer shaped in a way that would become awkward if wrapped as a workflow first


A good sanity check is:


If the unit of work naturally wants a run ID, retries, status, and a timeline,
it probably belongs in Inngest.

If it naturally wants a socket, a loop, a stream, or a daemon lifecycle,
it may not.


Now to your specific “we would still have an async host” point.


Yes. Absolutely.


Even if most async work flows through Inngest, you still want an async host role because something has to own:


/api/inngest runtime ingress

function registration

private internal routers if needed

schedule registration

external event consumers that bridge into Inngest

concurrency tuning for async execution

isolation from request/response traffic


So Inngest does not remove the async host.
It clarifies what the async host mostly is.


It becomes less “generic worker bucket” and more:


durable execution host


That is better.


Then the plugin semantics sharpen:


plugins/workflows/*


own trigger contracts/routers and Inngest functions

are the canonical business async path


plugins/worker/*


own async-host adapters

may register schedules, consumers, bridges, internal routers

usually dispatch into shared services and often into Inngest

only sometimes own direct non-Inngest execution


That is a much better split than “workflow vs worker are two competing async systems.”


You do not want two parallel async ideologies in one repo. Your docs already reject that kind of dual-path drift in the workflow boundary model


08-workflow-api-boundaries


. Keep that discipline.


So the answer is:


Yes, make Inngest the default route for async business work.
No, do not turn it into a religious rule for every asynchronous codepath.


My concrete recommendation:


Treat Inngest as the canonical async execution substrate for business workflows.

Keep the async host as a separate runtime role.

Let worker plugins exist, but define them narrowly as async-host adapters.

Make schedules usually dispatch into Inngest rather than directly running domain logic.

Allow direct non-Inngest worker execution only by exception.


That yields a very clean architecture:


packages/*     shared capability logic
services/*     private callable service boundaries

plugins/api/*        boundary APIs
plugins/workflows/*  trigger + durable execution via Inngest
plugins/worker/*     async-host adapters, bridges, schedules, consumers

server host   -> boundary traffic
async host    -> durable execution + async adapters


That is probably the best balance of simplicity, performance, and future scale.


The main thing to avoid is a world where:


some async business features use Inngest

some use ad hoc workers

some use cron directly

some use hidden queue consumers


That will fragment your observability and your mental model immediately.


So bias hard toward Inngest as the default async path.
Keep exceptions explicit.
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say: Okay wait — would it make more sense to remove the workflow trigger surface concern from the workflow plugin and just make it clear that it is solely where you define the execution bundle and not anything else? and then any actual workflow API would necessarily be part of an API plugin that allows directly calling workflows from the outside? Not all workflows would necessarily be exposed directly as part of an API, but inngest bundles would contain both internal and externally callable workflows — but they’re all the same bundle because they all run on the same process when inngest calls.
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
Thought for 17s


Yes. That is the cleaner model.


The workflow plugin should primarily own execution, not public exposure.


What you are circling is the right separation:


workflow plugin = defines durable execution units, schedules, internal trigger handles, Inngest function bundle

API plugin = decides what, if anything, is exposed to outside callers

worker/async host = mounts and serves the Inngest execution bundle


That is a better model than making workflow plugins own a public trigger surface by default.


Why this is cleaner:


A workflow is not inherently an API. It is an execution capability.


Some workflows are:


externally callable

only internally callable

only scheduled

only event-driven

only chained from other workflows


If the workflow plugin owns a public trigger router, you accidentally imply every workflow wants a first-class external surface. That is false, and it will pollute the plugin model.


So the cleaner layering is:


packages/*            shared business logic
services/*            private callable service boundaries

plugins/workflows/*   execution bundle + internal trigger contracts
plugins/api/*         public routes that may call workflows
plugins/worker/*      async adapters/schedules/consumers if separate from workflows

server host           mounts public APIs
async host            mounts Inngest bundle / execution runtime


The key distinction is this:


A workflow plugin should still expose a typed trigger interface.
It just should not automatically expose a public HTTP trigger surface.


That means the workflow plugin can export things like:


type SendDigestWorkflow = {
  trigger(input: SendDigestInput): Promise<{ runId: string }>;
};


or a richer registration object:


type WorkflowPluginRegistration = {
  capability: "notifications";
  workflows: {
    sendDigest: {
      triggerSchema: SendDigestInputSchema;
      trigger(input: SendDigestInput, ctx: WorkflowTriggerContext): Promise<TriggerResult>;
      function: InngestFunctionDef;
    };
  };
  schedules?: [...];
};


Then an API plugin can choose to map that to a public route:


POST /notifications/send-digest
  -> validate caller
  -> call notificationsWorkflows.sendDigest.trigger(...)


And an internal service can also call the same trigger.
And a schedule can call the same trigger.
And another workflow can call it too.


That is exactly the right kind of reuse.


So the shape becomes:


Workflow plugin owns:


workflow definitions

triggerable handles

schedules

event mappings

Inngest bundle registration

internal metadata about whether something is externally exposable or not


API plugin owns:


auth

public contract

caller experience

rate limiting

external route semantics

deciding which workflows are public at all


That separation is healthy.


The thing I would not do is make API plugins reach directly into raw Inngest internals in an ad hoc way. That gets sloppy fast.


So the best version is not:


“Workflow plugin is just raw function definitions and nothing else.”


It should be:


“Workflow plugin is the execution module plus typed internal trigger surface, but not the public boundary.”


That is the important refinement.


Otherwise you will end up re-implementing trigger semantics in every API plugin and lose consistency.


The final mental model should be:


workflow plugin = execution authority
api plugin      = exposure authority


That is the cleanest split.


On your other point: yes, internal and externally callable workflows can absolutely live in the same Inngest bundle.


That is normal.


Because the bundle is really just:


all functions this async host knows how to run


Whether a given workflow is triggered by:


public API

private service call

event bridge

cron schedule

another workflow


is a separate concern from where it executes.


So this is a good model:


one async host
  -> one Inngest runtime bundle
      -> workflows from many capabilities
         some public-triggerable
         some internal-only
         some scheduled-only


That is coherent.


The real boundary is not “public vs internal bundle.”
It is “public trigger path vs internal trigger path.”


Those can both target the same execution bundle.


This also solves your earlier host model cleanly:


server host owns boundary exposure

async host owns durable execution

workflow plugins define execution capabilities

API plugins decide which capabilities are externally callable


That is much cleaner than embedding boundary concerns inside workflow plugins.


The main caveat:


Do not make API plugins the only place where trigger contracts exist.
The workflow plugin should still define the canonical trigger input/output types and trigger helper/client. Otherwise the public layer becomes the de facto source of truth for workflow invocation, which is backward.


So the lock I would use is:


Workflow plugin owns:
- canonical workflow IDs
- trigger schemas
- execution functions
- schedule definitions
- internal trigger client/helpers

API plugin owns:
- external routes
- exposure policy
- auth and transport
- mapping public requests to workflow triggers


That gives you one canonical execution definition and many possible ingress paths.


That is the right model.
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say: Should we separate the worker host from a general async host for better semantics?
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
Received app response


No. Keep async host as the primary host semantic.


Do not split it into “async host” and “worker host” as two top-level host concepts unless you already know they are materially different runtime classes.


That split sounds cleaner for five minutes, then it muddies the architecture.


Why it muddies it:


“Async” is the real umbrella runtime role.
It includes:


Inngest durable execution

schedules

event consumers

internal trigger bridges

background jobs

worker-style consumers


“Worker” is only one subtype inside that world.


So if you make worker host peer to async host, you create an overlap problem immediately:


where do Inngest functions live?

where do cron/schedules live?

where do queue consumers that only trigger workflows live?

where do internal async adapters live?


You end up either duplicating semantics or inventing arbitrary rules.


The repo’s current manifest shape already supports the cleaner model: one manifest owns both the workflow trigger surface and one Inngest execution bundle, with the runtime bundle treated as host-owned composition rather than as some separate semantic universe


rawr.hq


. That strongly suggests the right abstraction is a single async runtime host that owns async execution concerns broadly.


So the clean model is:


server host = caller-facing sync boundary host
async host  = non-request execution host
cli host    = command host
web host    = frontend host optional


Inside the async host, you can still have multiple runtime lanes:


async host
  - workflow lane     (Inngest durable execution)
  - scheduler lane    (cron / timed dispatch)
  - consumer lane     (queues / streams / webhooks-to-events)
  - worker lane       (direct background processors when not using Inngest)
  - internal rpc lane (private service routers if needed)


That is the right level of precision.


So “worker” should usually be:


a plugin category, or

a lane inside the async host,
not a separate top-level host semantic.


The leverage/complexity tradeoff is straightforward.


If you create both:


async host

worker host


you now need to explain the difference forever. And the truthful answer will usually be “worker is one kind of async,” which means the split bought you nothing.


The only time I would separate them is if “worker host” becomes a real operational class with distinct requirements such as:


very high-throughput queue consumption

GPU or heavy CPU processing

separate autoscaling policy

different language/runtime

different failure tolerance

no shared relationship to Inngest workflows


At that point, yes, a dedicated worker host can make sense. But then it is not a semantic cleanup move. It is an operational specialization.


So the rule should be:


Keep async host as the canonical host role.
Treat worker as an execution subtype within async.
Promote worker into its own host only when there is an actual operational reason.


That preserves the simple mental model and still leaves room to scale.


The naming I would lock is:


hosts/
  server/
  async/
  cli/
  web/        optional


And then inside async composition:


plugins/workflows/*
plugins/worker/*
plugins/schedules/*   only if you really need it


Or better, even there, keep schedules under workflows unless they are truly non-workflow runtime tasks.


So my blunt answer:


Do not separate “worker host” from “general async host” for semantics alone.
That is taxonomy theater.
Keep one async host.
Let worker be one lane/plugin type inside it.
Split later only for operational reasons.
