nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
Yes. Host bundle is the better term.


“App” is too vague.
“Host” alone gets confused with machine/process.
“Host bundle” captures the actual thing:


a composed deployable runtime bundle that can instantiate one or more process roles.


That is the right abstraction.


So the model becomes:


packages/   capability logic
services/   private callable boundaries
plugins/    host-role contributions
apps/roles/ reusable role bootstraps

host-bundles/
  shared/
  sales/
  todo/


And each host bundle can declare which roles it includes:


host-bundles/shared/
  rawr.hq.ts
  server/
  async/
  cli/

host-bundles/sales/
  rawr.hq.ts
  server/
  async/
  web/


Or, if you want to keep it under apps/, that is still fine structurally, but semantically you should think of them as host bundles, not generic apps.


The clean hierarchy is:


host bundle
  -> one assembled HQ manifest/composition root
  -> one or more role entrypoints
      -> each role runs as a process
          -> processes run on hosts/machines


That is the final model.


So yes:


shared is a host bundle

sales can become its own host bundle

a host bundle can run n=1 all together

or only some roles

or be deployed across multiple Railway services/machines


That is the correct term to lock.
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say: woah but hold on — services is where the capability logic lives directly
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
Yes. Then lock that.


If in your architecture services are the real capability center of gravity, then say that plainly and stop softening it.


That means the hierarchy is:


services = capability logic and private callable capability boundaries
plugins = runtime-specific adapters/projections of services
host bundles = composed deployable assemblies of plugins + services
roles = process roles a host bundle can instantiate


So the corrected model is:


services/
  sales/
  todo/
  billing/

plugins/
  server/
  async/
  web/
  agent/
  cli/

apps/roles/
  server/
  async/
  cli/
  web/
  agent/

host-bundles/
  shared/
  sales/
  todo/


And the semantics are:


services/sales/* is where Sales capability actually lives

plugins/server/sales-api/* is just the server-facing adapter for Sales

plugins/async/sales-workflows/* is just the async adapter for Sales

plugins/web/sales-panel/* is just the web adapter for Sales

host-bundles/shared/* composes some or all of those into one deployable bundle


So yes — if services already own the capability directly, then my earlier “packages/ as capability center” framing was too generic for your actual model.


For your model, it should be:


services = capability truth
plugins  = runtime truth
host bundles = deployment/composition truth
roles = process/runtime role truth


That is much sharper.


This also means your host-first plugin split is even more defensible, because plugins are not pretending to be capability roots. They are explicitly runtime adapters over service-owned capability logic.


So your mental model should now be:


A service is not “something that must run remotely.”
It is the canonical capability unit.


A plugin is how that service gets projected into a specific runtime surface.


A host bundle is how a selected set of plugins/services gets assembled into a deployable system.


A role is one process/runtime role inside that bundle.


That also clarifies the transport question:


If services/todo defines the capability, then:


the server plugin can call it in-process if mounted locally

the async plugin can call it in-process if mounted locally

either can call it over RPC only if that service boundary is mounted remotely somewhere else


So services being the capability center does not force remote service hosts. It just means the service boundary is the canonical shape.


A good concrete structure would be:


services/
  todo/
    contract.ts
    router.ts
    client.ts
    impl.ts
    schemas.ts

plugins/
  server/
    todo-api/
      manifest.ts
      router.ts

  async/
    todo-workflows/
      manifest.ts
      workflows.ts

    todo-internal/
      manifest.ts
      router.ts

  cli/
    todo-admin/
      manifest.ts
      commands.ts


Here:


services/todo/* owns the Todo capability

plugins/server/todo-api exposes Todo to public server boundaries

plugins/async/todo-workflows exposes Todo execution in async land

plugins/async/todo-internal mounts a private internal router if needed

none of those plugins own Todo’s business truth


That is clean.


So yes, the corrected statement is:


Services is where the capability logic lives directly.


And from that, the final architecture sentence should be:


Services define the capability; plugins adapt it to runtime roles; host bundles compose those adaptations into deployable systems.


That is the version to lock.
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
Durable Endpoints are a good fit for some of this, but not as the universal wrapper around every private oRPC internal client.


The core distinction is this:


If you wrap a private service call in a Durable Endpoint and the caller still waits for the final business result, that is still basically a request/response durable API, not your general async plane. Durable Endpoints keep the request/response mental model and are designed to make endpoints fault-tolerant with step retries and observability. On the success path they behave like normal endpoints; on retry paths they can redirect the caller to an Inngest Cloud URL to await the final result. They are currently in beta, support TypeScript and Go, and have limitations including no flow control features like concurrency/rate limiting, no SSE, and currently limited POST body support in the docs. Inngest+3Inngest+3Inngest+3


So your question splits into two cases.


If what you want is:


API -> private internal call -> durable, retryable, observable endpoint -> return final result


then yes, Durable Endpoints can be a very nice fit. That is especially true for “semi-sync” operations where the user still expects a direct result, but you want step checkpointing and recovery. Think complex submit handlers, enrichment pipelines, or multi-step writes that should resume from the point of failure instead of restarting from scratch. Inngest+1


But if what you want is your real async plane:


API -> trigger -> return ack/runId quickly
async runtime -> execute later / separately


then Durable Endpoints are not the cleanest primary primitive. In that model, standard Inngest functions/workflows are a better fit, because the architectural point is not “make this endpoint durable while preserving request/response,” it is “decouple caller latency from work latency.” Durable Endpoints are explicitly about durable APIs that still behave like endpoints. Inngest+1


So your line:

otherwise it’s not async right?

Not quite. “Async” is not determined by whether you used Durable Endpoints. It is determined by whether the caller waits for the work.


Use this sanity check:


caller_latency ≈ total_work_latency
=> not really async, just durable/synchronous

caller_latency ≈ trigger_latency
=> async


That is the real test.


So the architecture I would lock is:


For public or internal request/response flows that should still feel like APIs but need checkpointing, retries, and observability, Durable Endpoints are strong.


For background execution, scheduled jobs, fan-out workflows, and anything where the caller should get back quickly while work continues elsewhere, use normal Inngest workflow/function execution.


That gives you a clean split:


server / API plane
  - normal APIs
  - durable endpoints for “still-an-endpoint” flows

async execution plane
  - Inngest functions/workflows
  - schedules
  - consumers
  - long-running/background work


Now to the naming question.


No, I do not think async is the wrong split name.


It is still the right umbrella because it describes the runtime role, not just one transport pattern. Your async host can contain:


true background workflows,

scheduled execution,

consumers/bridges,

durable endpoint handlers that are operationally part of the async plane,

private internal routers that only trigger work.


“Async” remains a good top-level name because it contrasts with your caller-facing server plane and captures “non-request-lifecycle execution concerns.” It does not require that every single thing in it be fire-and-forget. Some of it can be trigger-and-wait, or durable request/response. The umbrella still fits.


What I would not do is rename the whole thing around Inngest-specific terminology too early. async is a stable semantic. workflow or durable would be narrower and would break the moment you add consumers, internal routers, or non-Inngest helpers.


So the practical recommendation is:


Use Durable Endpoints selectively, not as the universal wrapper for all private service routers.


A good policy would be:


If the caller wants a direct final response and the flow is multi-step/failure-prone, consider Durable Endpoints.

If the caller should get an ack and the work should continue independently, trigger a normal Inngest workflow/function.

Keep async as the host/runtime umbrella name.


My blunt take: wrapping every private oRPC service in Durable Endpoints would blur your architecture. You would start treating “everything nontrivial” like an endpoint, when some of it is actually orchestration and should be modeled as execution, not request/response.


The one uncertainty to keep on the ledger: Durable Endpoints are still beta and the current docs call out meaningful limitations, so I would not make them the sole foundation of your internal runtime model yet. Inngest+2Inngest+2
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
You are mixing three distinct things:


A boundary plane
A durable/background execution plane
A service call plane


Once those are separated, the whole thing gets simpler.


The clean model for your system is:


server/API plane = caller-facing synchronous boundaries
async plane = durable/background execution
service plane = private callable capability boundaries used by either one


That means the answer to “should we have a synchronous host too?” is:


You already do. That is the server/API host.


You do not need a separate top-level “synchronous host” beyond that unless you are imagining a separate internal service mesh of request/response services. That is usually overkill early, and it is not the right first split for what you are building.


So for your architecture:


public/request-response/synchronous work lives in the server/API plane

durable/background/scheduled work lives in the async plane

private service boundaries can be called from either plane, in-process or over RPC later


Now the missing piece is: what is “long-running or background work” actually?


It is just work where the caller should not sit around waiting for completion, or work that needs its own execution lifecycle.


Typical examples:


send 10,000 emails

import a CSV and normalize records

generate embeddings for a knowledge base

run a billing reconciliation job

fan out notifications

poll an external provider every 5 minutes

consume Stripe or GitHub events and process them robustly

schedule a nightly digest

run a long AI pipeline

retry failed external syncs


Those all have one thing in common: they should not live inline inside a request/response path.


The next confusion is “how does that work begin?”


There are only a few real start modes.


A request triggers it.


User/API request
  -> server route
  -> trigger background work
  -> return ack/runId


A schedule triggers it.


Cron/scheduler
  -> trigger background work


An event/consumer triggers it.


Webhook / queue / stream / external event
  -> consumer/bridge
  -> trigger background work


Or, more rarely, a daemon loop runs continuously.


Long-lived process starts
  -> loop / poll / watch / consume forever


That is it. Almost all background work starts one of those four ways.


So when you ask:

Is it just defined once and then called to run?

Yes, mostly.


The normal shape is:


Define the work unit

Define how it gets triggered

Register it with the async host

Something triggers it later


That is the actual mental model.


A concrete example.


Suppose you have a “sales-digest” workflow.


The pieces are:


services/sales/
  logic for building digest content

plugins/async/workflows/sales-digest/
  durable execution definition

plugins/server/api/sales/
  route that triggers digest generation

async host
  mounts workflow bundle and runs it


Flow:


POST /sales/digest
  -> validate request
  -> trigger sales-digest workflow
  -> return { accepted: true, runId }

async host later/now
  -> run sales-digest workflow
  -> call services/sales logic
  -> write DB state
  -> send emails


That is how background work “actually begins.”


Now the other category: long-running background plugins that are not just “trigger one workflow.”


These are more like consumers or daemons.


Examples:


a Stripe event consumer

a GitHub webhook bridge

a Redis/queue consumer

a polling loop for some upstream API

a file watcher

a websocket relay process


Those are not “define once and wait for someone to call a function” in the same way. They are processes that start when the async host starts.


Their lifecycle is:


async host boots
  -> register consumer
  -> consumer subscribes / polls / listens
  -> incoming event arrives
  -> consumer handles directly or triggers a workflow


That is a different shape from an on-demand workflow.


So your async host usually contains two broad categories:


Triggered work:


workflows

scheduled jobs

one-off tasks


Resident runtime adapters:


consumers

bridges

pollers

daemon-like processes


That is why “async host” is a better name than “workflow host.”
It can own both.


Now to your “would it make sense to have a synchronous host as well?” question.


If by “synchronous host” you mean:


“A separate internal host where private request/response service routers live, distinct from the API host”


then the answer is: maybe later, but probably not as a first-class host role now.


Because the normal progression is:


Stage 1:


server/API host handles request/response boundaries

async host handles durable/background execution

private service boundaries are in-process by default


Stage 2:


some private service boundaries move remote

server or async calls them over internal RPC


Stage 3:


if a bounded domain becomes large enough, it gets its own host bundle


So yes, synchronous internal request/response services can exist.
But they are usually not a separate “plane” you need to enshrine from day one.


The two top-level planes you actually need are:


boundary/API plane

execution/async plane


Private request/response service calls are an internal implementation detail across those, not a top-level architecture pillar yet.


That is the part you were overcomplicating.


Now, how would a “long-running or background work plugin” actually look?


There are three useful shapes.


First: workflow plugin


Best when the work is a discrete run with retries/status/steps.


trigger -> workflow run -> done


Use for:


imports

fanout jobs

report generation

AI pipelines

nightly jobs

reconciliation


Second: schedule plugin


Best when the work starts on a clock.


every day at 9am -> trigger workflow


Usually this should trigger a workflow, not contain all business logic inline.


Third: consumer/daemon plugin


Best when the work is driven by an external system or continuous loop.


host start -> subscribe/poll/listen -> event arrives -> handle or trigger workflow


Use for:


queue consumers

provider event bridges

polling loops

watchers


That gives you a nice async subtree:


plugins/
  async/
    workflows/
    schedules/
    consumers/
    internal/


And the execution semantics are clear.


Here is the practical sanity check for where something belongs.


Put it in the API/server plane if:


the caller needs the final answer now

latency matters directly to the caller

it is request validation, auth, or orchestration

the work is short and safe inline


Put it in the async plane if:


caller should get an ack, not final completion

retries matter

it might run for seconds/minutes/hours

it fans out

it depends on flaky external systems

it is scheduled

it is event-driven

it needs isolated concurrency/failure handling


Put it in a consumer/daemon lane if:


it starts when the host starts and keeps running

it listens, polls, or subscribes continuously


That is the whole split.


The biggest misconception to kill is this:


“Background work is some mysterious thing that just runs on its own.”


No. It always has a lifecycle.
It either:


gets triggered,

gets scheduled,

gets consumed from an external source,

or starts as a resident loop.


That’s it.


So the architecture I would lock for you is:


Host roles
  server
    public API routes
    workflow trigger routes
    immediate request/response work

  async
    workflows
    schedules
    consumers/bridges
    private internal routers if needed


And do not add a separate “sync host” right now.
Your server/API host already is that.
A distinct internal synchronous host only becomes real when you have enough remote internal request/response traffic to justify it.


The baseline sanity check:


If the caller waits for final business completion, it belongs in the request/response path.
If the caller only initiates work, it belongs in the async path.
If the process wakes up and listens on its own, it is a consumer/daemon.


That is the clean mental model you were missing.
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
private service boundaries can be called from either plane, in-process or over RPC later


but if they are called over rpc, where do they run? in the server plane? that seems weird no? or I guess not because it’s still an internal server… so with the top level service directory the job is basically already done and you call to this as an in-process or rpc link
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
Yes. That is the idea.


“Called from either plane, in-process or over RPC later” means the service boundary is transport-flexible. It does not mean “all service calls magically belong to the server plane.”


The clean mental model is:


A service boundary is a callable capability boundary.
A host/plane is a runtime location where some service boundaries may be mounted.


So the real question is not “is it RPC?”
The real question is “which host mounted that service boundary?”


That determines where it runs.


There are three cases.


First, in-process.


server host
  -> calls service client
  -> service implementation runs in same process


or


async host
  -> calls service client
  -> service implementation runs in same process


In this mode, there is no network hop. The client is just an in-memory/router client wrapper. This is the simplest and probably your default.


Second, RPC to server host.


async host
  -> RPC link
  -> private service router mounted on server host
  -> service runs on server host


This is valid, but you should only do it if that service truly belongs on the server host.


Third, RPC to async host.


server host
  -> RPC link
  -> private service router mounted on async host
  -> service runs on async host


Also valid, if that service is owned by the async host.


So yes: if something is called over RPC, it runs wherever the target router is mounted. RPC is just transport.


That is the whole answer.


Now to your “that seems weird no?” point.


It only seems weird if you think “server plane” means “public synchronous HTTP only.” But really the server host can have:


public boundary routers

internal trusted routers

in-process services


Likewise the async host can have:


Inngest handlers

consumers/schedulers

internal trusted routers

in-process services


So an “internal server” is not weird. It is just a host exposing trusted-only RPC surfaces on a private network or private port.


The more important rule is: do not mount private services remotely unless there is a reason.


Your line here is basically right:

with the top level service directory the job is basically already done and you call to this as an in-process or rpc link

Yes. Exactly.


That is the right architecture.


The services/* layer defines:


the service boundary

the canonical router/contract

the transport-neutral client interface


Then a given host decides one of two things:


mount it locally and use it in-process

mount it remotely and call it over RPC


That means the top-level service directory is where the semantic job is done. Host placement is a later deployment/runtime decision.


So the pattern should look like this:


services/
  todo/
    contract.ts
    router.ts
    client.ts
    impl.ts


Then:


apps/server/
  mounts some services locally

apps/async/
  mounts some services locally


And callers do:


const todoService = resolveTodoServiceClient({
  mode: "in-process" | "rpc",
  target: "server" | "async",
});


The caller should not care much beyond that.


This is why I kept saying “private service boundaries can be called from either plane.” It means:


the server host can call a service

the async host can call a service

the service may be local or remote

transport is an implementation detail behind the client


Now the important architectural guardrail:


Do not turn every service into a remote RPC hop just because you can.


Default should be:


in-process when colocated

RPC only when the service is actually mounted elsewhere


Otherwise you create fake distributed architecture.


A clean rule:


same host/process -> in-process client
different host/process -> RPC client


That is enough.


Now to the subtle part: should services “belong” to server or async?


Not inherently.


A service boundary belongs to a capability, not to a plane.
A plane/host simply mounts the service where it makes operational sense.


Examples:


todo query service might live on server because it serves request/response paths.

billing reconciliation service might live on async because it supports workflows and background jobs.

shared account service might be mounted in both hosts in-process if both need it locally.


That is why the service layer should stay top-level and transport-neutral.


So the final clean model is:


services/* = capability-owned private callable boundaries

server plane = public APIs + whatever services it mounts
async plane  = workflows/consumers + whatever services it mounts

callers use:
  in-process client when local
  RPC link when remote


That is not weird. That is exactly the point of separating service boundaries from host placement.


The sanity check:


If you ask “where does this run?”, answer:
“on whichever host mounted the router.”


If you ask “how do I call it?”, answer:
“through the same service client interface, using in-process or RPC transport depending on placement.”


That is the model to lock.
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
shouldn’t the internal server live on a different host than the api server? like for isolation and also processing? is there any reason that a synchronous server like that would he needed? is that a worker?


keep in mind we’re building on railway
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
Thought for 7s


Not by default.


A separate internal synchronous server can exist, but it should not be a first-class host just because “internal RPC sounds cleaner.” On Railway especially, the cheapest honest model is usually:


public API/server host

private async host

optional internal private routers mounted on one or both

split into more services only when the runtime behavior actually diverges


Railway already makes private service-to-service networking easy inside one project environment via internal DNS on the private network, without exposing services publicly, and Railway explicitly recommends using private networking for inter-service communication. Railway Docs+2Railway Docs+2


So yes, you can put an internal synchronous service on a different Railway service/host for isolation. But the better question is whether you should. Usually, early on, no.


The reason is simple: “internal synchronous server” is not a runtime role on the same level as server vs async. It is just a private request/response boundary. That boundary can be mounted:


in-process on the API host

in-process on the async host

remotely on a separate internal service, if that later becomes necessary


That means the real first split is still:


server plane   = caller-facing request/response
async plane    = durable/background execution


An “internal sync service” is not a third core plane. It is a placement option for private service routers.


So to your direct question:


Shouldn’t the internal server live on a different host than the API server for isolation and processing?


Only when one of these becomes true:


the private service has materially different scaling needs

it is CPU or memory heavy enough to hurt the public API host

it needs stronger failure isolation

it has grown into a real bounded subsystem with its own lifecycle

multiple other hosts need to call it remotely as a shared service


If none of that is true, separating it is fake complexity.


Because a separate internal host buys you:


isolation

independent scaling

independent deploy cadence


But it also costs you:


another Railway service

more env/config wiring

network hops

internal auth/trust rules

more observability surface

more failure modes


That trade is only worth it when the runtime pressure is real.


Now the important semantic part:


Is that a worker?


No.


A synchronous internal service is not a worker.


A worker is an execution role for background or non-request-lifecycle work. A synchronous internal service is still request/response. It is just private.


So these are different:


server host
  -> calls internal sync service over RPC
  -> waits for result


That is still synchronous request/response.


Versus:


server host
  -> triggers async workflow / background job
  -> returns ack


That is async/worker territory.


So do not collapse “private” with “worker.” Private just means “not publicly exposed.”


A good mental split is:


public sync     = API routes
private sync    = internal service routers
private async   = workflows, schedules, consumers, workers


But that does not mean you need three host types.


Those concerns can still live on just two top-level hosts:


server host
  - public sync
  - optional private sync

async host
  - private async
  - optional private sync


That is the cleaner model.


Now, since you’re on Railway, this becomes even more practical.


Railway gives you internal private DNS per service within the same project/environment, so if you later decide the async host should call a private service on another host, or the API host should call a private internal service, you can do that over the private network without exposing it publicly. Services in the same project environment can use internal *.railway.internal DNS, and private traffic stays inside Railway’s infrastructure over encrypted networking. Railway Docs+2Railway Docs+2


That means you do not need to architect around a future lack of internal networking. Railway already supports the future split cleanly.


So the pragmatic recommendation is:


Start with two Railway services if you want honesty without overkill:


api
async


Inside those:


mount public API routes on api

mount Inngest/workflows/consumers/schedules on async

keep private service routers in-process by default

move a private service router to its own internal host only when it becomes a real shared or resource-heavy subsystem


If you later need it, then add something like:


api
async
internal-billing


and have api or async call internal-billing.railway.internal.


That is a real evolution path.


The baseline sanity check is:


If caller waits for final result:
  sync path

If caller triggers and returns:
  async path

If service is private but still request/response:
  private sync service, not worker


So the blunt answer is:


No, the internal synchronous server does not inherently need its own host from day one.
No, it is not a worker.
Yes, it can live on a different Railway service later when isolation/scaling actually justify it.
For now, the clean model is still two primary host roles: API/server and async.
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say: but hold on, if the service doesn’t run on a separate host, then why would it be called over RPC from the same host via the public api server? wouldn’t it just stay in process then? or at least functionally?
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
Yes. Exactly.


If the service is not on a separate host or process, it should usually just stay in-process.


Calling it over RPC on the same host is usually pointless unless you are very deliberately forcing all calls through the same transport abstraction for consistency or testing. But functionally, yes, it is still “local,” and most of the time you should not pay the RPC hop.


The clean rule is:


same process -> direct/in-process client
different process/host -> RPC client


That is the default you want.


So your intuition is right: if the API server and the service live in the same runtime, then the API route should just call the service in-process through the private client/router abstraction. No network. No self-call. No fake service hop.


That means the service layer still matters, but not because it must always be remote. It matters because it gives you:


a real private capability boundary

transport-neutral contracts

one place to define trusted internal semantics

the option to move remote later without rewriting call sites


So the value is not “always use RPC.”
The value is “define the service boundary once, choose transport later.”


That is the model.


A good implementation shape is:


type TodoServiceClient = {
  createTask(input: CreateTaskInput): Promise<CreateTaskOutput>;
};

function resolveTodoServiceClient(ctx: HostContext): TodoServiceClient {
  if (ctx.todoServicePlacement === "local") {
    return createInProcessTodoServiceClient(ctx);
  }

  return createRemoteTodoServiceClient({
    baseUrl: ctx.env.TODO_SERVICE_INTERNAL_URL,
  });
}


Then your API code does not care:


const todo = resolveTodoServiceClient(ctx);
await todo.createTask(input);


If local, it runs in-process.
If remote later, it runs over RPC.
Same semantic boundary.


This is why I kept separating:


service boundary

host placement

transport


They are different decisions.


So to answer your question directly:


If the service does not run separately, then yes, it should remain in-process.
Calling it over RPC from the same host would usually be unnecessary and worse.


The only reasons to still do same-host RPC are niche:


you want absolute transport parity in all environments

the service is actually another process on the same machine

you are intentionally testing/deploying it as if remote

you need process isolation, even without separate machines


But those are exceptions, not the default.


So the clean architecture becomes:


services/*
  define private callable boundaries

server host
  calls local services in-process
  calls remote services over RPC only when truly remote

async host
  same rule


That is the simplest correct model.


And it also answers one of your earlier confusions: a “private service” does not imply “separate internal server.” It just means “not public boundary.” It can be local or remote.


That is the lock:


private != remote
remote != async
async != worker


Those were the concepts getting tangled.


The real order is:


service boundary first
placement second
transport third


That is the durable mental model.
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
I guess I have a hard time imagining that a dedicated services host api (which offers all internal services) + a public API host (basically a tiny request/response proxy?) + async host isn’t common?


or maybe I’m misunderstanding the tradeoff math you’re making
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
It is common in some organizations. It is not a good default for yours.


You are imagining a shape that becomes common once a company has already committed to an internal service mesh or microservice-heavy topology:


public API host
  -> internal services host
  -> async host


That does exist.


But the public API host in that model is usually not just a “tiny request/response proxy.” If it is only a proxy, that is often a smell. Then you are paying for an extra hop, extra deploy unit, extra failure domain, and extra observability complexity without buying much.


That is the tradeoff math you are underrating.


The question is not “is this architecture seen in the wild?”
It is “what does this extra host buy us relative to what it costs?”


For your context, a dedicated internal services host only makes sense if it is doing real work as a distinct bounded runtime, such as:


owning a genuinely shared internal domain used by multiple hosts

scaling independently for CPU/memory reasons

isolating risky/failure-prone synchronous work from the public API host

becoming its own ecosystem with its own deploy cadence


If it is just:


public API route
  -> internal RPC call
  -> service host
  -> do business logic
  -> return


for almost everything, then your public API host has become a glorified edge adapter. That is usually not a win early.


Why not?


Because you add cost in six places immediately.


First, latency. Every synchronous request now has an extra network hop.


Second, failure modes. A healthy public API host can now fail user requests because the internal services host is degraded.


Third, ops surface. More Railway services, more env vars, more private DNS dependencies, more dashboards, more deploy coordination.


Fourth, debugging. Every “why is this request slow?” investigation now crosses service boundaries.


Fifth, local/dev ergonomics. You either run more processes or fake the topology.


Sixth, architecture pressure. Once you have the internal services host, teams start shoving everything through it because it exists, even when in-process would be better.


That is the math.


What do you gain?


isolation

independent scaling

clearer network boundaries

potential reuse across multiple callers


Those are real gains. They just are not free.


So the right question is:


Do you already have enough synchronous internal traffic and enough divergent runtime pressure to justify a dedicated internal services host?


For what you have described, I do not think the answer is yes yet.


The cleaner near-term model is:


public API host
  - public routes
  - local in-process service calls by default

async host
  - workflows
  - consumers
  - schedules
  - local in-process service calls by default

optional remote internal services later
  - only for domains that actually need isolation/shared ownership


That is not “less real.”
It is just the correct leverage order.


You are probably picturing the dedicated services host as the “proper architecture” because it feels neat:


one public layer

one internal sync layer

one async layer


But neatness is not the same as leverage.


A top-level internal sync layer becomes worth it when there is genuine many-to-many reuse across hosts or when one bounded domain deserves its own runtime. Until then, it is often just an extra indirection layer.


Here is the sanity check.


A dedicated internal services host makes sense when this is true:


multiple hosts
  -> call same internal domain remotely
the domain
  -> needs independent scaling/deploy/failure isolation


If instead the reality is:


public API host
  -> mostly calls service logic that could have run locally
async host
  -> mostly calls service logic that could have run locally


then the internal services host is mostly ceremony.


The difference between a good remote internal service and a fake one is this:


Good remote internal service:


billing ledger

search indexing cluster

document processing engine

recommendation service

auth/session authority

account/tenant core used by many hosts


Fake remote internal service:


“TodoService” moved out just because everything should be behind RPC

thin wrappers around local business logic with no independent scaling or ownership reason


That second category is what I am steering you away from.


On Railway specifically, this matters even more because Railway makes it easy to split later. Private networking is there when you need it. So you do not need to pre-pay the complexity tax just to preserve optionality.


That means the right progression is:


Stage 1:


api
async


Stage 2:


api
async
internal-domain-X   // only when truly justified


Stage 3:


multiple host bundles / ecosystems


That is the practical path.


The deeper mental model:


A public API host should not be a “tiny request/response proxy” unless its real job is edge concerns:


auth

rate limiting

public contract shaping

aggregation across multiple downstreams

tenancy enforcement

response composition


If it is doing those meaningfully, fine.
If it is just forwarding almost every call 1:1 to an internal service host, that is weak architecture unless you are already operating at a scale/organization shape that demands it.


So the tradeoff math is:


A dedicated internal services host is common in systems that are already paying for service distribution.
It is not the minimal honest architecture for your current shape unless one or more domains have already earned that separation.


The clean rule I would use:


Keep services transport-neutral and top-level.
Run them in-process by default on the host that needs them.
Promote a service/domain to its own internal host only when there is a concrete operational reason.


That is why I am not recommending the three-host model as the baseline.


Not because it is wrong in principle.
Because it is usually premature for your current leverage point.
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say: okay so the better model is just to add a dedicated api host when needed and split the central api tree to serve non overlapping
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
Yes. That is the better model.


Not “one permanent central internal services host.”
Instead:


Start with a small number of host roles:


api

async

optional web, agent, cli


Keep service boundaries top-level and transport-neutral.
Run them in-process on whichever host needs them.
Then, when a bounded slice actually earns separation, give it its own host.


So the evolution path is:


Stage 1
  api host
  async host

Stage 2
  api host
  async host
  dedicated host for domain X

Stage 3
  multiple host bundles / ecosystems as needed


And yes, when you add that dedicated host, the right move is usually to split the central API tree so responsibilities are non-overlapping.


That means one of two things happens.


Either the dedicated host owns its own public/internal boundaries outright:


api host
  - public routes for general app concerns

billing host
  - billing APIs
  - billing internal services
  - billing workflows


Or the main API host stays as the public edge and delegates only where that adds real value:


public api host
  - auth
  - tenant resolution
  - response shaping
  - aggregation

billing host
  - real billing execution and domain boundaries


The key is: do not keep the central API tree as the universal owner forever if domains are graduating. That creates overlap and confusion fast.


So the rule should be:


When a domain gets its own host, it should also get its own non-overlapping boundary ownership as much as possible.


Otherwise you end up with:


central API routes wrapping domain-host routes

duplicated contracts

unclear ownership

extra hops for no reason


The clean mental model is:


services/* define capability boundaries
hosts own runtime placement
APIs are partitioned by domain ownership when a domain graduates


So yes:
add dedicated API/domain hosts when needed,
and split the central API tree accordingly so each host owns a clear surface.


That is much better than inventing a permanent all-services internal host up front.
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say: what is a cli host? isnt my machine that?
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
Yes. Usually your machine is the CLI host.


“CLI host” is just the runtime role for command-based execution. It is not necessarily a server and not necessarily a deployed cloud host.


So in the same way that:


api host means “the process/environment running the API entrypoint”

async host means “the process/environment running async execution”


cli host means:


“the process/environment running the CLI entrypoint”


On your laptop, that is just your machine running bun run rawr ... or whatever your CLI command is.


In CI/CD (Continuous Integration/Continuous Delivery), the CLI host could be:


a GitHub Actions runner

a Railway job

a container task

any ephemeral machine/process running the CLI entrypoint


So “host” here does not mean “server on the internet.”
It means “the environment/process role that mounts and runs that runtime surface.”


That is why the term still works.


Examples:


Local dev
  your MacBook
    -> CLI host for rawr commands
    -> maybe also web host if running frontend dev server
    -> maybe also api host if running local API


Railway prod
  service A -> API host
  service B -> async host


GitHub Action
  runner -> CLI host


So yes, your machine is the CLI host when you are running CLI plugins/commands.


The reason it is still useful to name it is semantic consistency. It tells you:


where CLI plugins mount

what runtime context they expect

how they differ from API/async/web/agent runtime surfaces


The main thing not to do is over-literalize “host” into “always a separate deployed machine.” In your model, host really means:


runtime entrypoint + execution environment


That can be:


one process on your laptop

one Railway service

one CI runner

one container

one VM


So the clean answer is:


Yes, your machine is the CLI host when running the CLI.
No, it does not imply some extra server or cloud box.
It is just the CLI runtime role.
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say: so server is a process role that the host can play
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
Yes.


That is the right way to think about it.


A host is the environment/deployable runtime container.
A server is one process role that a host can run.


So more generally:


host = where runtime happens

process role = what kind of runtime process is being run there


Examples of process roles:


server

async

cli

web

agent


Examples of hosts:


your laptop

one Railway service

one container

one VM

one CI runner


That means one host can play one role:


Railway service A
  -> server


Or one host can play multiple roles:


your laptop
  -> server
  -> async
  -> web
  -> cli


Or a logical host bundle can be deployed across multiple concrete hosts:


RAWR bundle
  -> Railway service A = server
  -> Railway service B = async


So yes: server is a process/runtime role, not the deepest architectural unit.


That is why the cleaner hierarchy is:


capabilities/services
  -> projected through plugins
      -> composed into process roles
          -> run on hosts


That is the model you were trying to get to.
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model
Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
Yes. That is the cleaner long-term shape.


Do both.


Use one place for shared host role runtimes, and another for domain/ecosystem-specific assembled apps.


So the model is:


apps/
  host/
    server/
    async/
    cli/
    web/
    agent/

  sales/
    server/
    async/
    web/

  todo/
    server/
    async/

  platform/
    server/
    async/


That gives you two different layers that solve two different problems.


apps/host/* is where you define the generic runtime role shells.
These are not business apps. They are the reusable entrypoint patterns for:


mounting server contributions

mounting async contributions

running CLI commands

running web shells

running agent runtimes


Think of these as host-role templates or canonical role runtimes.


Then apps/<domain>/* is where you define an actual assembled deployable app/host bundle for a bounded domain or ecosystem.


That is the thing you actually deploy.


So the distinction is:


apps/host/*      = reusable runtime role bases
apps/<domain>/*  = concrete composed host apps


That is the right separation.


Why this is better than only apps/host/*


If you only have:


apps/host/server
apps/host/async


then you are missing the place where an actual bounded system says:


which plugins it includes

which services it owns

which manifests it composes

which runtime roles it actually ships


You need that assembly layer.


Why this is better than only apps/<domain>/*


If you only have:


apps/sales/server
apps/todo/server
apps/platform/server


then you will duplicate runtime boot logic, host wiring conventions, env loading, telemetry setup, route mounting conventions, and so on.


You want the generic host-role logic reusable.


So yes, the dual structure is the right move.


The clean mental model is:


packages/   pure shared logic
services/   private callable capability boundaries
plugins/    host-role contributions/adapters
apps/host/  reusable runtime role shells
apps/*/     concrete assembled ecosystems/hosts


I would tighten the naming slightly though.


I would probably not use apps/host/... singular if this is really “shared host role implementations.” Better options:


apps/_base/
  server/
  async/
  cli/
  web/
  agent/


or


apps/runtime/
  server/
  async/
  cli/
  web/
  agent/


or


apps/roles/
  server/
  async/
  cli/
  web/
  agent/


Because apps/host/server reads a little awkwardly. What you really mean is “runtime role base app,” not “the host host.”


If you want maximum semantic clarity, I would do:


apps/
  roles/
    server/
    async/
    cli/
    web/
    agent/

  platform/
    server/
    async/

  sales/
    server/
    async/
    web/
    agent/


That is probably the cleanest version.


Then each concrete app like apps/sales/server is very thin. It should mostly say:


load sales manifest / HQ assembly

use shared roles/server bootstrap

mount the server contributions for this domain bundle

apply domain-specific config only where needed


That prevents duplication.


So in practice:


apps/
  roles/
    server/
      bootstrap.ts
    async/
      bootstrap.ts
    cli/
      bootstrap.ts

  sales/
    rawr.hq.ts
    server/
      main.ts
    async/
      main.ts
    web/
      main.ts


Where apps/sales/server/main.ts is basically:


import { startServerRole } from "@/apps/roles/server/bootstrap";
import { createSalesHq } from "../rawr.hq";

const hq = createSalesHq();
await startServerRole({ hq });


That is the right level of reuse.


One more important point:


Do not force every domain to have every role.


Your shape should allow:


apps/todo/server
apps/todo/cli


with no async if Todo does not need it.


Or:


apps/reports/async


with no public server at all.


So the rule is not “every domain gets server/async/cli.”
The rule is “every domain can instantiate whichever roles it actually needs.”


That means this is better than your wording:


apps/{domain}/{at least one of: server, etc.}


Yes — exactly that.


Now the sanity check.


When a domain graduates into its own ecosystem, do you want:


to create a new concrete app assembly with one or more role entrypoints,

reusing shared role bootstraps?


Yes.
That is exactly what this structure gives you.


It also avoids the bad alternative of deploying individual plugins as apps. Instead, you deploy composed domain bundles.


So my recommendation is:


Use a two-level app structure:


shared runtime role bases

concrete domain/ecosystem assemblies


I would implement it as:


apps/
  roles/
    server/
    async/
    cli/
    web/
    agent/

  <domain>/
    rawr.hq.ts
    server/
    async/
    cli/
    web/
    agent/


That is the cleanest version of the model you have been converging on.
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
but wait—we do want to enable n=1 shared host plugin model, which can expand to a full host duplication OR just any part od the tree as i showed you


but does your model support n=1?
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
Yes. It supports n = 1 cleanly.


The trick is this:


You need to separate host role definitions from concrete assemblies and from deployment placement.


If you do that, the same model supports:


n = 1 shared host

partial tree split

full host duplication into separate ecosystems


So the answer is not “my model replaces your shared host model.”
It is: the shared host model should be the base assembly mode inside the same architecture.


The clean version is:


apps/
  roles/
    server/
    async/
    cli/
    web/
    agent/

  shared/
    rawr.hq.ts
    server/
    async/
    cli/
    web/
    agent/

  sales/
    rawr.hq.ts
    server/
    async/

  todo/
    rawr.hq.ts
    server/


Where:


apps/shared/* is your n=1 system-wide shared host assembly

apps/sales/*, apps/todo/*, etc. are promoted/split assemblies

apps/roles/* are reusable role bootstraps


That is the missing piece.


So yes, you absolutely want both:


one shared host assembly for the whole tree

the ability to carve out any subtree into its own assembly later


That means your “shared host plugin model” is not in conflict with the domain assembly model. It is just one concrete assembly among several possible ones.


The real abstraction is:


plugins + services + packages
  -> assembled into one or more HQ manifests
      -> each HQ manifest instantiates one or more roles


That supports n=1 naturally.


In n=1, you just have one assembly root:


apps/shared/rawr.hq.ts


and that manifest composes everything.


Later, if sales needs to split, you add:


apps/sales/rawr.hq.ts


which composes only the sales-relevant services/plugins.


That is the right promotion path.


So the hierarchy becomes:


shared capability tree
  -> can be assembled into:
     - one global shared host bundle
     - one domain host bundle
     - several domain host bundles
     - partial subtree host bundles


That is exactly the flexibility you want.


The thing to avoid is making the filesystem imply only one assembly mode.


So I would not make it just:


apps/<domain>/<role>


because that underrepresents the n=1 shared assembly.


And I would not make it just:


apps/shared/<role>


because that underrepresents future split assemblies.


You want both.


The clean structure is:


apps/
  roles/
    server/
    async/
    cli/
    web/
    agent/

  shared/
    rawr.hq.ts
    server/
    async/
    cli/
    web/
    agent/

  sales/
    rawr.hq.ts
    server/
    async/

  todo/
    rawr.hq.ts
    server/


Now the deeper part: does this support “just any part of the tree” being split out?


Yes, if the composition model is selector-based.


Meaning rawr.hq.ts should not be a giant handwritten monolith. It should be able to compose from declared plugin/service manifests by capability selectors or package lists.


For example:


createHq({
  includeCapabilities: ["sales", "billing"],
  includeRoles: ["server", "async"],
})


or more explicitly:


createHq({
  plugins: [
    salesServerApiPlugin,
    salesAsyncWorkflowPlugin,
    billingAsyncWorkflowPlugin,
  ],
  services: [
    salesService,
    billingService,
  ],
})


Then:


apps/shared/rawr.hq.ts includes everything

apps/sales/rawr.hq.ts includes just sales subtree contributions

apps/todo/rawr.hq.ts includes just todo contributions


That is how you get:


n=1

partial split

full split


without changing the core architecture.


So the answer to your question is:


Yes, the model supports n=1, but only if you explicitly represent shared assembly as a first-class app, not just domain apps.


The simplest statement of the final model is:


apps/roles/*      reusable role runtimes
apps/shared/*     n=1 whole-system assembly
apps/<domain>/*   split assemblies for promoted subtrees


That is the one to lock.


The sanity check is:


Can the same capability live:


in the shared assembly today

in its own domain assembly tomorrow

without changing its core service/plugin definitions?


If yes, the model is correct.


That is the goal.
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
why don’t we call shared “hq”


and roles would just go into an sdk package right?
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
Yes. hq is better than shared.


shared describes implementation reuse.
hq describes semantic authority.


What that assembly really is is not “the shared app.” It is the main composition authority / primary host bundle for the system. So hq is the stronger name.


I would prefer:


apps/
  hq/
    rawr.hq.ts
    server/
    async/
    cli/
    web/
    agent/

  sales/
    rawr.hq.ts
    server/
    async/


That makes the promotion path much clearer:


apps/hq/* = whole-system assembly

apps/sales/* = carved-out/promoted sub-assembly

both are peers as assembled host bundles


That is cleaner than shared, because once sales becomes its own thing, shared starts sounding like “misc leftovers,” while hq still correctly means “the primary composition root.”


And yes, the role bootstraps should generally move into an SDK/runtime package, not live under apps/roles.


That is the better model.


Because server, async, cli, web, agent role implementations are not really apps. They are reusable runtime bootstraps/tooling. That makes them package material.


So instead of:


apps/
  roles/
    server/
    async/


prefer something like:


packages/
  host-sdk/
    server/
    async/
    cli/
    web/
    agent/


or maybe even:


packages/
  hq-sdk/
    roles/
      server/
      async/
      cli/
      web/
      agent/


or, if this is the true runtime/kernel package:


packages/
  host/
    server/
    async/
    cli/
    web/
    agent/


I would probably choose one of these two:


packages/hq-sdk/
packages/host-sdk/


The naming depends on what else that package will hold.


If it is just role bootstraps and host composition helpers, host-sdk is clean.
If it is the primary system assembly toolkit, hq-sdk is stronger.


The semantic split then becomes:


packages/
  hq-sdk/         reusable host/runtime composition machinery
  services/...
  plugins/...
  packages/...

apps/
  hq/             main assembled system
  sales/          promoted sub-assembly
  todo/           promoted sub-assembly


That is a very good final shape.


More concretely:


packages/
  hq-sdk/
    create-hq.ts
    roles/
      server/
        bootstrap.ts
      async/
        bootstrap.ts
      cli/
        bootstrap.ts
      web/
        bootstrap.ts
      agent/
        bootstrap.ts

apps/
  hq/
    rawr.hq.ts
    server/
      main.ts
    async/
      main.ts

  sales/
    rawr.hq.ts
    server/
      main.ts


Then apps/hq/server/main.ts is thin glue:


import { startServerRole } from "@rawr/hq-sdk/roles/server";
import { createHq } from "../rawr.hq";

const hq = createHq();
await startServerRole({ hq });


That is the right layering.


So the short answer is:


Yes, call it hq.
Yes, move role bootstraps into an SDK/runtime package.


That is cleaner semantically and structurally than shared plus apps/roles.
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say: Okay, very cool. So, how are we looking overall? You know, in terms of if you were an agent tasked with solving a very difficult problem in a codebase that was fully equipped with this, with everything that you see and everything that we've planned, how would you feel?
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
Overall: very strong, if you keep the discipline and do not let the abstractions blur.


If I were an agent dropped into a difficult codebase built this way, I would feel unusually good about my odds. Not because the system is “fancy,” but because it is converging on the right kinds of separations.


What makes hard codebases hard for agents is not usually raw size. It is ambiguity. Specifically:


Where does truth live?
What runs where?
What is public vs private?
What is capability logic vs transport glue?
What is durable execution vs request/response?
What can be changed locally without breaking unrelated things?


Your planned model is increasingly good at answering those questions up front.


The strongest parts are these.


First, the center of gravity is moving toward stable capability boundaries instead of framework-shaped chaos. That matters a lot. If services/* and packages/* truly own the capability logic and plugins/* only project that logic into host/runtime surfaces, then an agent can reason locally. That is huge.


Second, the host model is becoming honest. You are no longer pretending every runtime concern is “just the app.” You have a real split between:


boundary/request-response

durable/background execution

CLI/operator surfaces

optional web/agent runtimes


That lets an agent ask the right question first: “which runtime role owns this failure?” Most bad systems do not allow that.


Third, the promotion path is excellent. The fact that you are explicitly designing for:


n = 1 HQ shared host

partial subtree extraction

full host/ecosystem promotion


means the architecture has a real growth path instead of a fake one. That is one of the best signs. It means the system is being designed around preserving meaning while allowing deployment evolution.


Fourth, the transport-flexible service boundary idea is very powerful. If the same service contract can be:


called in-process when colocated

called over oRPC when remote


then agents do not have to rewrite business semantics when topology changes. That reduces the amount of architectural damage every future split would otherwise cause.


So from an agent’s perspective, this is good because it gives me leverage on the real dimensions of the problem:


locality of reasoning

explicit runtime ownership

predictable promotion path

low ambiguity around boundaries


That said, this will only stay strong if you avoid three failure modes.


The first is taxonomy drift.


You are close to a good architecture, but you are also close to over-naming everything. If too many categories appear without hard composition consequences, the system will become semantically pretty and operationally muddy.


The rule should be brutal:
if a distinction does not change composition, ownership, execution, or validation, it probably does not deserve to be a top-level semantic.


The second is letting plugin folders become capability homes.


Right now the model works because capability truth is elsewhere and plugins are adapters. If, six months from now, people start putting real business logic in:


plugins/server/...

plugins/async/...


then the architecture will rot fast. At that point, host-first plugin organization becomes fragmentation instead of projection.


The third is turning “service boundary” into “everything must be remote.”


This is the most common self-inflicted wound in systems like the one you are describing. The correct power move is:
define service boundaries once,
run them in-process by default,
go remote only when justified.


If you hold that line, the system stays fast and comprehensible. If you do not, you end up with microservice theater.


So how would I feel as an agent?


I would feel like this codebase has a chance to be genuinely high-leverage.


Not because it is minimal. It is not minimal.
Not because it is simple. It is not simple.
But because it is trying to make the important things explicit:


capability truth

runtime role

host assembly

public/private boundary

promotion path


That is exactly what hard problem-solving agents need.


The best version of this environment is not “an AI-friendly codebase” in some marketing sense. It is a codebase where the agent can answer these five questions cheaply:


1. What capability owns this behavior?
2. Which runtime role executes it?
3. Which boundary exposes or triggers it?
4. Is this local or remote in this assembly?
5. If this grows, what is the promotion path?


Your architecture is getting very close to making all five answerable without archaeology.


That is rare.


My honest read, though: the architecture is stronger than the implementation discipline you have locked so far. Meaning the model is good enough that the biggest risk is no longer “bad top-level design.” The biggest risk is authoring drift:


inconsistent naming

duplicate patterns

exceptions that feel convenient

runtime logic sneaking into the wrong layer

too many half-types of plugins


So if I were tasked with solving a very difficult problem inside this system, I would feel optimistic, but I would also want hard guardrails:


generation templates

manifest schemas

allowed contribution shapes

lintable ownership rules

host-role composition tests

“this capability may only contribute these surfaces” constraints


Because once the semantics are this strong, the next leverage point is enforcement.


My overall verdict:


You are not building random architecture. You are converging on a serious operating model for a multi-surface, multi-runtime system. The pieces are coherent. The promotion path is coherent. The biggest risk is not that the model is wrong. The biggest risk is that you under-enforce it and end up with a system that describes clarity without actually preserving it.


If you keep the boundaries hard, this would be one of the better environments an agent could ask for. If you let the edges blur, it will become one more clever monorepo with nice nouns and weak locality.


Right now, I think you are on the good path.

nodes.say: look into the files! we plan to use NX.dev to solve half these problems
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
Thought for 11s


Thought for 23s


Yes — that changes the picture a lot.


I could not inspect your repo files directly through the GitHub connector because no repo is currently selected in this chat, so I could not verify the exact Nx config you already wrote. But your underlying point is right: Nx can solve a large chunk of the “authoring drift” risk I was warning about.


The important shift is this:


Without Nx, your architecture is mostly a social contract.
With Nx done properly, it becomes a partially executable contract.


That is a big difference.


Nx gives you three major levers that map directly onto the problems we were discussing.


First, the project graph. Nx models the workspace as projects and dependencies, and uses that graph as a first-class primitive. Projects are usually detected from package.json or project.json, and Nx can show you the graph and per-project details. Nx+1


That matters because your whole design depends on being able to say things like:


server plugins may depend on services and packages, but not async plugins

services may depend on packages, but not apps

apps assemble plugins, but should not contain deep business logic

some promoted domain assembly should only depend on its allowed subtree


That is exactly the kind of thing a graph-aware system can reason about.


Second, module boundary enforcement. Nx’s @nx/enforce-module-boundaries rule lets you tag projects and define dependency constraints so projects can only depend on the kinds of projects they are allowed to depend on. Nx also supports multiple tag dimensions, which is exactly what your architecture wants. Nx+1


This is where your architecture stops being just nice words. You can encode tags like:


type:app
type:plugin
type:service
type:package

runtime:server
runtime:async
runtime:web
runtime:cli
runtime:agent

scope:hq
scope:sales
scope:todo
scope:platform


And then define actual constraints such as:


type:plugin -> may depend on type:service and type:package
type:service -> may depend on type:package
type:app -> may depend on type:plugin, type:service, type:package
runtime:server -> may not depend on runtime:async plugin projects directly
runtime:async -> may depend on async workflows/internal consumers
scope:sales -> may depend on scope:shared and scope:sales


That is exactly the kind of discipline your model needs.


Third, Conformance. Nx’s Conformance plugin can enforce rules against the entire project graph, not just TypeScript imports. It supports custom rules in TypeScript and can enforce architectural standards, ownership, and configuration policies workspace-wide. But it requires an Nx Enterprise license. Nx+1


That matters because some of the rules you want are not just import rules. They are structural rules, like:


every plugin project must declare a manifest

every async workflow project must register through the approved shape

apps may not implement business logic directly

every project must declare an owner

every domain promotion app must only compose approved scopes

no project in plugins/server/* may import from plugins/async/* directly unless via approved contract


Those are exactly the sort of rules Conformance is good for.


So your instinct is right: Nx is not a side detail here. It is a core enforcement layer for the architecture.


The stronger framing is:


RAWR gives you the semantic model.
Nx gives you the enforcement and graph machinery.


That is a good pairing.


The real question becomes: how much should you lean on Nx, and where should you stop?


Here is the right split.


Use Nx for:


project graph visibility

dependency boundaries

project tagging

generation/scaffolding

affected runs

target conventions

cacheable task orchestration

conformance checks if you have Enterprise


Do not expect Nx alone to solve:


runtime semantics

transport flexibility

host-role composition logic

manifest assembly semantics

“what is a workflow plugin vs internal router” unless you encode those rules explicitly


In other words, Nx is a force multiplier, not the architecture itself.


The best version of your system would use Nx in four concrete ways.


First, make every meaningful unit a real Nx project.


That means:


each service boundary project

each plugin project

each app assembly

each host SDK/runtime package

maybe even each promoted domain bundle root


The goal is that Nx can actually see and enforce the structure.


Second, tag aggressively and in multiple dimensions.


I would use at least these dimensions:


kind:app | plugin | service | package | sdk
runtime:server | async | web | cli | agent | neutral
scope:hq | sales | todo | platform | shared
visibility:public | private | internal
execution:sync | workflow | consumer | schedule | command | ui


You do not need all of them on day one, but that is the shape.


Third, use generators heavily.


This is where Nx really helps your architecture survive contact with reality.


You should not want humans manually creating:


service projects

plugin projects

app assembly entrypoints

manifest files

tags

boilerplate role bootstraps


You want commands that generate a correct unit every time.


Examples:


nx g @rawr/workspace:service sales-account --scope=sales
nx g @rawr/workspace:plugin sales-api --runtime=server --scope=sales
nx g @rawr/workspace:plugin sales-workflows --runtime=async --execution=workflow --scope=sales
nx g @rawr/workspace:app sales --roles=server,async,web


That is how you stop drift.


Fourth, add graph-aware conformance and ownership rules where it matters most.


Even if you do not use Nx Enterprise right away, start with ESLint boundary rules and strict tagging. If you do have Enterprise, Conformance is exactly the next layer you want because it can inspect the full project graph and enforce broader organizational rules. Nx+1


So how are you looking overall, once Nx is in the picture?


Much stronger.


The architecture stops being “a good mental model” and starts becoming “a build-governed workspace model.”


That is a major upgrade.


If I were an agent inside a codebase like that, my confidence would go up a lot for a few reasons.


I could inspect the graph and see actual project boundaries instead of inferring them from folders. Nx explicitly treats the project graph as central to the workspace mental model. Nx+1


I could trust that illegal imports and dependency drift would get caught automatically rather than relying on code review memory. Nx’s boundary tooling is designed for exactly that. Nx+1


I could rely on generators to produce the right skeletons, which means the workspace would remain structurally consistent over time instead of each new project becoming a custom snowflake.


And if you add Conformance, I could rely on repo-wide architecture rules beyond simple import restrictions. Nx+1


That said, there is one important correction to make.


Nx does not magically solve “half these problems” unless you are willing to encode the architecture hard. If you just add Nx and a few tags, but leave the actual meaning loose, you will still get drift — just with nicer graphs.


The quality bar is:


every project gets meaningful tags

generators are the default creation path

illegal structures are blocked automatically

role/app/plugin/service distinctions are reflected in the graph

ownership and manifest conventions are enforced

promoted domain assemblies are represented explicitly


If you do that, Nx becomes a real architecture enforcement layer.


If you do not, it mostly becomes task orchestration plus caching.


My blunt read:


Your overall direction looks substantially better if Nx is part of the plan in the serious way you are implying. In that case, you are not just designing a nice monorepo. You are designing an operating system for the monorepo.


That is what hard codebases actually need.


The one thing I would do next is stop discussing Nx in the abstract and lock the actual tag taxonomy and dependency matrix. That is where the architecture becomes real.

nodes.say: bruh we got orpc for the rest. and inngest
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
Yes. Exactly.


That is the full stack:


Nx for workspace graph, ownership, generation, enforcement, orchestration

oRPC for typed boundary contracts and transport-flexible service/API surfaces

Inngest for durable execution, retries, schedules, workflow observability


That is why this architecture is actually pretty serious.


Each tool covers a different failure class.


Nx answers:


what can depend on what

what is a project/unit

how things are scaffolded

how the repo stays governable as it grows


oRPC answers:


what is the callable contract

how public vs private boundaries are typed

how a service can be in-process today and remote tomorrow without changing semantics


Inngest answers:


how long-running/background/durable work executes

retries

scheduling

orchestration

execution visibility


That is the trifecta you were reaching for.


So the “half these problems” thing was underselling it a bit. The stack together solves most of the architectural enforcement problem if you assign the responsibilities cleanly.


The clean model is:


Nx
  repo graph / boundaries / generation / orchestration

oRPC
  request-response contracts
  public APIs
  private internal service boundaries
  in-process or remote transport

Inngest
  durable async execution
  schedules
  retries
  workflow timelines


And this is why your earlier instinct that “we don’t need a separate internal service host by default” is right.


Because with oRPC, the internal service boundary already exists semantically.
And with Nx, the boundary can be enforced structurally.
And with Inngest, the async plane does not need to be faked with ad hoc workers.


So the combined operating model becomes:


services/*
  capability-owned private contracts + logic
  exposed through oRPC clients/routers

plugins/server/*
  public/boundary projections

plugins/async/workflows/*
  Inngest execution bundles

plugins/async/internal/*
  private internal routers where needed

apps/hq/*
  composed assemblies

Nx
  ensures the graph stays legal


That is strong.


The one thing to stay ruthless about is not letting responsibilities smear across tools.


Bad:


using Inngest as your general internal sync RPC layer

using oRPC to fake durable execution

expecting Nx tags alone to define runtime semantics


Good:


oRPC for call boundaries

Inngest for execution boundaries

Nx for workspace/governance boundaries


That is the real lock.


So yes — “bruh we got oRPC for the rest. and Inngest” is basically correct.


That is also why the architecture is feeling more solid now: the missing pieces are not conceptual anymore. They are mostly naming, file layout, and enforcement details.


The big remaining challenge is just making the repo shape line up cleanly with that split.
