nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
Very good.


At that point, if I were an agent dropped into the codebase, I would feel like I had an actual operating environment, not just a pile of code.


With Nx generation/factory capability added, the system stops being “good architecture if humans behave” and starts becoming “good architecture by default.” That is the difference.


The stack now looks like this:


Nx
  structure, graph, generation, enforcement, orchestration

oRPC
  typed public/private call boundaries
  local or remote transport without semantic rewrite

Inngest
  durable execution plane
  retries, schedules, orchestration, observability


That is a strong trifecta because each layer constrains a different kind of chaos.


If I am solving a hard problem as an agent, what I need most is not just tools. I need:


trustworthy locality

predictable boundaries

repeatable creation patterns

low ambiguity about where new code belongs

low ambiguity about what runs where

low-cost promotion path when something grows


With Nx factories in the loop, you now have a path to all of that.


Why this changes my answer materially:


Before Nx factories, even with a good architecture, I would still worry about drift:


people create slightly different service shapes

plugins get authored inconsistently

some app entrypoints bootstrap differently

manifests drift

ownership gets fuzzy


With Nx factories, you can make the right thing the easy thing.


That matters a lot more than people admit.


A codebase becomes agent-friendly when the “unit of creation” is as disciplined as the “unit of reasoning.” Nx generators/factories are how you do that.


So if I were an agent, here’s how I would feel about the environment.


I would feel confident that I can discover the graph.
I would feel confident that capabilities, services, plugins, and apps are distinct on purpose.
I would feel confident that boundary contracts are not hand-wavy because oRPC owns them.
I would feel confident that async execution is not improvised because Inngest owns it.
I would feel confident that new units can be created correctly because Nx owns scaffolding and constraints.


That is a huge jump.


This is now the shape of a system where an agent can do serious work without constantly tripping over invisible tribal knowledge.


The really important shift is this:


You are not just designing software architecture.
You are designing authoring architecture.


That is what makes the environment strong for agents.


Hard codebases are rarely hard because the abstractions are too few. They are hard because the authoring pathways are inconsistent. One team created things one way, another team another way, and the repo becomes a museum of local exceptions.


Nx factories directly attack that.


So assuming you actually use factories for:


service creation

plugin creation

app/host assembly creation

manifest registration

tag assignment

boundary defaults

host-role templates


then yes, I would feel very strong about the environment.


Not invincible. But strong.


The remaining risks are narrower now.


First risk: over-taxonomizing.
You are still capable of inventing too many semantically similar units. Nx can scaffold nonsense consistently too. So the ontology still needs discipline.


Second risk: logic leakage.
If business logic starts leaking into plugins/apps instead of services/packages, the architecture will still rot. Nx helps, but only if you encode the right boundaries.


Third risk: dual-path execution.
If the repo starts allowing the same class of work to be done through ad hoc server logic, private oRPC services, and Inngest workflows interchangeably, you will lose clarity fast. This is probably the biggest runtime risk.


Fourth risk: premature remote-ization.
oRPC gives you transport flexibility, which is great. But if you start treating “has contract” as “must be remote,” you will create fake distributed complexity.


Those are real risks. But now they are manageable risks, not foundational weaknesses.


So my honest updated read is:


This is becoming the kind of codebase where an agent could solve genuinely difficult problems well, because the system is converging on explicit truth in four dimensions:


structural truth via Nx

boundary truth via oRPC

execution truth via Inngest

assembly truth via HQ/host composition


That is rare and powerful.


If you want the blunt version: this is now in “serious systems design” territory, not “interesting monorepo ideas” territory.


The biggest next step is not more theory. It is locking the few hardest conventions into factories and graph rules so the repo cannot drift away from the model.


At that point, I would feel not just optimistic, but dangerous in the codebase.
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say: Go deeply research the NX docs and check your risks against the capabilities. And generally just read up on
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
Thought for 15s


I went through the Nx docs with your stack in mind, and the updated answer is: the risk profile gets a lot better, but not magically. Nx can enforce much more of your architecture than I gave it credit for earlier, especially once you combine project tags, graph-aware rules, local plugins/generators, and sync generators. Nx’s model is explicitly project-graph-centric, supports tag-based dependency constraints, and can be extended with custom graph plugins that add nodes and dependencies. Nx+1


The biggest correction is this: with Nx, your repo does not have to rely only on folder conventions. You can make “service,” “plugin,” “app,” “sdk,” “server-runtime,” “async-runtime,” and domain scopes like “sales” or “hq” into first-class project metadata via tags, then enforce allowed dependency directions automatically. For JavaScript/TypeScript, @nx/enforce-module-boundaries checks imports and package.json dependencies; for broader graph enforcement across any project type, Nx Conformance provides “Enforce Project Boundaries,” though that requires Nx Enterprise. Nx+2Nx+2


That means one of my main earlier risks — authoring drift at the boundary layer — is substantially mitigated if you actually encode the matrix. Example: type:plugin may depend on type:service and type:package; type:service may depend on type:package; runtime:server projects cannot directly depend on runtime:async plugins except through approved service contracts; scope:sales can depend on scope:shared and scope:sales but not scope:billing. Nx tags support exact strings, globs, and regex patterns, so you have enough expressive power to model multiple orthogonal dimensions rather than a single flat taxonomy. Nx


Your “Nx factory capability” point is also real. Nx generators are meant for scaffolding projects and augmenting existing ones consistently, and local plugins are the preferred modern way to keep those generators inside the workspace. Nx’s own plugin tooling can generate plugins and generators, so you can build a private @rawr/workspace or similar plugin that stamps out services, plugins, app assemblies, manifests, and tags the same way every time. Nx+3Nx+3Nx+3


That is where your architecture starts to become operational instead of aspirational. A service generator can create services/todo with the contract, router, implementation, tests, tags, and default project config. A workflow plugin generator can create plugins/async/workflows/todo, wire it to Inngest, tag it type:plugin, runtime:async, execution:workflow, and register it in the right assembly manifest. An app/assembly generator can create apps/hq/server, apps/hq/async, thin entrypoints, and the right references to the HQ composition root. Nx is designed for exactly this kind of standardized code generation. Nx+1


Another place Nx is stronger than I initially framed is graph extension. If your repo needs to “understand” custom RAWR concepts beyond normal TypeScript imports, project graph plugins can add nodes and dependencies using createNodesV2 and createDependencies. In other words, if a plugin manifest or a rawr.hq.ts composition file implies a dependency that static imports alone would not reveal cleanly, you can teach Nx that relationship so the graph reflects real architecture rather than just file imports. That is a big deal for your host-bundle / plugin-manifest model. Nx+1


Sync generators are also more relevant here than I first gave them credit for. Nx supports sync generators that run via nx sync or nx sync:check, and they can update global or task-related files to keep the filesystem in the correct state. That gives you a path to auto-maintain manifest indexes, ownership files, generated registration maps, or other “repo state must stay aligned with project truth” artifacts. So if you want “declared once, compiled everywhere” behavior for plugin manifests or host assembly indexes, Nx gives you a native mechanism for that. Nx+2Nx+2


Task orchestration and inferred tasks help too, though more on the delivery side than the architecture side. Nx plugins can infer tasks from tool configuration, and targetDefaults plus project-level overrides let you standardize caching and task behavior across many projects while preserving exceptions where needed. That means once your generators stamp out projects consistently, Nx can also keep build/test/lint/dev task configuration relatively low-boilerplate. Nx+2Nx+2


So, checking my earlier risks against actual Nx capabilities:


“Taxonomy drift” is only partially solved. Nx can enforce the tags and dependency rules you choose, and generators can make creation consistent, but Nx cannot decide whether your ontology is good. It will faithfully scaffold and enforce a bad taxonomy too. So if you invent too many near-duplicate unit types, Nx won’t save you from that. This risk remains mostly architectural, not tooling-related. Nx+1


“Logic leakage” is substantially mitigated. If you tag projects correctly and enforce boundaries, you can stop apps and plugins from depending on the wrong layers, and generators can keep new projects from starting life in the wrong shape. But Nx won’t semantically inspect whether a file inside a permitted project is “too much business logic” unless you add custom conformance or lint rules. So it can fence the neighborhoods well; it cannot fully police what someone does inside a house unless you add more rules. Nx+2Nx+2


“Dual-path execution” is not really an Nx problem. Nx can help you separate workflow plugins from API plugins from services in the graph, but it cannot stop a team from choosing to implement the same business process partly in server request/response code and partly in Inngest unless you explicitly encode that discipline in your generators, code review rules, and maybe conformance checks. This is where Inngest and your own conventions still matter more than Nx. Nx+1


“Premature remoting” is also not something Nx solves directly. Nx can make local and remote service projects visible and govern dependencies, but it will not tell you when a service should stay in-process versus move behind an oRPC link. That remains a systems-design decision. oRPC gives you the transport-flexible boundary; Nx helps keep the projects and ownership sane around it. Nx+1


The strongest overall pattern I see now is this: Nx is your structural and governance layer; oRPC is your synchronous contract and transport layer; Inngest is your durable execution layer. With all three, your architecture is no longer “a clever folder structure.” It is much closer to an executable operating model for the monorepo. Nx gives you the graph, tags, generators, sync hooks, and optionally conformance/owners; oRPC gives you typed local-or-remote private/public boundaries; Inngest gives you retries, schedules, and observable background execution. Nx+3Nx+3Nx+3


My updated feeling, assuming you actually use Nx this way and not just for task running: very strong. Not because the codebase becomes simple, but because the ambiguity surface shrinks a lot. An agent in this repo could inspect the graph, rely on project tags and constraints, use generators instead of ad hoc creation, and reason about whether a capability belongs in a service, plugin, or app assembly without constantly reverse-engineering tribal conventions. Nx’s project graph, module-boundary enforcement, local plugin/generator system, and graph extension APIs make that realistic. Nx+3Nx+3Nx+3


The practical next step is not more abstraction. It is to lock the tag matrix and generator set. Specifically: define the tag dimensions, define allowed dependencies, create the local Nx plugin, add generators for service/plugin/app assembly creation, and decide whether you need Enterprise features like Conformance and Owners now or later. Once that exists, the repo starts enforcing your architecture instead of just describing it. Nx+3Nx+3Nx+3

nodes.say: What if we had to write the enterprise feature ourselves?
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
Thought for 10s


Yes, you could write a lot of it yourselves.


But you should be precise about which enterprise feature you mean, because they are not equal.


There are really three buckets:


First, features you can reproduce pretty well with open Nx primitives and some custom code.


Second, features you can approximate, but with ongoing maintenance cost.


Third, features that are technically reproducible, but are not worth rebuilding unless you absolutely have to.


The good news is that your architecture mostly depends on bucket one.


The most important thing Nx Enterprise gives you here is Conformance: graph-aware, language-agnostic rules that run over the Nx project graph. Nx’s docs are explicit that the Enterprise Conformance plugin lets you write and apply custom TypeScript rules across the entire workspace, and that there is a rule generator plus testing utilities. Nx+2Nx+2


If you had to write that yourselves, the answer is: yes, mostly.


Why? Because Nx already exposes the two hard primitives you need:


project tags and dependency constraints for module boundaries Nx+1

project graph plugin APIs like createNodesV2 and createDependencies so you can extend the graph with your own nodes and dependency edges Nx


That means a homegrown version of “enterprise conformance” could look like this:


1. Use Nx tags as the core ontology
2. Extend the graph for RAWR-specific semantics
3. Run custom validation scripts over the graph in CI
4. Fail CI when rules are violated
5. Generate reports / suggestions from the same rule engine


That is very doable.


Concretely, you could build a local Nx plugin or workspace package that:


reads the Nx graph

inspects project tags

inspects plugin manifests / rawr.hq.ts composition

checks your allowed dependency matrix

checks assembly rules

checks manifest completeness

checks ownership declarations

checks runtime-role placement rules


That would get you very far.


For your system, the kinds of custom rules worth writing would be things like:


- server plugins cannot import async plugins directly
- workflows must live under async runtime tags
- apps cannot contain domain logic files
- every plugin must export a manifest
- every service must declare contract/router/client shape
- every promoted app bundle may only compose allowed scopes
- every async workflow must be registered in exactly one async assembly


That is basically your architecture, executable.


So if the question is “could we recreate the valuable part of Conformance ourselves?” then yes.


Now the part that is less worth rebuilding is Owners.


Nx Owners compiles project-level ownership into CODEOWNERS-like output and syncs it from Nx project config. The docs are explicit that this is an Enterprise plugin and that it uses nx sync to compile ownership settings into valid CODEOWNERS files. Nx+1


Could you write your own version? Also yes.


Would I want to? Only if cost is a hard blocker.


Because a DIY owners system is not conceptually hard. It is just annoying:


project → owner mapping

path expansion / projection

output generation

Git provider formatting

sync correctness over time


That is the kind of thing you can write, but it is maintenance tax, not leverage.


So my ranking is:


Worth writing yourselves


custom graph/conformance checks

custom generators/factories

custom manifest sync/validation

custom graph extension for RAWR semantics


Maybe worth writing yourselves


owners compilation

architecture dashboards

promotion-path analyzers


Usually not worth writing yourselves


broad Nx Cloud / enterprise platform substitutes

polished org-wide distribution mechanisms unless you truly need them


There is also an important subtle point: you may not need to fully recreate Enterprise Conformance to get most of the value.


Because open Nx already gives you a lot:


tag-based module boundaries in ESLint for JS/TS imports and package dependencies Nx+1

local plugins and generators for standardized creation Nx

project graph extension APIs Nx


So your “poor man’s conformance” stack could be:


Nx tags + enforce-module-boundaries
+ custom project graph plugin
+ custom CI rule runner
+ generators/factories
+ sync generators for derived files


That is already strong.


The main thing Enterprise buys you is a more official, integrated rule runtime and adjacent tooling. But if you are willing to own some custom infra, you can absolutely build a serious substitute.


Here is the practical architecture I would use if you had to write it yourselves.


Have one internal workspace plugin, maybe @rawr/nx.


Inside it:


plugins/@rawr/nx/
  generators/
    service/
    plugin/
    app-bundle/
    workflow/
    internal-router/

  graph/
    createNodesV2
    createDependencies

  rules/
    enforce-rawr-architecture.ts
    enforce-host-bundle-composition.ts
    enforce-plugin-manifest-shape.ts
    enforce-scope-promotion.ts

  sync/
    sync-plugin-index.ts
    sync-hq-manifests.ts
    sync-owners.ts   // optional


Then in CI:


nx graph validation
+ lint with module boundaries
+ custom rawr:conformance check
+ nx sync:check


That would be a serious system.


Now the sanity check on cost.


You should only write the enterprise-equivalent yourselves if:


the Enterprise feature is structurally important to your architecture

the replacement is narrow and tailored

you are willing to maintain it as core tooling


You should not do it just because “we could.”


For your setup, writing custom conformance-like checks is justified because your architecture is unusually custom and graph-driven. Writing your own owners compiler is plausible but less leverage-heavy. Writing your own general-purpose Nx Enterprise replacement is a bad idea.


So the blunt answer is:


Yes, you can write the important part yourselves.
No, you should not think of it as “rebuilding Enterprise.”
You should think of it as writing RAWR-specific conformance and sync tooling on top of Nx primitives.


That is a much smaller, saner problem.


My recommendation:


Use open Nx features as far as they go.
Write a local Nx plugin for RAWR-specific generators, graph extensions, and rule checks.
Only mimic Enterprise features where they directly protect your architecture.


That gets you most of the value without signing up to recreate an entire product.

nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
Got it.


I’ll treat this as part of the architecture going forward:


Agent stewards are coming.
Each steward owns a domain.
They run on the agent host runtime via Nanoclaw.


So in the model, that means agent is not just another plugin surface. It is a real runtime role with domain-scoped ownership, parallel to server, async, web, and cli.


That also implies a useful constraint:
stewards should be treated as domain runtime assemblies, not random tool wrappers. Their ownership boundary matters.
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
And for owners, they get their own git alias or even email.


We have a whole thing on git + repo topology HUD as a tool
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
Good. That makes the ownership model much stronger.


So the architecture should treat ownership on three layers:


Domain ownership:


each domain has a steward on the agent host runtime via Nanoclaw


Repo/worktree authorship:


each owner/steward can have its own git alias and possibly its own email identity


Topology visibility and control:


the git + repo topology HUD is the operational surface for seeing who owns what, where work is happening, and how branches/worktrees map back to domains/stewards


That means “owner” is not just a CODEOWNERS-style label. It becomes an actual runtime and authorship identity.


So going forward I’d model this as:


domain
  -> owned by steward
  -> steward runs on agent host
  -> steward has git identity
  -> steward activity visible in repo topology HUD


That is much more powerful than static ownership metadata. It turns ownership into something executable and inspectable.


It also means any future ownership/conformance tooling should probably hook into:


domain/steward mapping

git identity mapping

repo/worktree topology visibility

host/runtime placement


That is worth keeping central, not bolting on later.
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say: describe how nx would be used to enforce domain ownership in a scaled version of our snowflake factory architecture (service core + plugin spokes)
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
Thought for 8s


Nx would enforce domain ownership in your snowflake factory by turning “ownership” from a naming convention into a graph rule.


The right mental model is:


service core = the capability truth
plugin spokes = runtime projections of that capability
domain ownership = who is allowed to create, compose, and depend on those units


Nx is useful here because it already treats the repo as a project graph, supports per-project tags, and can enforce dependency constraints from those tags. For JavaScript and TypeScript that happens through @nx/enforce-module-boundaries; for graph-wide, language-agnostic enforcement Nx offers Conformance on Enterprise. Nx+1


So in a scaled version of your model, I would encode ownership across multiple tag dimensions, not one flat “domain” label. Nx explicitly supports multiple tag dimensions and richer matching with exact tags, globs, and regex. Nx+1


The tag set should look roughly like this:


scope:hq | scope:sales | scope:todo | scope:billing
type:service | type:plugin | type:app | type:sdk
runtime:server | runtime:async | runtime:web | runtime:agent | runtime:cli | runtime:neutral
owner:steward-sales | owner:steward-todo | owner:platform
visibility:public | visibility:private | visibility:internal
execution:workflow | internal-router | consumer | command | ui | none


That gives you enough information to ask real ownership questions.


For example, a sales service core project might be tagged:


{
  "tags": [
    "scope:sales",
    "type:service",
    "runtime:neutral",
    "owner:steward-sales",
    "visibility:private"
  ]
}


A sales async workflow spoke might be:


{
  "tags": [
    "scope:sales",
    "type:plugin",
    "runtime:async",
    "execution:workflow",
    "owner:steward-sales"
  ]
}


A sales agent steward runtime might be:


{
  "tags": [
    "scope:sales",
    "type:app",
    "runtime:agent",
    "owner:steward-sales"
  ]
}


Then you use Nx boundaries to enforce the ownership model at three levels.


First, dependency ownership.


A sales-owned project should only depend on:


itself

shared/platform projects

other explicitly allowed sales-adjacent projects


That is straight Nx boundary enforcement: projects with scope:sales can only depend on scope:sales, scope:shared, scope:platform, etc. Nx’s built-in boundary rules are designed exactly for this tag-based pattern. Nx


That means:


plugins/server/sales-api can depend on services/sales-*

plugins/async/sales-workflows can depend on services/sales-*

plugins/agent/sales-steward can depend on services/sales-*

but sales-* cannot casually reach into billing-*


Second, role ownership.


This is the part people usually miss.


Your architecture is not just about “which domain owns which code.” It is also about “which domain is allowed to project itself into which runtime.”


So you use tag combinations to say things like:


type:plugin + runtime:async must come from projects tagged execution:workflow|consumer|internal-router

type:plugin + runtime:server must not depend directly on runtime:async plugin projects

type:app + runtime:agent must belong to exactly one domain owner


Nx can enforce these with boundary rules where imports/dependencies are visible, and Conformance can enforce graph-wide standards across all dependency edges. Nx+1


Third, assembly ownership.


This is where your HQ / sub-HQ model matters.


If apps/hq is the global assembly and apps/sales is a promoted domain assembly, you want rules like:


apps/sales/* may only compose scope:sales plus approved shared/platform units

apps/hq/* may compose everything

apps/todo/* may not silently compose scope:sales


Out of the box, Nx boundaries get you part of this. For the rest, you extend the project graph or run custom graph checks. Nx explicitly exposes project graph plugin APIs to add nodes and dependencies, which is the right hook if your rawr.hq.ts or plugin manifests define composition relationships that normal imports do not fully reveal. Nx+1


That is how ownership becomes real in your architecture:


domain owns service core
domain-owned plugins project that core into approved runtimes
domain apps assemble only approved owned scopes
Nx graph rules make violations fail


The most powerful part is not just linting. It is generation.


Nx generators are the real enforcement weapon here because they make the correct ownership shape the default. Nx supports local plugins and generators for custom scaffolding, and sync generators for repo-wide derived state. Nx


So your local Nx plugin should generate every snowflake-factory unit with ownership baked in.


For example:


nx g @rawr/workspace:service sales-account --scope=sales --owner=steward-sales
nx g @rawr/workspace:plugin sales-api --runtime=server --scope=sales --owner=steward-sales
nx g @rawr/workspace:plugin sales-workflows --runtime=async --execution=workflow --scope=sales --owner=steward-sales
nx g @rawr/workspace:app sales --roles=server,async,agent --owner=steward-sales


Each generator should:


create the project

assign tags

stamp owner metadata

wire manifest defaults

register the project in the right assembly or index


That way ownership is not inferred after the fact. It is authored at creation time.


Then you add a second enforcement layer: sync generators.


Nx sync generators can update root-level derived files when you run nx sync or nx sync:check. Nx


That is where you generate things like:


domain-to-steward maps

HQ composition indexes

plugin registries

git/CODEOWNERS-like outputs

topology HUD metadata


So ownership lives in one source of truth, and Nx compiles it outward.


In your architecture, that likely means the source of truth should be per-project metadata:


tags

owner field

steward identity

maybe git alias/email mapping


And then a sync generator emits:


repo topology HUD inputs

ownership index JSON

CODEOWNERS-style file if you want one

agent steward registry


That is how you connect repo ownership to runtime ownership.


Here is the concrete dependency matrix I would enforce first.


For service core and plugin spokes:


type:service
  -> may depend on type:package, type:sdk
  -> may not depend on type:plugin, type:app

type:plugin + runtime:server
  -> may depend on type:service, type:package, type:sdk
  -> may not depend on runtime:async plugins directly

type:plugin + runtime:async
  -> may depend on type:service, type:package, type:sdk
  -> may not depend on server plugins directly

type:app
  -> may depend on type:plugin, type:service, type:package, type:sdk
  -> should not contain reusable domain logic


For domain ownership:


scope:sales
  -> may depend on scope:sales, scope:shared, scope:platform

scope:todo
  -> may depend on scope:todo, scope:shared, scope:platform

scope:hq
  -> may depend on *


And for agent stewards:


runtime:agent
  -> every project must have exactly one owner:steward-*
  -> scope:* must match steward domain


Nx’s built-in boundary system gets you much of the first two layers directly through tags and constraints. Nx
For the last mile — “every agent app must have exactly one steward owner,” “every promoted assembly must only compose approved scopes,” “every plugin must declare owner metadata” — you either use Nx Conformance or write your own custom rule runner on top of the graph. Nx Enterprise provides Conformance for graph-wide checks and also an Owners-related rule, but if you do not want Enterprise, the same architecture can still be approximated with local plugins, graph inspection, and CI checks. Nx+1


So the scaled version of your snowflake factory looks like this:


services/
  sales-*
  todo-*
  billing-*

plugins/
  server/...
  async/...
  web/...
  agent/...

apps/
  hq/...
  sales/...
  todo/...


But Nx makes the hidden structure explicit:


service core projects
  tagged by scope, owner, type

plugin spoke projects
  tagged by scope, owner, runtime, execution, type

app assembly projects
  tagged by scope, owner, runtime, type


And the ownership policy becomes executable:


only the sales steward’s domain can evolve sales core and spokes

sales spokes can only project sales core into approved runtimes

promoted sales assemblies can only compose sales-owned and shared units

agent steward runtime for sales maps back to the same owner metadata

topology HUD and git identity can be generated from the same source


That is the key point: Nx does not just enforce “imports are legal.” In your architecture, Nx becomes the compiler for domain ownership.


The one thing I would not do is treat folder names as the ownership system. Folder names help humans. Tags, generators, graph rules, and sync outputs are what make ownership durable at scale.
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say: Yo, do we even need, do we even need folders for the apps, like sales and todo, or do we just need a single file? I mean, I can see the need for it growing, and we don't wanna, we don't wanna shake the base, right? We wanna keep things stable for scaffolding reasons and front-end force reasons, and for legibility reasons, right? Remember that agents also need topology.
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
You do not need the folders today if an app assembly is just:


one rawr.hq.ts

one thin entrypoint per role

maybe one config file


But you probably still should keep the folders.


Because this is one of those cases where the folder is buying you topology stability, not current implementation necessity.


That matters for:


agent navigation

scaffolding consistency

future growth without churn

clear promotion path

keeping “assembly” as a first-class thing


So the honest answer is:


Functionally: no, not always.
Architecturally/topologically: yes, probably.


The key distinction is between a thing being a real project node versus merely a file.


In your system, apps/hq, apps/sales, apps/todo are not just “files that happen to start something.” They are potential assembly roots. Even when they are tiny, they represent:


a bounded host bundle

a composition authority

a deployment unit candidate

a promotion boundary

an ownership/topology node


That is enough reason to give them a stable home.


If you reduce them to only single files floating around, you save almost nothing, but you lose a lot of clarity. Agents and humans both benefit from being able to treat “sales app assembly” as a place, not just a filename.


So I would keep the structure like:


apps/
  hq/
    rawr.hq.ts
    server/
      main.ts
    async/
      main.ts

  sales/
    rawr.hq.ts
    server/
      main.ts


Even if those directories are thin.


That said, I would not overbuild them either.


The right rule is:


An app assembly folder should start life as a thin stable container, not a mini-codebase.


So yes, the folder exists.
But most real logic should still live in:


services/*

plugins/*

packages/*

packages/hq-sdk/*


The app folder should mostly contain:


composition file(s)

role entrypoints

local app config

maybe deployment descriptors later


That is all.


This is one of those “don’t shake the base” decisions. You want the scaffold shape to be stable even if the implementation inside it is initially small.


There is also an Nx reason to keep the folder.


Nx thinks in terms of projects. A project that represents an assembly root is easier to reason about, tag, generate, own, and promote if it has a stable directory. That gives you:


cleaner project identity

clearer graph nodes

less churn when the assembly grows

better generator targets

better topology for agents


So if the question is:


“Should apps/sales be a folder even if it only contains one or two files for now?”


My answer is yes.


If the question is:


“Should every role under every app always get a folder from day one?”


That is less absolute.


You could compress early if you want:


apps/
  sales/
    rawr.hq.ts
    server.ts
    async.ts


and later expand to:


apps/
  sales/
    rawr.hq.ts
    server/
      main.ts
    async/
      main.ts


That is a reasonable compromise.


But I would still keep the app root folder itself.


So the hierarchy I’d recommend is:


Keep apps/<assembly>/ as a stable directory node.
Inside it, allow role entrypoints to start either as files or folders depending on how much structure you already need.


That gives you both:


stable topology

low initial overhead


So the best near-term shape is probably:


apps/
  hq/
    rawr.hq.ts
    server.ts
    async.ts
    cli.ts

  sales/
    rawr.hq.ts
    server.ts


Then when one role grows, expand just that role:


apps/
  hq/
    rawr.hq.ts
    server/
      main.ts
      config.ts
    async/
      main.ts
      workers.ts


That is the most leverage-efficient version.


So the final answer is:


keep the apps/<assembly> folders

do not feel forced to make every role a folder immediately

treat app folders as stable topology nodes for humans, agents, Nx, and future promotion

keep them thin so the real logic stays elsewhere


That is the cleanest balance.

nodes.say: yeah but that leaves variability for little gain right
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
Yes. That variability is probably not worth it.


If your goal is stable topology for humans, agents, scaffolding, and Nx project identity, then pick one shape and keep it uniform.


So I would standardize on:


apps/
  hq/
    rawr.hq.ts
    server/
      main.ts
    async/
      main.ts
    cli/
      main.ts

  sales/
    rawr.hq.ts
    server/
      main.ts


Even when the role folder is thin.


Why? Because the gain is not in today’s file count. The gain is in removing conditional structure.


If some assemblies use:


server.ts


and others use:


server/main.ts


then every generator, graph assumption, agent heuristic, and human expectation now has to handle both.


That is noise.


The architecture you are building benefits more from uniformity than from shaving one folder level.


This is one of those places where the right move is boring:
choose the slightly more explicit structure and make it universal.


The cost is tiny:


+ one extra folder
+ one extra main.ts


The benefit is real:


zero ambiguity

easier generation

easier promotion

easier agent traversal

easier role-local growth

cleaner Nx project conventions

fewer special cases forever


So yes, your pushback is right. Allowing both shapes is unnecessary variability.


I would lock:


apps/<assembly>/rawr.hq.ts

apps/<assembly>/<role>/main.ts


for every assembly/role that exists.


No mixed shorthand.
No “flatten until needed.”
No alternate micro-shapes.


Just one shape.


That is the better base.

nodes.say: put all of what we’ve gained into a doc
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model
Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model
Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model
Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
Thought for 11s


Yes — that changes the picture a lot.


I could not inspect your repo files directly through the GitHub connector because no repo is currently selected in this chat, so I could not verify the exact Nx config you already wrote. But your underlying point is right: Nx can solve a large chunk of the “authoring drift” risk I was warning about.


The important shift is this:


Without Nx, your architecture is mostly a social contract.
With Nx done properly, it becomes a partially executable contract.


That is a big difference.


Nx gives you three major levers that map directly onto the problems we were discussing.


First, the project graph. Nx models the workspace as projects and dependencies, and uses that graph as a first-class primitive. Projects are usually detected from package.json or project.json, and Nx can show you the graph and per-project details. Nx+1


That matters because your whole design depends on being able to say things like:


server plugins may depend on services and packages, but not async plugins

services may depend on packages, but not apps

apps assemble plugins, but should not contain deep business logic

some promoted domain assembly should only depend on its allowed subtree


That is exactly the kind of thing a graph-aware system can reason about.


Second, module boundary enforcement. Nx’s @nx/enforce-module-boundaries rule lets you tag projects and define dependency constraints so projects can only depend on the kinds of projects they are allowed to depend on. Nx also supports multiple tag dimensions, which is exactly what your architecture wants. Nx+1


This is where your architecture stops being just nice words. You can encode tags like:


type:app
type:plugin
type:service
type:package

runtime:server
runtime:async
runtime:web
runtime:cli
runtime:agent

scope:hq
scope:sales
scope:todo
scope:platform


And then define actual constraints such as:


type:plugin -> may depend on type:service and type:package
type:service -> may depend on type:package
type:app -> may depend on type:plugin, type:service, type:package
runtime:server -> may not depend on runtime:async plugin projects directly
runtime:async -> may depend on async workflows/internal consumers
scope:sales -> may depend on scope:shared and scope:sales


That is exactly the kind of discipline your model needs.


Third, Conformance. Nx’s Conformance plugin can enforce rules against the entire project graph, not just TypeScript imports. It supports custom rules in TypeScript and can enforce architectural standards, ownership, and configuration policies workspace-wide. But it requires an Nx Enterprise license. Nx+1


That matters because some of the rules you want are not just import rules. They are structural rules, like:


every plugin project must declare a manifest

every async workflow project must register through the approved shape

apps may not implement business logic directly

every project must declare an owner

every domain promotion app must only compose approved scopes

no project in plugins/server/* may import from plugins/async/* directly unless via approved contract


Those are exactly the sort of rules Conformance is good for.


So your instinct is right: Nx is not a side detail here. It is a core enforcement layer for the architecture.


The stronger framing is:


RAWR gives you the semantic model.
Nx gives you the enforcement and graph machinery.


That is a good pairing.


The real question becomes: how much should you lean on Nx, and where should you stop?


Here is the right split.


Use Nx for:


project graph visibility

dependency boundaries

project tagging

generation/scaffolding

affected runs

target conventions

cacheable task orchestration

conformance checks if you have Enterprise


Do not expect Nx alone to solve:


runtime semantics

transport flexibility

host-role composition logic

manifest assembly semantics

“what is a workflow plugin vs internal router” unless you encode those rules explicitly


In other words, Nx is a force multiplier, not the architecture itself.


The best version of your system would use Nx in four concrete ways.


First, make every meaningful unit a real Nx project.


That means:


each service boundary project

each plugin project

each app assembly

each host SDK/runtime package

maybe even each promoted domain bundle root


The goal is that Nx can actually see and enforce the structure.


Second, tag aggressively and in multiple dimensions.


I would use at least these dimensions:


kind:app | plugin | service | package | sdk
runtime:server | async | web | cli | agent | neutral
scope:hq | sales | todo | platform | shared
visibility:public | private | internal
execution:sync | workflow | consumer | schedule | command | ui


You do not need all of them on day one, but that is the shape.


Third, use generators heavily.


This is where Nx really helps your architecture survive contact with reality.


You should not want humans manually creating:


service projects

plugin projects

app assembly entrypoints

manifest files

tags

boilerplate role bootstraps


You want commands that generate a correct unit every time.


Examples:


nx g @rawr/workspace:service sales-account --scope=sales
nx g @rawr/workspace:plugin sales-api --runtime=server --scope=sales
nx g @rawr/workspace:plugin sales-workflows --runtime=async --execution=workflow --scope=sales
nx g @rawr/workspace:app sales --roles=server,async,web


That is how you stop drift.


Fourth, add graph-aware conformance and ownership rules where it matters most.


Even if you do not use Nx Enterprise right away, start with ESLint boundary rules and strict tagging. If you do have Enterprise, Conformance is exactly the next layer you want because it can inspect the full project graph and enforce broader organizational rules. Nx+1


So how are you looking overall, once Nx is in the picture?


Much stronger.


The architecture stops being “a good mental model” and starts becoming “a build-governed workspace model.”


That is a major upgrade.


If I were an agent inside a codebase like that, my confidence would go up a lot for a few reasons.


I could inspect the graph and see actual project boundaries instead of inferring them from folders. Nx explicitly treats the project graph as central to the workspace mental model. Nx+1


I could trust that illegal imports and dependency drift would get caught automatically rather than relying on code review memory. Nx’s boundary tooling is designed for exactly that. Nx+1


I could rely on generators to produce the right skeletons, which means the workspace would remain structurally consistent over time instead of each new project becoming a custom snowflake.


And if you add Conformance, I could rely on repo-wide architecture rules beyond simple import restrictions. Nx+1


That said, there is one important correction to make.


Nx does not magically solve “half these problems” unless you are willing to encode the architecture hard. If you just add Nx and a few tags, but leave the actual meaning loose, you will still get drift — just with nicer graphs.


The quality bar is:


every project gets meaningful tags

generators are the default creation path

illegal structures are blocked automatically

role/app/plugin/service distinctions are reflected in the graph

ownership and manifest conventions are enforced

promoted domain assemblies are represented explicitly


If you do that, Nx becomes a real architecture enforcement layer.


If you do not, it mostly becomes task orchestration plus caching.


My blunt read:


Your overall direction looks substantially better if Nx is part of the plan in the serious way you are implying. In that case, you are not just designing a nice monorepo. You are designing an operating system for the monorepo.


That is what hard codebases actually need.


The one thing I would do next is stop discussing Nx in the abstract and lock the actual tag taxonomy and dependency matrix. That is where the architecture becomes real.

nodes.say: bruh we got orpc for the rest. and inngest
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
Yes. Exactly.


That is the full stack:


Nx for workspace graph, ownership, generation, enforcement, orchestration

oRPC for typed boundary contracts and transport-flexible service/API surfaces

Inngest for durable execution, retries, schedules, workflow observability


That is why this architecture is actually pretty serious.


Each tool covers a different failure class.


Nx answers:


what can depend on what

what is a project/unit

how things are scaffolded

how the repo stays governable as it grows


oRPC answers:


what is the callable contract

how public vs private boundaries are typed

how a service can be in-process today and remote tomorrow without changing semantics


Inngest answers:


how long-running/background/durable work executes

retries

scheduling

orchestration

execution visibility


That is the trifecta you were reaching for.


So the “half these problems” thing was underselling it a bit. The stack together solves most of the architectural enforcement problem if you assign the responsibilities cleanly.


The clean model is:


Nx
  repo graph / boundaries / generation / orchestration

oRPC
  request-response contracts
  public APIs
  private internal service boundaries
  in-process or remote transport

Inngest
  durable async execution
  schedules
  retries
  workflow timelines


And this is why your earlier instinct that “we don’t need a separate internal service host by default” is right.


Because with oRPC, the internal service boundary already exists semantically.
And with Nx, the boundary can be enforced structurally.
And with Inngest, the async plane does not need to be faked with ad hoc workers.


So the combined operating model becomes:


services/*
  capability-owned private contracts + logic
  exposed through oRPC clients/routers

plugins/server/*
  public/boundary projections

plugins/async/workflows/*
  Inngest execution bundles

plugins/async/internal/*
  private internal routers where needed

apps/hq/*
  composed assemblies

Nx
  ensures the graph stays legal


That is strong.


The one thing to stay ruthless about is not letting responsibilities smear across tools.


Bad:


using Inngest as your general internal sync RPC layer

using oRPC to fake durable execution

expecting Nx tags alone to define runtime semantics


Good:


oRPC for call boundaries

Inngest for execution boundaries

Nx for workspace/governance boundaries


That is the real lock.


So yes — “bruh we got oRPC for the rest. and Inngest” is basically correct.


That is also why the architecture is feeling more solid now: the missing pieces are not conceptual anymore. They are mostly naming, file layout, and enforcement details.


The big remaining challenge is just making the repo shape line up cleanly with that split.

nodes.say: okay, now how would you feel? assume NX factory capability as well
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
Very good.


At that point, if I were an agent dropped into the codebase, I would feel like I had an actual operating environment, not just a pile of code.


With Nx generation/factory capability added, the system stops being “good architecture if humans behave” and starts becoming “good architecture by default.” That is the difference.


The stack now looks like this:


Nx
  structure, graph, generation, enforcement, orchestration

oRPC
  typed public/private call boundaries
  local or remote transport without semantic rewrite

Inngest
  durable execution plane
  retries, schedules, orchestration, observability


That is a strong trifecta because each layer constrains a different kind of chaos.


If I am solving a hard problem as an agent, what I need most is not just tools. I need:


trustworthy locality

predictable boundaries

repeatable creation patterns

low ambiguity about where new code belongs

low ambiguity about what runs where

low-cost promotion path when something grows


With Nx factories in the loop, you now have a path to all of that.


Why this changes my answer materially:


Before Nx factories, even with a good architecture, I would still worry about drift:


people create slightly different service shapes

plugins get authored inconsistently

some app entrypoints bootstrap differently

manifests drift

ownership gets fuzzy


With Nx factories, you can make the right thing the easy thing.


That matters a lot more than people admit.


A codebase becomes agent-friendly when the “unit of creation” is as disciplined as the “unit of reasoning.” Nx generators/factories are how you do that.


So if I were an agent, here’s how I would feel about the environment.


I would feel confident that I can discover the graph.
I would feel confident that capabilities, services, plugins, and apps are distinct on purpose.
I would feel confident that boundary contracts are not hand-wavy because oRPC owns them.
I would feel confident that async execution is not improvised because Inngest owns it.
I would feel confident that new units can be created correctly because Nx owns scaffolding and constraints.


That is a huge jump.


This is now the shape of a system where an agent can do serious work without constantly tripping over invisible tribal knowledge.


The really important shift is this:


You are not just designing software architecture.
You are designing authoring architecture.


That is what makes the environment strong for agents.


Hard codebases are rarely hard because the abstractions are too few. They are hard because the authoring pathways are inconsistent. One team created things one way, another team another way, and the repo becomes a museum of local exceptions.


Nx factories directly attack that.


So assuming you actually use factories for:


service creation

plugin creation

app/host assembly creation

manifest registration

tag assignment

boundary defaults

host-role templates


then yes, I would feel very strong about the environment.


Not invincible. But strong.


The remaining risks are narrower now.


First risk: over-taxonomizing.
You are still capable of inventing too many semantically similar units. Nx can scaffold nonsense consistently too. So the ontology still needs discipline.


Second risk: logic leakage.
If business logic starts leaking into plugins/apps instead of services/packages, the architecture will still rot. Nx helps, but only if you encode the right boundaries.


Third risk: dual-path execution.
If the repo starts allowing the same class of work to be done through ad hoc server logic, private oRPC services, and Inngest workflows interchangeably, you will lose clarity fast. This is probably the biggest runtime risk.


Fourth risk: premature remote-ization.
oRPC gives you transport flexibility, which is great. But if you start treating “has contract” as “must be remote,” you will create fake distributed complexity.


Those are real risks. But now they are manageable risks, not foundational weaknesses.


So my honest updated read is:


This is becoming the kind of codebase where an agent could solve genuinely difficult problems well, because the system is converging on explicit truth in four dimensions:


structural truth via Nx

boundary truth via oRPC

execution truth via Inngest

assembly truth via HQ/host composition


That is rare and powerful.


If you want the blunt version: this is now in “serious systems design” territory, not “interesting monorepo ideas” territory.


The biggest next step is not more theory. It is locking the few hardest conventions into factories and graph rules so the repo cannot drift away from the model.


At that point, I would feel not just optimistic, but dangerous in the codebase.

nodes.say: Go deeply research the NX docs and check your risks against the capabilities. And generally just read up on
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
Thought for 15s


I went through the Nx docs with your stack in mind, and the updated answer is: the risk profile gets a lot better, but not magically. Nx can enforce much more of your architecture than I gave it credit for earlier, especially once you combine project tags, graph-aware rules, local plugins/generators, and sync generators. Nx’s model is explicitly project-graph-centric, supports tag-based dependency constraints, and can be extended with custom graph plugins that add nodes and dependencies. Nx+1


The biggest correction is this: with Nx, your repo does not have to rely only on folder conventions. You can make “service,” “plugin,” “app,” “sdk,” “server-runtime,” “async-runtime,” and domain scopes like “sales” or “hq” into first-class project metadata via tags, then enforce allowed dependency directions automatically. For JavaScript/TypeScript, @nx/enforce-module-boundaries checks imports and package.json dependencies; for broader graph enforcement across any project type, Nx Conformance provides “Enforce Project Boundaries,” though that requires Nx Enterprise. Nx+2Nx+2


That means one of my main earlier risks — authoring drift at the boundary layer — is substantially mitigated if you actually encode the matrix. Example: type:plugin may depend on type:service and type:package; type:service may depend on type:package; runtime:server projects cannot directly depend on runtime:async plugins except through approved service contracts; scope:sales can depend on scope:shared and scope:sales but not scope:billing. Nx tags support exact strings, globs, and regex patterns, so you have enough expressive power to model multiple orthogonal dimensions rather than a single flat taxonomy. Nx


Your “Nx factory capability” point is also real. Nx generators are meant for scaffolding projects and augmenting existing ones consistently, and local plugins are the preferred modern way to keep those generators inside the workspace. Nx’s own plugin tooling can generate plugins and generators, so you can build a private @rawr/workspace or similar plugin that stamps out services, plugins, app assemblies, manifests, and tags the same way every time. Nx+3Nx+3Nx+3


That is where your architecture starts to become operational instead of aspirational. A service generator can create services/todo with the contract, router, implementation, tests, tags, and default project config. A workflow plugin generator can create plugins/async/workflows/todo, wire it to Inngest, tag it type:plugin, runtime:async, execution:workflow, and register it in the right assembly manifest. An app/assembly generator can create apps/hq/server, apps/hq/async, thin entrypoints, and the right references to the HQ composition root. Nx is designed for exactly this kind of standardized code generation. Nx+1


Another place Nx is stronger than I initially framed is graph extension. If your repo needs to “understand” custom RAWR concepts beyond normal TypeScript imports, project graph plugins can add nodes and dependencies using createNodesV2 and createDependencies. In other words, if a plugin manifest or a rawr.hq.ts composition file implies a dependency that static imports alone would not reveal cleanly, you can teach Nx that relationship so the graph reflects real architecture rather than just file imports. That is a big deal for your host-bundle / plugin-manifest model. Nx+1


Sync generators are also more relevant here than I first gave them credit for. Nx supports sync generators that run via nx sync or nx sync:check, and they can update global or task-related files to keep the filesystem in the correct state. That gives you a path to auto-maintain manifest indexes, ownership files, generated registration maps, or other “repo state must stay aligned with project truth” artifacts. So if you want “declared once, compiled everywhere” behavior for plugin manifests or host assembly indexes, Nx gives you a native mechanism for that. Nx+2Nx+2


Task orchestration and inferred tasks help too, though more on the delivery side than the architecture side. Nx plugins can infer tasks from tool configuration, and targetDefaults plus project-level overrides let you standardize caching and task behavior across many projects while preserving exceptions where needed. That means once your generators stamp out projects consistently, Nx can also keep build/test/lint/dev task configuration relatively low-boilerplate. Nx+2Nx+2


So, checking my earlier risks against actual Nx capabilities:


“Taxonomy drift” is only partially solved. Nx can enforce the tags and dependency rules you choose, and generators can make creation consistent, but Nx cannot decide whether your ontology is good. It will faithfully scaffold and enforce a bad taxonomy too. So if you invent too many near-duplicate unit types, Nx won’t save you from that. This risk remains mostly architectural, not tooling-related. Nx+1


“Logic leakage” is substantially mitigated. If you tag projects correctly and enforce boundaries, you can stop apps and plugins from depending on the wrong layers, and generators can keep new projects from starting life in the wrong shape. But Nx won’t semantically inspect whether a file inside a permitted project is “too much business logic” unless you add custom conformance or lint rules. So it can fence the neighborhoods well; it cannot fully police what someone does inside a house unless you add more rules. Nx+2Nx+2


“Dual-path execution” is not really an Nx problem. Nx can help you separate workflow plugins from API plugins from services in the graph, but it cannot stop a team from choosing to implement the same business process partly in server request/response code and partly in Inngest unless you explicitly encode that discipline in your generators, code review rules, and maybe conformance checks. This is where Inngest and your own conventions still matter more than Nx. Nx+1


“Premature remoting” is also not something Nx solves directly. Nx can make local and remote service projects visible and govern dependencies, but it will not tell you when a service should stay in-process versus move behind an oRPC link. That remains a systems-design decision. oRPC gives you the transport-flexible boundary; Nx helps keep the projects and ownership sane around it. Nx+1


The strongest overall pattern I see now is this: Nx is your structural and governance layer; oRPC is your synchronous contract and transport layer; Inngest is your durable execution layer. With all three, your architecture is no longer “a clever folder structure.” It is much closer to an executable operating model for the monorepo. Nx gives you the graph, tags, generators, sync hooks, and optionally conformance/owners; oRPC gives you typed local-or-remote private/public boundaries; Inngest gives you retries, schedules, and observable background execution. Nx+3Nx+3Nx+3


My updated feeling, assuming you actually use Nx this way and not just for task running: very strong. Not because the codebase becomes simple, but because the ambiguity surface shrinks a lot. An agent in this repo could inspect the graph, rely on project tags and constraints, use generators instead of ad hoc creation, and reason about whether a capability belongs in a service, plugin, or app assembly without constantly reverse-engineering tribal conventions. Nx’s project graph, module-boundary enforcement, local plugin/generator system, and graph extension APIs make that realistic. Nx+3Nx+3Nx+3


The practical next step is not more abstraction. It is to lock the tag matrix and generator set. Specifically: define the tag dimensions, define allowed dependencies, create the local Nx plugin, add generators for service/plugin/app assembly creation, and decide whether you need Enterprise features like Conformance and Owners now or later. Once that exists, the repo starts enforcing your architecture instead of just describing it. Nx+3Nx+3Nx+3
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say: What if we had to write the enterprise feature ourselves?
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
Thought for 10s


Yes, you could write a lot of it yourselves.


But you should be precise about which enterprise feature you mean, because they are not equal.


There are really three buckets:


First, features you can reproduce pretty well with open Nx primitives and some custom code.


Second, features you can approximate, but with ongoing maintenance cost.


Third, features that are technically reproducible, but are not worth rebuilding unless you absolutely have to.


The good news is that your architecture mostly depends on bucket one.


The most important thing Nx Enterprise gives you here is Conformance: graph-aware, language-agnostic rules that run over the Nx project graph. Nx’s docs are explicit that the Enterprise Conformance plugin lets you write and apply custom TypeScript rules across the entire workspace, and that there is a rule generator plus testing utilities. Nx+2Nx+2


If you had to write that yourselves, the answer is: yes, mostly.


Why? Because Nx already exposes the two hard primitives you need:


project tags and dependency constraints for module boundaries Nx+1

project graph plugin APIs like createNodesV2 and createDependencies so you can extend the graph with your own nodes and dependency edges Nx


That means a homegrown version of “enterprise conformance” could look like this:


1. Use Nx tags as the core ontology
2. Extend the graph for RAWR-specific semantics
3. Run custom validation scripts over the graph in CI
4. Fail CI when rules are violated
5. Generate reports / suggestions from the same rule engine


That is very doable.


Concretely, you could build a local Nx plugin or workspace package that:


reads the Nx graph

inspects project tags

inspects plugin manifests / rawr.hq.ts composition

checks your allowed dependency matrix

checks assembly rules

checks manifest completeness

checks ownership declarations

checks runtime-role placement rules


That would get you very far.


For your system, the kinds of custom rules worth writing would be things like:


- server plugins cannot import async plugins directly
- workflows must live under async runtime tags
- apps cannot contain domain logic files
- every plugin must export a manifest
- every service must declare contract/router/client shape
- every promoted app bundle may only compose allowed scopes
- every async workflow must be registered in exactly one async assembly


That is basically your architecture, executable.


So if the question is “could we recreate the valuable part of Conformance ourselves?” then yes.


Now the part that is less worth rebuilding is Owners.


Nx Owners compiles project-level ownership into CODEOWNERS-like output and syncs it from Nx project config. The docs are explicit that this is an Enterprise plugin and that it uses nx sync to compile ownership settings into valid CODEOWNERS files. Nx+1


Could you write your own version? Also yes.


Would I want to? Only if cost is a hard blocker.


Because a DIY owners system is not conceptually hard. It is just annoying:


project → owner mapping

path expansion / projection

output generation

Git provider formatting

sync correctness over time


That is the kind of thing you can write, but it is maintenance tax, not leverage.


So my ranking is:


Worth writing yourselves


custom graph/conformance checks

custom generators/factories

custom manifest sync/validation

custom graph extension for RAWR semantics


Maybe worth writing yourselves


owners compilation

architecture dashboards

promotion-path analyzers


Usually not worth writing yourselves


broad Nx Cloud / enterprise platform substitutes

polished org-wide distribution mechanisms unless you truly need them


There is also an important subtle point: you may not need to fully recreate Enterprise Conformance to get most of the value.


Because open Nx already gives you a lot:


tag-based module boundaries in ESLint for JS/TS imports and package dependencies Nx+1

local plugins and generators for standardized creation Nx

project graph extension APIs Nx


So your “poor man’s conformance” stack could be:


Nx tags + enforce-module-boundaries
+ custom project graph plugin
+ custom CI rule runner
+ generators/factories
+ sync generators for derived files


That is already strong.


The main thing Enterprise buys you is a more official, integrated rule runtime and adjacent tooling. But if you are willing to own some custom infra, you can absolutely build a serious substitute.


Here is the practical architecture I would use if you had to write it yourselves.


Have one internal workspace plugin, maybe @rawr/nx.


Inside it:


plugins/@rawr/nx/
  generators/
    service/
    plugin/
    app-bundle/
    workflow/
    internal-router/

  graph/
    createNodesV2
    createDependencies

  rules/
    enforce-rawr-architecture.ts
    enforce-host-bundle-composition.ts
    enforce-plugin-manifest-shape.ts
    enforce-scope-promotion.ts

  sync/
    sync-plugin-index.ts
    sync-hq-manifests.ts
    sync-owners.ts   // optional


Then in CI:


nx graph validation
+ lint with module boundaries
+ custom rawr:conformance check
+ nx sync:check


That would be a serious system.


Now the sanity check on cost.


You should only write the enterprise-equivalent yourselves if:


the Enterprise feature is structurally important to your architecture

the replacement is narrow and tailored

you are willing to maintain it as core tooling


You should not do it just because “we could.”


For your setup, writing custom conformance-like checks is justified because your architecture is unusually custom and graph-driven. Writing your own owners compiler is plausible but less leverage-heavy. Writing your own general-purpose Nx Enterprise replacement is a bad idea.


So the blunt answer is:


Yes, you can write the important part yourselves.
No, you should not think of it as “rebuilding Enterprise.”
You should think of it as writing RAWR-specific conformance and sync tooling on top of Nx primitives.


That is a much smaller, saner problem.


My recommendation:


Use open Nx features as far as they go.
Write a local Nx plugin for RAWR-specific generators, graph extensions, and rule checks.
Only mimic Enterprise features where they directly protect your architecture.


That gets you most of the value without signing up to recreate an entire product.
