nodes.say: idk what that is but remember that we have agent stewards coming, each which has ownership over a domain and runs on the agent host runtime from nanoclaw
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
Got it.


I’ll treat this as part of the architecture going forward:


Agent stewards are coming.
Each steward owns a domain.
They run on the agent host runtime via Nanoclaw.


So in the model, that means agent is not just another plugin surface. It is a real runtime role with domain-scoped ownership, parallel to server, async, web, and cli.


That also implies a useful constraint:
stewards should be treated as domain runtime assemblies, not random tool wrappers. Their ownership boundary matters.
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
And for owners, they get their own git alias or even email.


We have a whole thing on git + repo topology HUD as a tool
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
Good. That makes the ownership model much stronger.


So the architecture should treat ownership on three layers:


Domain ownership:


each domain has a steward on the agent host runtime via Nanoclaw


Repo/worktree authorship:


each owner/steward can have its own git alias and possibly its own email identity


Topology visibility and control:


the git + repo topology HUD is the operational surface for seeing who owns what, where work is happening, and how branches/worktrees map back to domains/stewards


That means “owner” is not just a CODEOWNERS-style label. It becomes an actual runtime and authorship identity.


So going forward I’d model this as:


domain
  -> owned by steward
  -> steward runs on agent host
  -> steward has git identity
  -> steward activity visible in repo topology HUD


That is much more powerful than static ownership metadata. It turns ownership into something executable and inspectable.


It also means any future ownership/conformance tooling should probably hook into:


domain/steward mapping

git identity mapping

repo/worktree topology visibility

host/runtime placement


That is worth keeping central, not bolting on later.
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say: describe how nx would be used to enforce domain ownership in a scaled version of our snowflake factory architecture (service core + plugin spokes)
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
Thought for 8s


Nx would enforce domain ownership in your snowflake factory by turning “ownership” from a naming convention into a graph rule.


The right mental model is:


service core = the capability truth
plugin spokes = runtime projections of that capability
domain ownership = who is allowed to create, compose, and depend on those units


Nx is useful here because it already treats the repo as a project graph, supports per-project tags, and can enforce dependency constraints from those tags. For JavaScript and TypeScript that happens through @nx/enforce-module-boundaries; for graph-wide, language-agnostic enforcement Nx offers Conformance on Enterprise. Nx+1


So in a scaled version of your model, I would encode ownership across multiple tag dimensions, not one flat “domain” label. Nx explicitly supports multiple tag dimensions and richer matching with exact tags, globs, and regex. Nx+1


The tag set should look roughly like this:


scope:hq | scope:sales | scope:todo | scope:billing
type:service | type:plugin | type:app | type:sdk
runtime:server | runtime:async | runtime:web | runtime:agent | runtime:cli | runtime:neutral
owner:steward-sales | owner:steward-todo | owner:platform
visibility:public | visibility:private | visibility:internal
execution:workflow | internal-router | consumer | command | ui | none


That gives you enough information to ask real ownership questions.


For example, a sales service core project might be tagged:


{
  "tags": [
    "scope:sales",
    "type:service",
    "runtime:neutral",
    "owner:steward-sales",
    "visibility:private"
  ]
}


A sales async workflow spoke might be:


{
  "tags": [
    "scope:sales",
    "type:plugin",
    "runtime:async",
    "execution:workflow",
    "owner:steward-sales"
  ]
}


A sales agent steward runtime might be:


{
  "tags": [
    "scope:sales",
    "type:app",
    "runtime:agent",
    "owner:steward-sales"
  ]
}


Then you use Nx boundaries to enforce the ownership model at three levels.


First, dependency ownership.


A sales-owned project should only depend on:


itself

shared/platform projects

other explicitly allowed sales-adjacent projects


That is straight Nx boundary enforcement: projects with scope:sales can only depend on scope:sales, scope:shared, scope:platform, etc. Nx’s built-in boundary rules are designed exactly for this tag-based pattern. Nx


That means:


plugins/server/sales-api can depend on services/sales-*

plugins/async/sales-workflows can depend on services/sales-*

plugins/agent/sales-steward can depend on services/sales-*

but sales-* cannot casually reach into billing-*


Second, role ownership.


This is the part people usually miss.


Your architecture is not just about “which domain owns which code.” It is also about “which domain is allowed to project itself into which runtime.”


So you use tag combinations to say things like:


type:plugin + runtime:async must come from projects tagged execution:workflow|consumer|internal-router

type:plugin + runtime:server must not depend directly on runtime:async plugin projects

type:app + runtime:agent must belong to exactly one domain owner


Nx can enforce these with boundary rules where imports/dependencies are visible, and Conformance can enforce graph-wide standards across all dependency edges. Nx+1


Third, assembly ownership.


This is where your HQ / sub-HQ model matters.


If apps/hq is the global assembly and apps/sales is a promoted domain assembly, you want rules like:


apps/sales/* may only compose scope:sales plus approved shared/platform units

apps/hq/* may compose everything

apps/todo/* may not silently compose scope:sales


Out of the box, Nx boundaries get you part of this. For the rest, you extend the project graph or run custom graph checks. Nx explicitly exposes project graph plugin APIs to add nodes and dependencies, which is the right hook if your rawr.hq.ts or plugin manifests define composition relationships that normal imports do not fully reveal. Nx+1


That is how ownership becomes real in your architecture:


domain owns service core
domain-owned plugins project that core into approved runtimes
domain apps assemble only approved owned scopes
Nx graph rules make violations fail


The most powerful part is not just linting. It is generation.


Nx generators are the real enforcement weapon here because they make the correct ownership shape the default. Nx supports local plugins and generators for custom scaffolding, and sync generators for repo-wide derived state. Nx


So your local Nx plugin should generate every snowflake-factory unit with ownership baked in.


For example:


nx g @rawr/workspace:service sales-account --scope=sales --owner=steward-sales
nx g @rawr/workspace:plugin sales-api --runtime=server --scope=sales --owner=steward-sales
nx g @rawr/workspace:plugin sales-workflows --runtime=async --execution=workflow --scope=sales --owner=steward-sales
nx g @rawr/workspace:app sales --roles=server,async,agent --owner=steward-sales


Each generator should:


create the project

assign tags

stamp owner metadata

wire manifest defaults

register the project in the right assembly or index


That way ownership is not inferred after the fact. It is authored at creation time.


Then you add a second enforcement layer: sync generators.


Nx sync generators can update root-level derived files when you run nx sync or nx sync:check. Nx


That is where you generate things like:


domain-to-steward maps

HQ composition indexes

plugin registries

git/CODEOWNERS-like outputs

topology HUD metadata


So ownership lives in one source of truth, and Nx compiles it outward.


In your architecture, that likely means the source of truth should be per-project metadata:


tags

owner field

steward identity

maybe git alias/email mapping


And then a sync generator emits:


repo topology HUD inputs

ownership index JSON

CODEOWNERS-style file if you want one

agent steward registry


That is how you connect repo ownership to runtime ownership.


Here is the concrete dependency matrix I would enforce first.


For service core and plugin spokes:


type:service
  -> may depend on type:package, type:sdk
  -> may not depend on type:plugin, type:app

type:plugin + runtime:server
  -> may depend on type:service, type:package, type:sdk
  -> may not depend on runtime:async plugins directly

type:plugin + runtime:async
  -> may depend on type:service, type:package, type:sdk
  -> may not depend on server plugins directly

type:app
  -> may depend on type:plugin, type:service, type:package, type:sdk
  -> should not contain reusable domain logic


For domain ownership:


scope:sales
  -> may depend on scope:sales, scope:shared, scope:platform

scope:todo
  -> may depend on scope:todo, scope:shared, scope:platform

scope:hq
  -> may depend on *


And for agent stewards:


runtime:agent
  -> every project must have exactly one owner:steward-*
  -> scope:* must match steward domain


Nx’s built-in boundary system gets you much of the first two layers directly through tags and constraints. Nx
For the last mile — “every agent app must have exactly one steward owner,” “every promoted assembly must only compose approved scopes,” “every plugin must declare owner metadata” — you either use Nx Conformance or write your own custom rule runner on top of the graph. Nx Enterprise provides Conformance for graph-wide checks and also an Owners-related rule, but if you do not want Enterprise, the same architecture can still be approximated with local plugins, graph inspection, and CI checks. Nx+1


So the scaled version of your snowflake factory looks like this:


services/
  sales-*
  todo-*
  billing-*

plugins/
  server/...
  async/...
  web/...
  agent/...

apps/
  hq/...
  sales/...
  todo/...


But Nx makes the hidden structure explicit:


service core projects
  tagged by scope, owner, type

plugin spoke projects
  tagged by scope, owner, runtime, execution, type

app assembly projects
  tagged by scope, owner, runtime, type


And the ownership policy becomes executable:


only the sales steward’s domain can evolve sales core and spokes

sales spokes can only project sales core into approved runtimes

promoted sales assemblies can only compose sales-owned and shared units

agent steward runtime for sales maps back to the same owner metadata

topology HUD and git identity can be generated from the same source


That is the key point: Nx does not just enforce “imports are legal.” In your architecture, Nx becomes the compiler for domain ownership.


The one thing I would not do is treat folder names as the ownership system. Folder names help humans. Tags, generators, graph rules, and sync outputs are what make ownership durable at scale.
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say: Yo, do we even need, do we even need folders for the apps, like sales and todo, or do we just need a single file? I mean, I can see the need for it growing, and we don't wanna, we don't wanna shake the base, right? We wanna keep things stable for scaffolding reasons and front-end force reasons, and for legibility reasons, right? Remember that agents also need topology.
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
You do not need the folders today if an app assembly is just:


one rawr.hq.ts

one thin entrypoint per role

maybe one config file


But you probably still should keep the folders.


Because this is one of those cases where the folder is buying you topology stability, not current implementation necessity.


That matters for:


agent navigation

scaffolding consistency

future growth without churn

clear promotion path

keeping “assembly” as a first-class thing


So the honest answer is:


Functionally: no, not always.
Architecturally/topologically: yes, probably.


The key distinction is between a thing being a real project node versus merely a file.


In your system, apps/hq, apps/sales, apps/todo are not just “files that happen to start something.” They are potential assembly roots. Even when they are tiny, they represent:


a bounded host bundle

a composition authority

a deployment unit candidate

a promotion boundary

an ownership/topology node


That is enough reason to give them a stable home.


If you reduce them to only single files floating around, you save almost nothing, but you lose a lot of clarity. Agents and humans both benefit from being able to treat “sales app assembly” as a place, not just a filename.


So I would keep the structure like:


apps/
  hq/
    rawr.hq.ts
    server/
      main.ts
    async/
      main.ts

  sales/
    rawr.hq.ts
    server/
      main.ts


Even if those directories are thin.


That said, I would not overbuild them either.


The right rule is:


An app assembly folder should start life as a thin stable container, not a mini-codebase.


So yes, the folder exists.
But most real logic should still live in:


services/*

plugins/*

packages/*

packages/hq-sdk/*


The app folder should mostly contain:


composition file(s)

role entrypoints

local app config

maybe deployment descriptors later


That is all.


This is one of those “don’t shake the base” decisions. You want the scaffold shape to be stable even if the implementation inside it is initially small.


There is also an Nx reason to keep the folder.


Nx thinks in terms of projects. A project that represents an assembly root is easier to reason about, tag, generate, own, and promote if it has a stable directory. That gives you:


cleaner project identity

clearer graph nodes

less churn when the assembly grows

better generator targets

better topology for agents


So if the question is:


“Should apps/sales be a folder even if it only contains one or two files for now?”


My answer is yes.


If the question is:


“Should every role under every app always get a folder from day one?”


That is less absolute.


You could compress early if you want:


apps/
  sales/
    rawr.hq.ts
    server.ts
    async.ts


and later expand to:


apps/
  sales/
    rawr.hq.ts
    server/
      main.ts
    async/
      main.ts


That is a reasonable compromise.


But I would still keep the app root folder itself.


So the hierarchy I’d recommend is:


Keep apps/<assembly>/ as a stable directory node.
Inside it, allow role entrypoints to start either as files or folders depending on how much structure you already need.


That gives you both:


stable topology

low initial overhead


So the best near-term shape is probably:


apps/
  hq/
    rawr.hq.ts
    server.ts
    async.ts
    cli.ts

  sales/
    rawr.hq.ts
    server.ts


Then when one role grows, expand just that role:


apps/
  hq/
    rawr.hq.ts
    server/
      main.ts
      config.ts
    async/
      main.ts
      workers.ts


That is the most leverage-efficient version.


So the final answer is:


keep the apps/<assembly> folders

do not feel forced to make every role a folder immediately

treat app folders as stable topology nodes for humans, agents, Nx, and future promotion

keep them thin so the real logic stays elsewhere


That is the cleanest balance.
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say: yeah but that leaves variability for little gain right
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
Yes. That variability is probably not worth it.


If your goal is stable topology for humans, agents, scaffolding, and Nx project identity, then pick one shape and keep it uniform.


So I would standardize on:


apps/
  hq/
    rawr.hq.ts
    server/
      main.ts
    async/
      main.ts
    cli/
      main.ts

  sales/
    rawr.hq.ts
    server/
      main.ts


Even when the role folder is thin.


Why? Because the gain is not in today’s file count. The gain is in removing conditional structure.


If some assemblies use:


server.ts


and others use:


server/main.ts


then every generator, graph assumption, agent heuristic, and human expectation now has to handle both.


That is noise.


The architecture you are building benefits more from uniformity than from shaving one folder level.


This is one of those places where the right move is boring:
choose the slightly more explicit structure and make it universal.


The cost is tiny:


+ one extra folder
+ one extra main.ts


The benefit is real:


zero ambiguity

easier generation

easier promotion

easier agent traversal

easier role-local growth

cleaner Nx project conventions

fewer special cases forever


So yes, your pushback is right. Allowing both shapes is unnecessary variability.


I would lock:


apps/<assembly>/rawr.hq.ts

apps/<assembly>/<role>/main.ts


for every assembly/role that exists.


No mixed shorthand.
No “flatten until needed.”
No alternate micro-shapes.


Just one shape.


That is the better base.
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say: but wait… what even goes into those folderV
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
Very little, by design.


Those folders should be assembly entrypoint folders, not where the real app logic lives.


So apps/<assembly>/<role>/ exists mostly to hold the runtime-specific glue for that assembled host role.


The rule should be:


If something is reusable across assemblies, it does not belong there.
If something is domain/business logic, it does not belong there.
If something is a runtime-entry concern for this exact assembly+role, it probably does.


So in:


apps/hq/server/
apps/hq/async/
apps/sales/server/


the contents should be things like:


main.ts
The actual entrypoint. Boot the role runtime, load the assembly, start the process.


config.ts
Only if this assembly+role has local config shaping that is not generic enough for the host SDK.


env.ts
Only if you want explicit environment parsing/binding local to that role entrypoint.


telemetry.ts
Only if there is role-local telemetry bootstrap that should happen before startup.


routes.ts or mount.ts
Only if you need a tiny local composition wrapper before handing off to the SDK bootstrap.


README.md
Optional, but useful if the role has non-obvious startup/deploy semantics.


That’s basically it.


What should not go there:


service logic

plugin logic

reusable host bootstrap logic

shared schemas

domain workflows

business rules

general utilities


Those belong in:


services/*

plugins/*

packages/*

packages/hq-sdk/*


So the ideal main.ts is almost boring.


Example for server:


import { startServerRole } from "@rawr/hq-sdk/roles/server";
import { createHqAssembly } from "../rawr.hq";

const assembly = createHqAssembly();

await startServerRole({
  assembly,
});


Example for async:


import { startAsyncRole } from "@rawr/hq-sdk/roles/async";
import { createHqAssembly } from "../rawr.hq";

const assembly = createHqAssembly();

await startAsyncRole({
  assembly,
});


That is the point. The folder is not there because it is full of code. It is there because it is a stable topology node.


So think of the contents as falling into three buckets.


First, entrypoint glue.
The minimal code needed to start that exact role for that exact assembly.


Second, role-local config/bootstrap.
Env parsing, startup flags, transport bindings, telemetry setup, shutdown hooks.


Third, deployment-adjacent metadata.
Anything specific to how this role is run, built, or deployed.


If a folder has more than that, something is probably leaking into the wrong layer.


A concrete shape could be:


apps/
  hq/
    rawr.hq.ts

    server/
      main.ts
      env.ts
      config.ts

    async/
      main.ts
      env.ts

  sales/
    rawr.hq.ts

    server/
      main.ts


And many of those files may stay tiny for a long time.


Sanity check:


Question: could this file be shared across assemblies?
Yes -> put it in hq-sdk/package/service/plugin layer.

Question: is this file about starting this exact assembly role?
Yes -> it can live in apps/<assembly>/<role>/.


So the blunt answer is:


Almost nothing goes in those folders.
That’s why they’re good.
They are stable runtime entrypoint containers, not logic buckets.
